questions.question: From the clues given, identify this great Sikh ruler holding court. Known as the Lion of Punjab, his presence held back the Company until his death in 1839.
questions.explanation: Maharaja Ranjit Singh (1780 –1839) was the leader of the Sikh Empire, which ruled the northwest Indian subcontinent in the early half of the 19th century. He survived smallpox in infancy but lost sight in his left eye. He fought his first battle alongside his father at age 10. After his father died, he fought several wars to expel the Afghans in his teenage years and was proclaimed as the "Maharaja of Punjab" at age 21. His empire grew in the Punjab region under his leadership through 1839.

questions.question: Who is considered as the chief architect of the Doctrine of Lapse?
questions.explanation: The 'doctrine of lapse' was an annexation policy applied by the British East India Company in India before 1858. According to the doctrine, any princely state or territory under the direct influence (paramountcy) of the British East India Company as a vassal state under the British subsidiary system, would automatically be annexed if the ruler was either "manifestly incompetent or died without a male heir". The policy is most commonly associated with Lord Dalhousie, who was the Governor-General for the East India Company in India between 1848 and 1856.

questions.question: If an Indian ruler died without a male heir, his kingdom would lapse i.e., become part of the East India Company territory name the doctrine which states this, introduced by Lord Dalhousie?
questions.explanation: The 'doctrine of lapse' was an annexation policy applied by the British East India Company in India before 1858. According to the doctrine, any princely state or territory under the direct influence (paramountcy) of the British East India Company, as a vassal state under the British subsidiary system, would automatically be annexed if the ruler was either "manifestly incompetent or died without a male heir". The policy is most commonly associated with Lord Dalhousie, who was the Governor-General for the East India Company in India between 1848 and 1856.

questions.question: Satara, Sambalpur, Udaipur, Nagpur were annexed by the British by applying the Doctrine of Lapse. Which kingdom among the following options can be added to this list?
questions.explanation: Jhansi was annexed by applying the Doctrine of Lapse in 1854.

questions.question: The people of which kingdom, enraged by the humiliating way in which the Nawab Wajid Ali Shah was deposed joined the great revolt that broke out in 1857?
questions.explanation: On 29 March 1857, a young soldier, Mangal Pandey, was hanged to death for attacking his officers in Barrackpore. Nana Saheb, the adopted son of the late Peshwa Baji Rao who lived near Kanpur, gathered armed forces and expelled the British garrison from the city. He proclaimed himself Peshwa. He declared that he was a governor under Emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar. In Lucknow, Birjis Qadr, the son of the deposed Nawab Wajid Ali Shah, was proclaimed the new Nawab. He too acknowledged the suzerainty of Bahadur Shah Zafar. His mother Begum Hazrat Mahal took an active part in organising the uprising against the British. In Jhansi, Rani Lakshmibai joined the rebel sepoys. A situation of widespread popular rebellion developed in the region of Awadh in particular.

questions.question: Pick the one name that can be given to the British administrative units?
questions.explanation: British territories were broadly divided into administrative units called Presidencies. There were three Presidencies: Bengal, Madras, and Bombay. Each was ruled by a Governor. The supreme head of the administration was the Governor-General. Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General, introduced several administrative reforms, notably in the sphere of justice.

questions.question: Name the three important Presidencies in the British administrative setup?
questions.explanation: British territories were broadly divided into administrative units called Presidencies. There were three Presidencies: Bengal, Madras, and Bombay. Each was ruled by a Governor. The supreme head of the administration was the Governor-General. Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General, introduced several administrative reforms, notably in the sphere of justice.

questions.question: According to the British administrative setup, who was the supreme head?
questions.explanation: British territories were broadly divided into administrative units called Presidencies. There were three Presidencies: Bengal, Madras, and Bombay. Each was ruled by a Governor. The supreme head of the administration was the Governor-General. Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General (from 1773 to 1785)introduced several administrative reforms, notably in the sphere of justice.

questions.question: A new system of justice was established by the British in India with two courts, Criminal court and Civil court in each district. Name the year in which this system was introduced by the British.
questions.explanation: From 1772 a new system of justice was established. Each district was to have two courts - a criminal court (Faujdari Adalat) and a civil court (Diwani Adalat). Maulvis and Hindu pandits interpreted Indian laws for the European district collectors who presided over civil courts. The criminal courts were still under a qazi and a mufti but under the supervision of the collectors.

questions.question: Compete for the following with respect to the judicial set up in India by the British. The criminal courts were still under a qazi and a mufti but under the supervision of the ________.
questions.explanation: From 1772 a new system of justice was established. Each district was to have two courts - a criminal court (Faujdari Adalat) and a civil court (Diwani Adalat). Maulvis and Hindu pandits interpreted Indian laws for the European district collectors who presided over civil courts. The criminal courts were still under a qazi and a mufti but under the supervision of the collectors.

questions.question: Who is referred to as a Qazi?
questions.explanation: A qazi is the magistrate or judge of the Shariʿa court, who also exercised extra-judicial functions, such as mediation, guardianship over orphans and minors, and supervision and auditing of public works. The word "qazi" comes from a verb meaning to "judge" or to "decide".

questions.question: Who is called a Mufti?

options.html: A jurist of the Muslim community
A scholar who has written social rules and codes of behavior.

questions.explanation: A mufti is an Islamic scholar who interprets and expounds Islamic law. Muftis are jurists qualified to give authoritative legal opinions known as fatwas. Historically, they were members of the ulama ranking above qadis.

questions.question: Select the option that best describes the Sanskrit texts prescribing social rules and codes of behavior composed from c.500 BCE onwards.
questions.explanation: Dharmasastra is a genre of Sanskrit texts and refers to the treatises (shastras) of Hinduism on dharma. There are many Dharmashastras, variously estimated to be 18 to about 100, with different and conflicting points of view. Each of these texts exists in many different versions, and each is rooted in Dharmasutras texts dated to 1st millennium BCE that emerged from Kalpa (Vedanga) studies in the Vedic era.

questions.question: What does a Musket refer to?

options.html: A heavy gun used by infantry soldiers
Sanskrit text with social rules written

questions.explanation: A rifled musket or rifle-musket is a type of firearm made in the mid-19th century. Originally the term referred only to muskets that had been produced as a smoothbore weapon and later had their barrels replaced with rifled barrels.

questions.question: From the given list of options, choose the one that best describes a Matchlock?

options.html: Sanskrit text with social rules written

questions.explanation: As warfare technology changed from the 1820s, the cavalry requirements of the Company’s army declined. This is because the British empire was fighting in Burma, Afghanistan and Egypt where soldiers were armed with muskets and matchlocks. The soldiers of the Company’s army had to keep pace with changing military requirements and its infantry regiments now became more important. Matchlock is an early type of gun in which the powder was ignited by a match.

  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The image given depicts an important Governor General of the East India Company accepting the Diwani of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa from the Mughal ruler in 1765. Name this British personality.
questions.explanation: Robert Clive is accepting the Diwani of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa from the Mughal ruler in 1765. The actual event most probably took place in Robert Clive's tent, with a few Englishmen and Indians as witnesses.

questions.question: From the given dates, on which date did the Mughal emperor appoint the East India Company as the Diwan of Bengal?
questions.explanation: On 12 August 1765, the Mughal emperor appointed the East India Company as the Diwan of Bengal. A painter was commissioned by Clive to record the memorable events in Clive's life.

questions.question:
What was the chief advantage of the Company when appointed as the Diwan of Bengal?

  1. The Company became the chief financial administrator of the territory under its control and they could raise revenue.
  2. The Company could trade to whichever country they wanted.
  3. The Company helped the artisans to sell their goods at higher prices.
  4. Agricultural production saw an increase in Bengal.
questions.explanation: As Diwan, the Company became the chief financial administrator of the territory under its control. Now it had to think of administering the land and organising its revenue resources. A trading company had also to ensure that it could buy the products it needed and sell what it wanted.

questions.question:
A few statements with respect to the Bengal economy after the Company had become the Diwan are given. Pick out the one that is not applicable to the Bengal economy.

  1. The artisans started deserting villages as they were forced to sell their goods to the Company at low prices.
  2. The Bengal economy thrived and progressed.
  3. Peasants were unable to pay the dues that were being demanded from them.
  4. Artisanal production and agricultural cultivation showed signs of collapse.
questions.explanation: The Bengal economy was facing deep crises. Agricultural cultivation showed signs of collapse. Then in 1770, a terrible famine killed ten million people in Bengal. About one-third population was wiped out.

questions.question:
What happened in 1770 in Bengal so that one-third of the population was wiped out?

  1. Robert Clive had to wage a battle with the locals and killed many.
  2. The British and the French had a war in Bengal and many were killed.
  3. Due to a terrible famine, ten million people in Bengal were wiped out.
  4. The British shipped a lot of locals to Britain and the met with an accident.
questions.explanation: In 1770 a terrible famine killed ten million people in Bengal. About one-third of the population was wiped out. Weekly markets (haats) were badly affected during the time of this economic crisis.

questions.question: In which year did Bengal witness the Great Famine?
questions.explanation: After Company became the Diwan of Bengal in 1765, soon it had to face deep economic crises. Things worsened when in 1770 a terrible famine broke out in Bengal in which as many as ten million people were killed.

questions.question: From the given possibilities, which year did the British introduce the Permanent Settlement of Bengal?
questions.explanation: Most Company officials began to feel that investment in the land had to be encouraged and agriculture had to be improved. After two decades of debates, the Company finally introduced the Permanent Settlement in 1793.

questions.question: Which Governor General was responsible for the introduction of Permanent Settlement of Bengal? Identify from the picture given .
questions.explanation: Charles Cornwallis was the Governor-General of India when Permanent Settlement was introduced. He was appointed in February 1786 to serve as both commander -in-chief of British India and Governor of the Presidency of Fort Williams also known as Bengal Presidency.

questions.question: According to the terms of the Permanent Settlement, who were recognized as zamindars?
questions.explanation: By the terms of Permanent Settlement, the Rajas and Taluqdars were recognised as zamindars. They were asked to collect rent from the peasants and pay revenue to the company. The amount to be paid was fixed permanently, that is, it was not to be increased ever in the future.

questions.question:
How did the Company ask the Rajas to collect revenue?

  1. Rajas had to collect rent from the peasants and pay revenue to the Company.
  2. Rajas had to give it from their treasury.
  3. Rajas had to give it in the form of jewellery and gold.
  4. Rajas had to raise revenue by selling their own land.
questions.explanation: The rajas and taluqdars were asked to collect rent from the peasants and pay revenue to the company. The amount to be paid was fixed permanently, that is, it was not to be increased ever in the future. It was felt that it would ensure a regular flow of revenue into the Company's coffers.

questions.question:
A set of statements on how the Permanent Settlement system was advantageous to the British are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

  1. The revenue amount was not a fixed one and the zamindars did not benefit from increased production from the land.
  2. The British could get a continuous flow of revenue.
  3. This system created a new class of landlords who were loyal to the British.
  4. The rajas and taluqdars were recognized as zamindars.
questions.explanation: The amount to be paid was fixed permanently, that is, it was not to be increased ever in the future. It was felt that this would ensure a regular flow of revenue into the Company's coffers.

questions.question: The following set of statements show the problems faced by the Permanent Settlement system, choose the one that is not true.

options.html: Landowners who failed to pay the revenue lost their zamindari and became landless labourers.
Zamindars did not invest in the improvement of land.
Eviction of landowners due to the absence of relevant documents.
The Company had to bear the loss of the Zamindars.

questions.explanation: The revenue that had been fixed was so high that the zamindars found it difficult to pay. Anyone who failed to pay the revenue lost his zamindari. Numerous zamindaris were sold off at auctions organised by the Company.

questions.question: In which year was the Permanent Settlement System abolished?
questions.explanation: On 16th December 1950, a new bill called the East Bengal State Acquisition and Tenancy Bill 1950 became an Act entitled East Bengal State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950. Under the Act, the permanent settlement was at last abolished. During the period 1948-1950, some of the princely states joined India, and the Indian zamindaris were also abolished. In return for surrendering the government of their states in the case of Princes, and estates in the case of Zamindars, together with their revenues and military forces, the former ruling princes were guaranteed their hereditary styles and titles, certain privileges of rank and honour, as well as privy purses to cover the living expenses of themselves and their families. However, this too was abolished in 1956.

questions.question: The under tenants depressed by an excessive rent In kind and by usurious returns of the cattle, seed and subsistence, advance to them, can never extricate themselves from debt. In so abject a state, they cannot labour in spirit, while they earn a scanty subsistence without hope of bettering their situation. These words of an Englishman described the pathetic conditions of the under tenants of Bengal in 1806. With the help of the picture given, name the Englishman who wrote this.
questions.explanation: In many villages of Bengal, some of the powerful ryots did not cultivate, but instead gave out their lands to others(the under-tenants), taking from them very high rents. Hence in 1806 H.T.Colebrook described the conditions of these under tenants.

questions.question: Name the Englishman who introduced the Mahalwari Settlement System as a substitute to the Permanent Settlement System.
questions.explanation: In the North-Western Provinces of the Bengal Presidency, an Englishman called Holt Mackenzie devised the new system which came into effect in 1822.

This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History Ruling the Countryside CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements are true with respect to the Mahalwari System?

options.html: Cultivators had to pay half of the revenue as tax.
The village headman was responsible for the tax payment.
Zamindars had to pay a fixed amount to the Government.
A new class of loyal landlords emerged under this system.

questions.explanation: Under the system of the Mahalwari settlement, the charge of collecting the revenue and paying it to the Company was given to the village headman, rather than the Zamindar.

questions.question: Under the Mahalwari system, the estimated revenue of each plot within a village was added up to calculate the revenue that each mahal had to pay. From the given list of options, which one is closest in meaning to the word mahal?
questions.explanation: The new system of Mahalwari settlement devised by Holt Mackenzie the village was an important social institution. Under his directions, collectors went from village to village, inspecting the land, measuring the fields, and recording the customs and rights of different groups. The estimated revenue of each plot within a village was added up to calculate the revenue that each village (mahal) had to pay.

questions.question: From among the regions mentioned below, which region introduced the Mahalwari Settlement System in 1822?
questions.explanation: In the North-Western Provinces of the Bengal Presidency (most of this area is now in Uttar Pradesh), an Englishman called Holt Mackenziedevised the new system of Mahalwari settlement which came into effect in 1882.

questions.question: The Company tried many experiments to increase the land revenue. Name the Settlement system introduced in the Madras and Bombay presidencies?
questions.explanation: The ryotwari system was tried on a small scale by Captain Alexander Read in some of the areas that were taken over by the Company after the wars with Tipu Sultan. Subsequently developed by Thomas Munro, this system was gradually extended all over south India.

questions.question: Who among the following introduced the Ryotwari Settlement system?
questions.explanation: Thomas Munro, Governor of Madras (1819-1826) developed the system. He thought that the British should act as paternal father figures protecting the ryots under their charge.

questions.question: An important blue cloth dye was produced from a plant. The Indian plant was famous for the rich blue colour that it gave to the cloth. The British forced the peasants to grow more of these plants and the peasants refused as this plant made the land barren. Which plant is this?
questions.explanation: It is likely that the blue dye used in the Morris prints in nineteenth-century Britain was manufactured from Indigo plants cultivated in India. India was the biggest supplier of Indigo in the world at that time.

questions.question: What is the literal meaning of the Greek word indikon?
questions.explanation: The term Indikon is made up of the Spanish word indico, via the Greek indikon meaning “blue dye from India” or literally “Indian" (substance). The indigo plant grows primarily in the tropics. By the thirteenth century, Indian indigo was being used by cloth manufacturers in Italy, France and Britain to dye cloth. However, only small amounts of Indian indigo reached the European market and its price was very high.

questions.question:
Why was there a popular demand for Indian indigo in Italy, France, and Britain?

  1. India was the biggest producer of indigo and no other country produced indigo.
  2. Indigo was used to dye cloth and there was a huge demand.
  3. Indigo imports were tax-free.
  4. There was a ban on indigo import in other countries.
questions.explanation: Cloth dyers preferred indigo as a dye. Indigo produced a rich blue colour, whereas the dye from easily available woad was pale and dull.

questions.question: The Europeans want to ban the import of indigo. Which was the plant that they thought had a competition with indigo?
questions.explanation: European cloth manufacturers depended on another plant called woad to make violet and blue dyes. Being a plant of temperate zones, woad was easily available in Europe. Worried by the competition from indigo, woad producers in Europe pressurised their governments to ban the import of indigo.

questions.question:
The following table shows the different places of indigo cultivation by the different European powers. Complete the table by choosing the correct option from the given list

French St Domingue, Caribbean islands
Spanish Venezuela
Portuguese -
questions.explanation: The French began cultivating indigo in St Dominigue in the Caribean islands, the Portuguese in Brazil, the English in Jamaica, and the Spanish in Venezuela. Indigo plantations also came up in many parts of North America.

questions.question: What do you mean by nij and ryoti?
questions.explanation: There were two main systems of indigo cultivation: Nij and Ryoti Cultivation. Under NIJ cultivation, the planters produced indigo in lands that were directly under his control. They directly employed hired labourers. Under the ryoti system, the planters forced the ryots (farmers) to sign a contract, an agreement (Satta). Those who signed the contracts were given cash advances from planters at low rates of interest to produce indigo.

questions.question: Why many Scotsmen and Englishmen came to India and became planters?

options.html: The prospect of high profits of indigo attracted them.
Many rich landlords lost their interest in cultivation.
Indian farmers could not pay the revenue and auctioned their land.
Relaxation of private land ownership rules by the British.

questions.explanation: Attracted by the prospect of high profits, numerous Scotsmen and Englishmen came to India and became planters. Those who had no money to produce indigo could get loans from the Company and the banks that were coming up at the time.

questions.question: Given below are some points with regard to the nij cultivation of indigo. Select the one that is not related to the nij cultivation.

options.html: Large areas in compact blocks were needed for the cultivation.
Planters directly employed hired labourers for the cultivation.
Planters were forced to sign a contract called satta.
Planters directly controlled the lands of cultivation.

questions.explanation: Under the system of nij cultivation, the planter produced indigo in lands that he directly controlled. He either bought the land or rented it from other zamindars and produced indigo by directly employing hired labourers. It was under the ryoti system that the planters forced the ryots to sign a contract, an agreement (satta).

questions.question: Who are called the ryots?

options.html: The cultivators who actually worked on the land
Women worker who used to carry the indigo plant
The village headmen who controlled the village land

questions.explanation: Under the ryoti system, the planters forced the ryots to sign a contract, an agreement. At times they pressurised the village headman to sign the contract on behalf of the ryots.

questions.question:
A set of statements with respect to the ryot cultivation of indigo is given below. Select the one that is not applicable to the ryot cultivation.

  1. The planters produced indigo in the lands that he directly controlled.
  2. The planters were forced to sign a contract, an agreement (Satta).
  3. Those who signed the contract got cash advances from the planters at low rates of interest to produce indigo.
  4. The planter provided the seed and the drill, while the cultivators prepared the soil, sowed the seed and looked after the crop.
questions.explanation: Under the Ryoti system, the planters forced the ryots to sign a contract and those who signed the contract got cash advances from the planters at low interest to produce indigo. It was under nij cultivation the planters produced indigo on the lands that he directly controlled.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The picture shows an indigo factory, located near the indigo fields. After harvest, the women usually carry the indigo plant to a fermenting storage vessel to manufacture the dye. Name this storage unit used for fermenting the indigo leaves
questions.explanation: After the harvest, indigo plant was taken to vats in the indigo factory.Vat was a fermenting or storage vessel. Three to four vats were needed to manufacture the dye. Each vat had a seperate function. The vat beater stirred the solution in the vat. These workers had to remain in waist deep water for over eight hours to beat the indigo solution.

questions.question: The picture shows a Vat beater. What does a Vat beater do?

options.html: A Vat beater stirs the indigo solution in the vat by standing waist deep water for over eight hours.
A Vat beater beats the indigo plant and extracts the leaves
A Vat beater carries the indigo plants to the big vats
A vat beater owns an indigo factory

questions.explanation: In the second vat (known as the beater vat)the solution was continously stirred and beaten with paddles by the vat beater.When the liquid gradually turned green and then blue,lime water was added to the vat.

questions.question:
A process to make indigo pulp is given below. Fill up the missing link from the choices given so that the cycle is complete from the harvest till ready for sale :
Step-1: After harvest, the indigo plant is taken to the vats in the factory
Step-2: Leaves removed and soaked in warm water for fermenting
Step-3: ________
Step-4: When liquid turn green and then blue, lime water added to the vat
Step-5: Liquid drained off and the muddy sediment, the indigo pulp pressed and dried for sale.

options.html: Workers stamp and cut the pressed indigo pulp
Rotten leaves taken out, solution stirred with paddles by Vat beaters
Workers press and mould the indigo pulp
Women carried indigo plant to the factory

questions.explanation: Now the rotten leaves were taken out and the liquid drained into another vat that was placed just below the first vat. In the second vat(known as the beater vat)the solution was continuously stirred and beaten with paddles.

questions.question:
Initially the peasants were tempted by the loans, but soon they realized how harsh the system was. Why?

  1. The British said the quality of the indigo not up to the mark.
  2. Many landlords stopped cultivation of indigo as they were forced to sign up contracts and take loans.
  3. Peasants preferred to cultivate rice and were to forced to cultivate indigo in good yielding lands.
  4. The price they got for the indigo was very low and the cycle of loans never ended.
questions.explanation: When the crop was delivered to the planter after the harvest, a new loan was given to the ryot, the cycle started all over again.

questions.question: Why, after an indigo harvest, the land could not be sown with rice?

options.html: Because the British wanted only the cultivation of indigo
Because indigo cultivation made the soil infertile
Because many colonies were set up and crop cultivation was not possible
Because the climate was not suitable for rice cultivation

questions.explanation: The planters usually insisted on cultivating indigo on the best soils in which peasants preferred to grow rice. Indigo had deep roots that exhausted the fertility of the land rapidly. After an indigo harvest, the land became unfit for rice cultivation.

questions.question:
What happened on 20th March 1860, with respect to the indigo cultivation?

  1. Thousands of ryots refused to grow indigo and started a rebellion.
  2. Thousands of ryots started fresh new cultivation of indigo.
  3. There was a very good harvest on indigo.
  4. The price of indigo was increased by the British to encourage more cultivation.
questions.explanation: As the rebellion spread, ryots refused to pay rents to the planters and attacked indigo factories armed with swords and spears, bows and arrows. Women turned up to fight with pots, pans and kitchen implements.

questions.question:
Some reasons for the Blue Rebellion are given below. Which one among the options is not a valid one.

  1. Ryots refused to pay rents to the planters.
  2. Ryots attacked indigo factories armed with swords and spears, bows and arrows.
  3. Those who worked for the planters were socially boycotted and the agents of planters beaten up.
  4. The local zamindars and the village headmen convinced the peasants that indigo cultivation made the land fertile and not barren.
questions.explanation: Indigo had deep roots and it exhausted the soil rapidly. After an indigo harvest land could not be sown with rice. Ryots swore they would no longer take advances to sow indigo nor be bullied by the planters lathiyals.

questions.question: Who are called gomasthas?

options.html: Agents of planters who came and collected rent

questions.explanation: Gomasthas were the agents of the planters who were beaten up by the ryots during the rebellion when they came to collect rent from the ryots.

questions.question: Name the magistrate who issued a notice in Barasat, stating that ryots would not be compelled to accept indigo contracts?
questions.explanation: The magistrate Ashley Eden issued a notice stating that ryots would not be compelled to accept indigo contracts. Eden was trying to placate the peasants and control the explosive situation, but his action was read as support for the rebellion.

questions.question: I would rather beg than sow Indigo. Name the Indigo cultivator who said these words to the President of the Indigo Commission?
questions.explanation: Hadji Mulla, an indigo cultivator of Chandpore, Thana Hardi, was interviewed by the members of the Indigo commission on Tuesday, 5 June 1860.

questions.question:
From the given map, identify the place marked in red with the help of the clue given :
This place in Bihar witnessed a massive uprising against the British indigo planters under the leadership of Mahatma Gandhi
questions.explanation: When Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa,a peasant from Bihar persuaded him to visit Champaran and see the plight of Indigo cultivators there.

questions.question: Who was the great national leader who supported the agitation of Indigo planters?
questions.explanation: When Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa, a peasant from Bihar persuaded him to visit Champaran and see the plight of indigo cultivators there. Mahatma Gandhi's visit in 1917 marked the beginning of the Champaran movement against the indigo planters.

questions.question: From the given list of names, pick out the name of the novel written by Dinabandhu Mitra highlighting the plight of the indigo planters.
questions.explanation: As the rebellion spread, intellectuals from Calcutta rushed to the indigo districts. They wrote of the misery of the ryots, the tyranny of the planters and the horrors of the indigo system.

questions.question: This image is a pictorial representation of all the stages of indigo production in the French slave plantations of an important island. Which island is this?
questions.explanation: In the early eighteenth century,a French missionary,Jean Baptiste Labat,travelled to Caribean islands,and wrote extensively about the region.Published in one of his books, this image shows all the stages of indigo production in the French slave plantations of this region.

questions.question: Select the name of the French missionary who travelled to the Caribbean islands and wrote extensively about the region and slave indigo workers.
questions.explanation: In the early eighteenth century, a French missionary, Jean Baptiste Labat, travelled to the Caribean islands and wrote extensively about the region.

questions.question: From the following list of choices, which one would be the most appropriate meaning of the term Adivasis?
questions.explanation: The term 'Adivasis' derives from Hindi word 'adi' which means of earliest times or from the beginning and 'vasi' meaning inhabitant or residents. Adivasis also means indigenous peoples, they are communities who lived and often continue to live in close association with forests.

questions.question: A few statements related to the Adivasis are given below. Choose the one that is not applicable to the tribals of India.

options.html: They did not like to live in communities
Isolated, they stayed away from society
They lived in forests and hills

questions.explanation: The term Adivasi itself is a collective term of indigenous people so it would be incorrect to say that they did not like to live in communities or groups.

questions.question:
Given below are a few reasons as to why the tribals felt uncomfortable under the British. One statement is not true, pick out the one.

  1. Tribals were exploited even by the money lenders.
  2. Tribals who had depended on the forest for food and fodder were forced to stay in the forest and hills by the British.
  3. Tribals were forced to work as labourers under the British.
  4. Tribals were paid very low wages.
questions.explanation: Adivasi has always lived in closed proximity of forests. The entire life and livelihood of Adivasis are based on forests so they were not forced by the British to live in the forests and hills but on the contrary, they are the aboriginal population of the forests.

questions.question: Which one of the following refers to the correct meaning of habitats?
questions.explanation: A habitat is the natural home or environment of a plant-animal or any other organism. So it can be also called the dwelling place.

questions.question: Name the Act passed by the British in 1865 that gave the British the power to declare any forest land as Government land.
questions.explanation: Forest Regulation Act was passed by the British in 1865 which truncated the age-old traditional use by communities of their forests and secure the colonial Government control over the forests and the forest produce or resources.

questions.question: Apart from the Forest Regulation Act, which was the other Act passed by the British in 1871 which labelled all the Adivasi groups that rebelled against the British as criminals.
questions.explanation: Besides Forests Regulations Act of 1865, the British enacted the Criminal Tribes Act or CTA, in 1871. The British branded a number of marginalized groups as innately criminals and made elaborate arrangements for their surveillance. It imposed restrictions on the movements of such groups, they were forced to change their places of living and even occupations so that they could not access the forests and the forest resources as well.  They were penalized and rigorously punished if they were found outside their prescribed area.

questions.question: Tribals went to work in the ________ estates of Assam.
questions.explanation: Tribals were recruited in large numbers to work the tea plantations of Assam.

questions.question: From this photograph, identify this tribal hero from Chottanagpur in Bihar, who had miraculous powers to cure all diseases.
questions.explanation: The picture is of Birsa Munda a young freedom fighter and a tribal leader from Chottanagpur plateau who mobilised the tribal communities against the British and had also forced the colonial officials to introduce laws protecting the land rights of the tribals .

questions.question: To which tribe did Birsa belong to?
questions.explanation: Birsa Munda belonged to the Munda tribe. His father's name was Sugana Munda.

questions.question: How did Birsa Munda die?

options.html: He was captured by the British and killed

questions.explanation: Birsa Munda died on June 9, 1900, at the age of 25 in Ranchi Jail where he was imprisoned. The British government declared that he died of cholera although he showed no symptoms of the disease, fuelling rumours that he might have been poisoned.

questions.question:
In what way did the movement by Birsa Munda prove to be a significant one?

  1. This movement proved that British was much stronger than the tribals.
  2. This movement helped the British to easily implement the Permanent settlement.
  3. This movement helped the British to impose huge land taxes on the Adivasis.
  4. He proved that tribal people had the capacity to protest against injustice and rebel against colonial rule.
questions.explanation: Birsa Munda's movement is considered to be significant because he proved that the tribals had the capacity to protest against the injustice and rebel against the colonial Government. He forced the British to introduce laws protecting the land rights of the tribals.

questions.question:
Which one from the following options is closest in meaning to Guerilla warfare?

  1. Non-conventional warfare of smaller groups attacking a large army.
  2. Non-conventional warfare of a large army attacking a small group.
  3. An operation by the Government to control a large group of people who rebel.
  4. The suppression of a rebellion using non-violence.
questions.explanation: Guerrilla warfare is a war tactic in which the civilian population or other people fight against an organised army.

questions.question:
A sequence of events that happened in the revolt led by Birsa Munda is given below. Arrange them in the correct order.

  1. Several members of the Munda tribe gave petitions to reclaim their old ancestral right for free fuel, grazing, and hunting which was rejected by the British.
  2. Birsa Munda adopted guerrilla warfare and launched surprise attacks killing many police officials
  3. The revolt or ulgulan was finally suppressed and Birsa Munda arrested.
  4. Since Adivasis refused to cultivate the crops demanded by the British, outsiders were brought in to cultivate the land
  5. The Munda chiefs lost their authority and a few of them were treated as farm labourers
questions.explanation: Several members of the Munda tribe gave petitions to reclaim their old ancestral right for free fuel, grazing, and hunting which was rejected by the British. Since Adivasis refused to cultivate the crops demanded by the British, outsiders were brought in to cultivate the land. The Munda chiefs lost their authority and a few of them were treated as farm labourers. Birsa Munda adopted guerrilla warfare and launched surprise attacks killing many police officials. The revolt or ulgulan was finally suppressed and Birsa Munda arrested.

questions.question:
What is Jhum or Jhoom Cultivation?

  1. In this type of cultivation, the tribal chief divided the forest land and asked the tribals to grow crops.
  2. In this type, the British allotted different lands to the tribals and asked them to grow indigo plants instead of crops.
  3. This type of cultivation cleared a small patch of land, grew crops like rice, vegetable, fruits, etc., and when the soil loses fertility, moved and shifted to another patch of virgin land for cultivation.
  4. This type of cultivation encouraged the farmers to grow various crops simultaneously according to the climate.
questions.explanation: Jhum Cultivation is a primitive practice of cultivation in which farmers slash and burn a patch of the land grow food crops. When the soil fertility declines they shift to another place. The land is cultivated and fallowed cyclically so that the soil gets the nutrients lost due to over plantation.

questions.question: Which among the following options best describe broadcasting or scattering?

options.html: The method of sowing seeds in Jhum cultivation.
The method of granting titles to the tribal chiefs by the British.
The method of raising cattle in poultries.
The method of harvesting in Jhum cultivation.

questions.explanation: Broadcasting or scattering the seeds is one of the ancient methods of farming to sow seeds over a wide area by hand. It facilitates to irrigate the fields easily and derive the maximum yield of crops. The method of sowing in Jhum Cultivation is known as broadcasting or scattering of seeds.

questions.question:
Some important features of the Jhum cultivation are given below. Pick out the one that is not related to the Jhum cultivation.

  1. This type of cultivation is done on small patches of land and hence not suitable for forests cultivation by the tribals.
  2. The cultivators cut the treetops to allow sunlight to reach the ground and burnt the vegetation on the land to clear it for cultivation.
  3. Potash, the ash from the burning of the vegetation used to fertilise the soil.
  4. Once a crop is harvested they move to another virgin land and the old one remained fallow for many years.
questions.explanation: Since in Jhum Cultivation forests land was cultivated it covered a vast area and not small patches of land.

questions.question: What is the literal meaning of the word fallow?

options.html: A field left uncultivated for a while so that the soil recovers fertility
A luxuriant land with a lot of crops cultivated on it

questions.explanation: The word fallow means inactive. It is a practice of leaving a land uncultivated for a season so that it regains its nutrients that are sucked up due to over plantation.

questions.question:
Lists of statements with respect to tribal life are given below. Choose the one that does not pertain to the tribal life.

  1. The tribal groups lived by hunting animals and gathering forest produce.
  2. The tribals saw forests as essential for survival.
  3. Most of the tribals ate fruits and roots collected from the forest and cooked food with the oil extracted from the seeds.
  4. The tribals always migrated and hence did not have a permanent forest habitat.
questions.explanation: The life of tribals revolves around forests. The forests them habitat, shelter, raw materials for household equipment, medicinal herbs, firewood, fodder, etc. The life and economy of the tribals are intimately related so they did not migrate often but had permanent habitat in the forests.

questions.question: From the seeds of which tree did the tribals extract oil to cook?
questions.explanation: The oil extracted from seeds of Sal is edible oil. Sal is the major means of survival for forest dwellers in the Central Indian States so the tribals extracted oil from the seeds of Sal Tree.

questions.question: From the seeds of Sal and Mahua, the tribal extract oil to cook. What exactly is Mahua?

options.html: A flower that is eaten or used to make alcohol

questions.explanation: Mahua flower is edible and is a food item for the tribals. They are fermented to produce alcoholic country liquor too.

questions.question:
Why were the Baigas of Central India reluctant to do work for others?

  1. Since the Baigas were money lenders, they thought lending money was more profitable.
  2. Since Baigas were tribal chieftains, they thought they cannot work as labourers.
  3. Since Baigas saw themselves as people of the forest, they thought it below their dignity to work as a labourer.
  4. Since Baigas only reared cattle, they thought they cannot work as labourers.
questions.explanation: The Baigas were hunters and gatherers who could only live on the forest produce. They claimed to be harbingers of the human race and hence were reluctant to work for others.

questions.question: Which part of India did the Khonds community live?
questions.explanation: Khonds are an ethnic group and indigenous tribal people of India. They originated from Odisha and are also found in Bihar, Andhra Pradesh, Chattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and West Bengal.

questions.question: The tribal chiefs got land titles in central India under the ________ land settlements.
questions.explanation: Under British rule, the functions and powers of the tribal chiefs changed considerably. They were allowed to keep their land titles over a cluster of villages and rent outlands.

questions.question: According to the list given below, who were the Van Gujjars of the Punjab hills?
questions.explanation: The Van Gujjars of Punjab hills were Cattle herders. They were a pastoral semi-nomadic community.

questions.question: From the given number of options, choose the one that can be best tells us about the Gaddis of Kulu.
questions.explanation: Gaddis is a sizeable population of Tribes in Himachal Pradesh. The main occupation of the tribe is sheep grazing. So the option Shepherds is correct.

questions.question: What did the Bakarwals of Kashmir rear?
questions.explanation: The Bakarwals of Kashmir are the pastoralists of Jammu and Kashmir and they herd goat and sheep.

questions.question: Which one of the following best describes the Labadis of Andhra Pradesh?
questions.explanation: The Labadie of Andhra Pradesh were pastoralists and they moved with their herd of cattle or sheep. So Shepherds is the correct option.

questions.question:
What did the Tribal groups do when they wanted to buy goods not produced in their locality?

  1. Tribals went to another locality worked there as labourers in return for the goods.
  2. Like a barter system, Tribals bought goods they wanted in return for the goods they produced.
  3. Tribals did not buy such goods at all.
  4. Tribals bought goods for higher prices from traders and money lenders gave loan for this.
questions.explanation: Tribal groups often needed to buy and sell in order to be able to get the goods that were not produced in their locality. Traders came around with things for sale and sold goods at high prices. Money Lenders gave loans with which the tribals met their cash needs, adding to what they earned. But the interest charged on loans was usually very high.

questions.question: What does the word dikus refer to in the Chhota Nagpur region?
questions.explanation: When the Europeans first started ruling villages, the local tribes called them Dikus. This term was earlier used for merchants, businessmen, or peasants who were not part of the tribe. Hence Dikus were referred to as outsiders.

questions.question: Why did the tribals saw the moneylenders and traders as evil outsiders and the cause for their misery?

options.html: Tribals thought themselves as forest people and hence did not want any money lender or trader to interfere in their affairs
Tribals often gave the goods they produced to the traders and hence they were without food most of the time
Tribals had to often buy goods from the traders at higher price with the money given as a loan by the moneylenders and they were always in debt and poverty
Tribals had to compulsorily work for the traders and money lenders as their labourers

questions.explanation: The subsequent dependence of tribals on traders to buy goods that were not produced within the locality at a higher price and the moneylenders for their cash needs in the form of loans for which the interest charged was very high led to indebtedness and poverty. The tribals understood the consequences of what was happening and why it was happening and hence saw the market and traders as their main enemies.

questions.question:
Identify the person based on the clues given below:

  1. He was a British anthropologist
  2. A tribal activist, he lived among the Baigas and Khonds of central India for many years
  3. He is married to a Raj Khond tribal girl and later to a Pardhan tribe girl from Chattisgarh.
questions.explanation: The clues are given identities to be Better Elwin who came to India as a Missionary. He came out with numerous works on various tribal groups in India the best acclaimed being those on Maria and Baigas.

questions.question: According to Verrier Elwin, which tribal group were the best hunters?
questions.explanation: The answer is correct because Verrier Elwin in his Notes on Khonds have mentioned that the Baigas were the best hunters.

questions.question: Carefully observe the image and identify to which group of tribals did the tribal girl belong to ? These tribal children used to go with their mothers to the forest to gather forest produce.
questions.explanation: There can be no explanation for the picture

questions.question:
What does the term Bewar refer to?

  1. A term used in Madhya Pradesh for shifting cultivation.
  2. A term used to refer to a particular tribal community in Madhya Pradesh.
  3. A term used to refer to particular forest produce by the tribal community of Madhya Pradesh.
  4. A tribal chieftain of Madhya Pradesh.
questions.explanation: Faced with crop losses because of erratic rainfall and extreme weather, tribal farmers of Madhya Pradesh turn to Beware form of cultivation which involves no cost or loan, are less laborious, and yield more nutritious and varied food than conventional cultivation. So Shifting cultivation is correct.

questions.question:
Complete the following with respect to the tribals of India.
The British officers saw settled tribal groups like the ________ and Santhals as more civilized than hunter-gatherers or shifting cultivators.
questions.explanation: The British considered the Honda and Santhal tribes to be civilized than the other hunters and gatherers tribes as they were settled cultivators and we're easier to control and administer than people who were always on move.

questions.question: To which area did the Mundas group belong to?
questions.explanation: The Munda's originated in the Chottanagpur plateau region.

questions.question:
A few changes that occurred to the tribals during the British rule are given below. Pick out the one that is not true.

  1. The tribal chiefs were allowed to fulfil the traditional functions.
  2. The tribal chiefs lost their administrative power.
  3. The tribal chiefs were forced to follow laws made by the British authorities.
  4. The tribal chiefs had to pay tribute to the British and discipline the tribal groups on behalf of the British.
questions.explanation: The tribal chiefs were not allowed to fulfil their traditional functions by the British as they were allowed to keep their land titles but they lost much of their administrative power and were forced to follow laws made by the British officials in India. They also had to pay tribute to the British and discipline the tribal groups on behalf of the British. They lost the authority they had earlier enjoyed amongst their people.

questions.question: The image given below shows tribal women Bhil, cultivating on a forest. In which state of India do you find the Bhil group of people?
questions.explanation: The Bhils racially belonging to the proto Australoid group are found in the states of Gujarat, Rajasthan, Maharashtra,Goa and the Union Territories of Daman and Diu and Dadra and Nagar Haveli.

questions.question:
Why was settled plough cultivation not easy in certain areas?

  1. In some areas of the forest, there was soil erosion and hence not suitable for cultivation.
  2. In some areas, water was scarce and soil dry and hence plough cultivation suffered.
  3. Use of direct drilling techniques in some areas reduced the plough cultivation produce.
  4. In some areas, plough cultivation increased the nitrogen content and hence not suitable.
questions.explanation: Settled plough cultivation was not possible in certain areas due to lack of sufficient land and water for irrigation.

questions.question:
Why did the British introduce Land settlements?

  1. The British wanted a regular revenue for the state.
  2. The British wanted to control the Landowners.
  3. The British wanted to increase their trade.
  4. The British wanted to keep a check on the traditional practices.
questions.explanation: After acquiring the Diwani or control the revenues of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa in 1665, the British continued the old system of revenue collection through zamindar. In 1773 it decided to collect revenues directly. But this experiment was not successful. It introduced instability in the Company's revenues. Neither the ryots nor the zamindar would do anything to improve cultivation when they did not know what the next year's assessment would be or who would be the next year's revenue collector. The British then decided to introduce Land Settlement which could extract more and regular revenues.

questions.question:
How did the British introduce the Land Settlement?

  1. The British asked fixed the revenue to the Landowners without measuring.
  2. The British fixed a ceiling to the cultivators and collected from their produce.
  3. The British fixed the revenue proportional to the produce from the land.
  4. The British measured the land, defined the rights of each individual to that land, and fixed the revenue demand for the state.
questions.explanation: The British inherited the institutional form of the agrarian system from the Mughals. They superimposed a system over the existing pattern in tune with British customs and laws relating to land revenue. The revenue system under Sam nears was arbitrary, not fixed, and plagued with uncertainty. So the Company fixed the rates of revenues, made the zamindar the authority over land and revenue. At places where there was no zamindar, the peasants were made the owners of land and they directly paid the revenue to the Company in cash or kind. At other places, the Group of villages were responsible for revenue payment to the Company.  Thus the British measured the land, defined rights to each individual to that land, and fixed the revenue demand for that state.

questions.question: The Jhum cultivators in Northeast India insisted on continuing with their traditional practice, they were allowed the right to carry on shifting cultivation in some parts of the forest. Who allowed the Jhum cultivators to carry on shifting cultivation?
questions.explanation: The British were not able to control Shifting Cultivation completely. Water was scarce in many areas and the tribals had to move from place to place so that they could grow crops where water was available. So the Jhum cultivators in north-east India insisted on continuing with their traditional practice. Facing widespread protests the British had to allow them the right to carry on shifting cultivation in some parts of the forests.

questions.question: What is the other word that can be used for thekedars?
questions.explanation: The word thekedar in Hindi means a contractor. As the landlords were the contractor of collecting revenues from the peasants on behalf of the British they were also called thekedar.

questions.question: Complete the missing link with respect to the tribal lives. The life of the tribals was directly connected to the forest, so changes in ________ had a considerable effect on tribal lives.
questions.explanation: The lives of the tribals revolve around the forests. They were directly connected to the forests and were free to move about in the forests and use forests to produce for their livelihood. When the British imposed the Forest Laws they extended control over all forests, the tribals were not allowed to move freely practise shifting cultivation, collect fruits a or hunt animals. So the changes in Forest laws had a considerable effect on tribal lives.

questions.question:
Why were some forests classified as Reserved Forests by the British?

  1. These forests were reserved only to the tribals.
  2. The produce of these forests was not sold at all.
  3. These forests produced the timber that the British wanted.
  4. The produce of these forests was only exported by the British without any price.
questions.explanation: The British classified certain Forests as Reserved Forests because such forests were under the direct supervision of the Government where no public entry was allowed for collecting timber and grazing cattle.

questions.question:
Some statements related to the Reserved Forests are given below. Pick out the one that is not true pertaining to Reserved Forests.

  1. In these forests, people were allowed to move freely.
  2. Some forests were classified as Reserved Forests as they produced timber which the British wanted.
  3. In these forests, people were not allowed to practice Jhum cultivation.
  4. In these forests, people were not allowed to collect fruits or hunt animals.
questions.explanation: The Reserved Forests were under the direct supervision of the British Government and no public entry was allowed to collect timber and grazing animals, Jhum cultivation or collect fruits. The movement was thus restricted.

questions.question:
How did the tribals react to the Forest Laws?

  1. They disobeyed the new rules, continued with practices that were declared illegal and at times rebelled.
  2. They accepted the new rules and did not rebel.
  3. They helped the British to destruct their livelihood.
  4. The tribal chiefs discussed and advised the British to change the laws.
questions.explanation: The Forest Laws evoked popular discontent among the tribals and they disobeyed the laws. They took up arms against the British, started a rebellion, killed the zamindar and outsiders. They strongly resented to British intrusion and disrespect of their religious beliefs and customs.

questions.question: In Jharkhand, which tribal group rebelled against the British?
questions.explanation: In Jharkhand, the Santhals rebelled against the British. This insurrection was the consequence of the Permanent Settlement of 1793. The Santhals were obliged to pay all kinds of taxes and levies. They were disposed of their property. They were abused. The representatives of Zamindars inflicted personal violence on the Santhals. The rich peasant trespassed on the land of the tenants. They took away their cattle. The moneylenders who came to be known as Dikus charged interest which was incredibly high. The Europeans who were employed in Bihar for railroad construction forced abduction of Santhal women and oppressed the men. The zamindars, moneylenders, traders, and European employees this oppressed the Santhals to such an extent that they took to insurrection.

questions.question: Who led the Santhals in their rebellion against the British?
questions.explanation: The Santhal Rebellion was led by Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu. On June 30, 1855, Sidhu Murmu had accumulated ten thousand Santhals at Bhagnani Digital and rose in arms against the inequities of the British revenue system. The Santhals under the leadership of Sidhu assembled to run a parallel Government against the British government. The basic purpose was to collect taxes by making their own laws.

questions.question: What was the tribal revolt in Assam in 1906 called?
questions.explanation: The Songram Sangma was an open rebellion against the British by the tribals in Assam in 1906. The tribals protested against the forest laws.

questions.question: Where in Assam was the most notable agitation of the tribals against the British in 1894?
questions.explanation: The peasants of Assam burdened under the opium Monopoly and the usury by moneylenders rose against revolt. Numerous peasants decided against paying the taxes. The protests culminated in a bayonet charge against the protesters at Pathuraghat in 1894. In the violent repression that followed villagers were tortured and their properties destroyed or looted.

questions.question: The British described the tribal people as ________.
questions.explanation: The British described the tribal people as Wild and Savage.

questions.question: Under whose leadership did the tribals rebel for imposing taxes on the Adivasis.
questions.explanation: U Kiang Nangbah was a patriotic Jaintia tribe leader who Ignited the fire of mass movement against the British.

questions.question: Apart from U Kiang Nangbah, in Meghalaya, who supported the tribals and tried to confront the British by using crude weapons, but was ultimately defeated by the British?
questions.explanation: Pa Togan Samman was a Garo tribe leader who led the local Garo tribe warriors against the British troops. The Garo warriors fell in an unmatched battle since the British had access to advance warfare armaments.

questions.question: Cocoons were bought from Santhals and sold by the traders at ________ times the purchase price.
questions.explanation: The traders dealing in silk sent in their agents who gave loans to the tribal people and collected the cocoons. The growers were paid Rs 3 to Rs 4 for a thousand cocoons. These were then exported to Burdwan or Gaya where they were sold at five times the price.

questions.question: Name the leader who advised the tribals for a non-violent protest. He also advised people to give up animal sacrifice, drinking, and other social evils.
questions.explanation: Birsa's movement was aimed at reforming tribal society. He urged the Mundas to give up drinking liquor, clean their village, and stop believing in witchcraft and sorcery.

questions.question: In which area in the present Jharkand the Santhals reared cocoons?
questions.explanation: Hazaribagh in the present-day Jharkhand was the place where the traders, the Santhals reared cocoons.

questions.question: What was the price given to the Santhals for rearing a thousand cocoons?
questions.explanation: The Santhals who reared cocoons were paid ₹ 3 to 4 for 1,000 cocoons and the middlemen who exported it made huge profits by selling it vat 5 times the price.

questions.question:
Why were the tribals recruited in large numbers to work in Assam and Jharkhand?

  1. To work in the forest of Assam and Jharkhand.
  2. To work in the tea plantations of Assam and the coal mines of Jharkhand.
  3. To work in lands of Assam and Jharkand and cultivate the spices.
  4. To help in conquering the hill territories of Assam and Jharkhand.
questions.explanation: From the late nineteenth century, tea plantations started coming u, and mining became an important industry. Tribals were recruited in large numbers to work in tea plantations of Assam and coal mines of Jharkhand. They were recruited through contractors who paid them miserably low wages and prevented them from returning home.

questions.question: Birsa had a vision of bringing a ________ age.
questions.explanation: Deeply influenced by many of the ideas he came in touch with, Birsa had a vision of bringing a golden age.

questions.question:
By the mid 18th century, what actually happened to the Nawabs and Rajas in India?

  1. They lost their authority and honour, residents stationed in many kingdoms.
  2. They became more popular and extended their kingdom.
  3. The East India Company started constructing its empire with the help of Nawabs and Rajas.
  4. Many Nawabs and Rajas were dead after a major war with the British.
questions.explanation: In the mid 18th century, the powers of Nawabs, Rajas, zamindar, etc., were eroded. The freedom of Indian rulers was reduced, their at forces were disbanded, and their revenue and territories were taken by stages. Residents had been stationed in many courts by the British as their representatives. These residents kept informing the governors about the important developments in every kingdom. So the Indians lost their authority and honour, residents in many kingdoms.

questions.question:
Which one of the following best describes a Resident with respect to British India?

  1. A senior British Government administrative official positioned in a local place and controlling the happenings of the kingdom.
  2. A local landlord to keep a check on the land revenue.
  3. A local money lender collecting revenue.
  4. An official who administers an oath to the soldiers.
questions.explanation: The British Resident, also known as Political Agents of British East India Company, was a government official who took up residence in a princely state and conducted several official diplomatic functions that aided in the British indirect rule.

questions.question:
The attached picture shows the statue of a great personality kept in Solapur, Maharashtra. Identify her from the given clues:
She is from the kingdom of Jhansi
She wanted the East India Company to recognize her adopted son as the heir to the kingdom
questions.explanation: The picture is of Rani Laxmi Bai of Jhansi an epitome of bravery and courage. She was the queen of Jhansi and since her child could not survive had adopted Damodar Rao. As per Hindu tradition he was the legal heir to the throne after her husband Gangadhar Rao, the Maharaja of Jhansi. However, the British refused to accept Damodar Rao as the legal heir. As per Doctrine of Lapse, Lord Dalhousie decided to seize the state of Jhansi but Rani Laxmi Bai fought with the British and tried till her last breath to save her kingdom. She died in her battle with the British. She is referred to as the 'Icon of the Indian Nationalist Movement '.

questions.question: Identify this personality in the attached image, pleaded with the British to give his father's pension when the latter died?
questions.explanation: The picture is of Nana Saheb the son of Baji Rao II, the Peshwa of Maratha Empire. Nana Saheb pleaded the British to entitle him the pension of his father after the latter died but to continue the same. As a result Nana Saheb joined the revolt of 1857 against the British.

questions.question: Name the ruler of Kanpur who called himself Peshwa.
questions.explanation: Nana Saheb, the adopted son of the late Peshwa Baji Rao who lived near Kanpur, gathered armed forces and expelled the British garrison from the city. He proclaimed himself Peshwa. He declared that he was a governor under Emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar.

questions.question:
Identify the kingdom based on the clues given

  1. One of the last territories to be annexed by the British
  2. A subsidiary alliance was imposed here on 1801 and later taken over in 1856 by the British
  3. Lord Dalhousie declared that the territory was being misgoverned and British rule was needed to ensure proper administration
questions.explanation: Awadh was an independent kingdom ruled by the Nawabs from the capital city of Lucknow. The British interest in Awadh began in 1760 due to its economic and political importance. In the battle of Buxar subsequent battles thereafter the British defeated the Nawabs of Awadh and took half of the kingdom through signing treaties. Since then the Nawabs of Awadh were dependants of the British. The Nawab of Awadh had many heirs and therefore could not be covered by the Doctrine of Lapse. In 1856 Lord Dalhousie through the imposition of Subsidiary Alliance annexed Awadh without any proper provocations and deposed Nawab Wajid Ali Shah accusing him of misgoverning his state and refusing to introduce any reforms.

questions.question: Name the Governor-General who declared that Awadh was being misgoverned and British rule was needed to ensure proper administration.
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie was the Governor-General of India when the Kingdom of Awadh was annexed. He implied the Subsidiary Alliance and annexed Awadh on grounds of misgoverning.

questions.question:
What did the East India Company do from the coins minted by the Company?

  1. The name of the British King was removed from the coins.
  2. The name of the Governor-general was removed from the coins.
  3. The name of the Mughal emperor was removed from the coins.
  4. The name India was removed from the coins.
questions.explanation: After the Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 the administration of British India was transferred from the East India Company to the British Circulation coins were minted under the direct authority of the Crown. The Gold Mohurs, silver coins, and fractional values which were previously struck in the name of the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II now changed in the name of Queen Victoria.

questions.question: What did Lord Dalhousie announce after the death of King Bahadur Shah Zafar?

options.html: The family members were imprisoned and the Company annexed the state of Delhi.
The family can continue staying in Red Fort and his son would be crowned as the next king.
The family of the king would be shifted out of the Red Fort and given another place to live in.
The British set fire to the royal palace and killed all the family members.

questions.explanation: After the death of Bahadur Shah Zafar Lord Dalhousie announced that his successors would not be permitted to use Red Fort as their palace and also that they would not be allowed to use imperial titles with their names.

questions.question: In 1856, which Governor-General decided that Bahadur Shah Zafar was the last Mughal King and none of his descendants would be recognized as Kings hereafter?
questions.explanation: In 1856, Lord Canning announced that after the death of Bahadur Shah Zafar, his successors would not be allowed to use imperial titles with their names.

questions.question:
Why did the countryside peasants and zamindars resent the British?

  1. They resented high taxes and the rigid methods of revenue collection and many lost the lands to the money lenders.
  2. They resented the Succession policy of the British.
  3. They resented the British colonization.
  4. They resented the racial discrimination policy of the British by denying high-rank jobs to Indians.
questions.explanation: The British imposed very high taxes and used strict methods to collect revenue. The peasants and zamindars resented the high taxes as they were unable to pay them. They had to borrow money from the moneylenders and eventually lost the lands they had tilted for generations. So they found the British system very oppressive.

questions.question: Looking at the image given, pick out from the given options the one that best describes the word Sepoy?

options.html: An money lender who help people in times of need
A British officer residing in a local place controlling the reigns of the kingdom for the Company
An Indian soldier who served under the British

questions.explanation: Sepoy was a native soldier employed by the East India Company to maintain order and defend trading centres. The Sepoys were generally under the command of the British officers.

questions.question: Which one of the following options is derived from the Persian word Sipah meaning infantry soldier?
questions.explanation: The term "Sepoy" comes from the Urdu word "Sipahi" which itself is derived from the Persian word "Sipah" meaning "army" or "horsemen".

questions.question: A few statements as to why the Indian sepoys were unhappy with the British. Pick out the one that is not true.

options.html: Due to the high taxes and rigid methods of revenue collection.
Due to the violation of their religious sensibilities and beliefs.
Due to revolt in the countryside.
Due to pay, allowances, and conditions of service.

questions.explanation: In the countryside, peasants and zamindars resented the high taxes and the rigid methods of revenue collection. Many failed to pay back their loans to the moneylenders and gradually lost the lands they had tilled for generations. This was a matter of concern for the peasants and not the Sepoys.

questions.question:
In 1824, when the sepoys were asked to go to Burma by sea route to fight for the Company, they refused to follow the order. Why?

  1. It was widely believed that they were taken as prisoners and will be put in jail there.
  2. It was believed that Burma was a non-friendly country and hence did not welcome foreigners.
  3. There was a belief that the sepoys will not be paid if they go to Burma.
  4. It was widely believed that if they crossed the sea they would lose their religion and caste.
questions.explanation: The Sepoys refused to go to Burma by sea-route because their religion forbade them to cross the " black water". They believed that if they did so their soul will go to hell after death. The conservative beliefs of the Sepoys were thus shaken and they reacted strongly.
