questions.question: What was the new law regarding the employment of Indian sepoys passed by the East India Company in 1856?

options.html: Had to collect the revenues set by the British

questions.explanation: During that era, there was a belief that if people crossed any sea they would lose their religion and caste. In 1824, the sepoys were told to go to Burma by the sea route to fight for the Company, they refused to follow the order, though they agreed to go by the land route. In 1856, the East India Company passed an Act that every new recruit in the army had to agree to serve overseas if required. They thought that their religion was being attacked by the British.

questions.question:
In what way did the Christian missionaries help the British East India Company? From the statements given below, choose the one that is not true with respect to the missionaries.

  1. The Christian missionaries did not encourage the education of women.
  2. The Christian missionaries converted the people as Christians.
  3. The Christian missionaries functioned freely and even owned land and property.
  4. An Indian converted to Christianity could inherit the property of ancestors.
questions.explanation: As a result of a new wave of Christian missionaries organised activity to have important consequences for female education.

questions.question: Who wrote the book Majha Pravaas?
questions.explanation: Majha Pravaas which translates into English "My Travels", the story of The Mutiny of 1857 is wrong Vishnubhat Godse depicting the incidents of the Mutiny of 1857.

questions.question: Who was Subedar Sitaram Pande?

options.html: A resident appointed by the British in the Bengal region.
The sepoy who attacked Lieutenant Baugh.
A sepoy who helped the British to suppress the rebellion.

questions.explanation: Sitaram Pande was one of the many Indian soldiers who helped the British to conquer India and thereafter to hold it. Sitaram Pande was recruited in 1812 as a sepoy in the Bengal Native Army. He served the English for 48 years and retired in 1860. He helped the British to suppress the rebellion though his own son who was a rebel was killed by the British in front of his eyes. On retirement, he was persuaded to write his memoirs (From Sepoy to Subedar).

questions.question: Name the book written by Sitaram Pande.
questions.explanation: Subedar Sitaram Pande completed the writing in 1861 in Awadhi and Norgate translated it into English and had it published under the title From Sepoy to Subedar.

questions.question: From the outline map of India , name the place A, where the on 9 May 1857, a group of soldiers mutinied and walked out of their military barracks and a rebellion started
questions.explanation: On 24th April, many Indian soldiers refused to accept the greased cartridges. On 9th of May 85 of them were dismissed, sentenced to 10 years of imprisonment and put in jail. This sparked of a general Mutiny among the Indian soldiers stationed at Meerut on 10th May, 1857.

questions.question: When soldiers as a group disobey their officers in the army then it is called a ________.
questions.explanation: Mutiny is a rebellion against the constituted authority by the soldiers. It is to rise against or refuse to obey the lawful authority in the military service.

questions.question:
A sequence of events that happened on 9 May 1857 are given below in a jumbled up order. Pick up the correct order from the given set of statements.

  1. Other soldiers stationed at Meerut revolted and British officers release the imprisoned soldiers.
  2. A group of soldiers mutinied and walked out of their military barracks.
  3. The released soldiers kill the British officers and after marching to Delhi proclaim the Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar as the emperor of India.
  4. The soldiers were dismissed and sentenced to 10 years imprisonment for refusing to use the greased cartridges.
questions.explanation: A group of soldiers mutinied and walked out of their military barracks. The soldiers were dismissed and sentenced to 10 years imprisonment for refusing to use the greased cartridges. Other soldiers stationed at Meerut revolted and British officers release the imprisoned soldiers. The released soldiers kill the British officers and after marching to Delhi proclaim the Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar as the emperor of India.

questions.question: Who led the revolt in Kanpur?
questions.explanation: In Kanpur the revolt was led by Nana Saheb, the adopted son of the last Peshwa Baji Rao II. Nana Saheb expelled the British from Kanpur with the help of the Sepoys and proclaimed himself as the Peshwa.

questions.question: Name the able commander in chief of Nana Saheb who led the revolt in Kanpur.
questions.explanation: Tantiya Tope was Maratha Brahmin in the service of former Peshwa Baji Rao II and his adopted son Nana Saheb. He was an intimate friend and the commander in chief of Nana Saheb's rebel forces and was present at Nana Saheb's massacre of the British colony in Kanpur.

questions.question: Recognise this able personality from Awadh who led the revolt from Lucknow along with Maulvi Ahmadullah.
questions.explanation: The picture is of Begum Hazrat Mahal, a great Indian freedom fighter who played a major role in India's first War of Independence (1857-58). She was the second wife of Nawab Wajid Ali Shah and when the British exiled the Nawab after the annexation of Awadh she took charge of the affairs of the state of Awadh. Combining strengths with the revolutionary forces of the First War of Independence, she was successful in seizing Lucknow from the British. She was named the Laxmi Bai of Awadh as she supported fellow mutineers and greatly motivated the masses to rebel against the British Raj.

questions.question: Among the following Residents of British India, who was forced to take refuge in a residency and killed by the forces of Begum Hazrat Mahal?
questions.explanation: During the mutiny of 1857, Begum Hazrat Mahal led the revolt from Lucknow. The British were led by their Resident, Sir Henry Lawrence. The sudden attack forced Sir Lawrence to take refuge in the Residency Building. However, the Begum and her forces followed him there too and Sir Lawrence was successfully killed in the attack.

questions.question: Name the Indian soldier who tried to rebel against the British at Barrackpore, but was arrested and hanged to death on 8th April 1857.
questions.explanation: Mangal Pandey was an Indian soldier who played a key role in the events immediately preceding the outbreak of the Indian rebellion of 1857. His attack on the British officers on March 29, 1857, in Barrackpore, was the first major incident of the Sepoy Mutiny. He was arrested and was hanged to death.

questions.question: The East India Company began to plan how to bring the Mughal dynasty to an end. In 1849, Governor-General Dalhousie announced the death of ________.
questions.explanation: In 1849, Governor-General Dalhousie announced that after the death of Bahadur Shah Zafar, the family of the king would be shifted out of the Red Fort and given another place in Delhi to reside in. Governor-General Canning decided that Bahadur Shah Zafar would be the last Mughal king and after his death, none of his descendants would be recognised as kings – they would just be called princes.

questions.question: Since the ________ century, nawabs and rajas had seen their power erode.
questions.explanation: Since the mid-eighteenth century, nawabs and rajas had seen their power erode. They had gradually lost their authority and honour.

tests.name: When People Rebel 1857 and After Test 03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History When People Rebel 1857 and After CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which one from the following options refers to the term Firangis?
questions.explanation: Firangi means foreigners particularly, British or white people. During the British Rule, the Indians called the white people firangi.

questions.question: From the names given below, which one is not called as an alternative to The Great Revolt of 1857.
questions.explanation: The revolt of 1857 or the Sepoy Mutiny is popularly known as the First War of Independence and not the Second War. It was the first War in which people from almost all parts of the country fought against the British of a single motive of Independence.

questions.question:
Given below are some of the important political causes of the Great revolt of 1857. Pick out the one that is not a political cause.

  1. Because of the implementation of the Doctrine of Lapse, Jhansi, Nagpur, and Satara were annexed.
  2. Nana Saheb was denied a pension of his father.
  3. Christian missionaries forcibly converted many people to Christianity.
  4. Lord Dalhousie announced that Bahadur Shah Zafar's family was to move out of Red fort after his death.
questions.explanation: Christianity is a religion and Christian missionaries were religious organisations and not political. The Christian missionaries forcibly converted the Indians to Christianity. It was their religious duty to spread their religion to other parts of the world. But we cannot deny the fact that they were a form of imperialism too. A country would send the missionaries who would convert natives of whatever foreign land it was and to make more and more people aware of their religious superiority. The British government had three roles in India, first of a trader, second of a ruler, and then of a Christian propagandist. Hence missionaries though not being political had a political influence also. The option though correct is not totally agreeable.

questions.question:
A few important causes of the Great Revolt of 1857 are given; select the one that is not an economic cause.

  1. The peasants had to pay high rates of land revenue to the British.
  2. Awadh was annexed due to maladministration.
  3. The British imposed a heavy duty on Indian goods.
  4. The livelihood of Indian artisans and craftsmen were affected as cheap machine-made goods were available.
questions.explanation: The annexation of Awadh on grounds of maladministration is political and not an economic cause of Revolt of 1857.

questions.question:
Some of the important causes of the Great Revolt of 1857 are given in the following statements, select the one that is not a socio-religious cause.

  1. The livelihood of Indian artisans and craftsmen were affected as cheap machine-made goods were available.
  2. Christian missionaries tried to convert forcibly many people to Christianity.
  3. Many social reforms like abolition of Sati, widow remarriage, encouraging women's education, etc hurt the feelings of Hindus.
  4. The introduction of Western education hurt the feelings of people.
questions.explanation: The effect on the livelihood of artisans and craftsmen due to the availability of cheap machine-made goods is an economic cause and not socio-religious cause.

questions.question: Which one of the following is considered as an immediate cause of the great revolt of 1857?

options.html: Annexation of Jhansi, Nagpur, Satara, etc under Doctrine of Lapse.
Hindu sentiments were hurt by the abolition of certain social practices, like Sati.
Issue of the cartridges (suspected of being coated with the fat of cows and pigs) used in the new Enfield rifle introduced by the British.
The livelihood of Indian artisans and craftsmen were affected as cheap machine-made goods were available.

questions.explanation: The government sent parties of men from each regiment to different garrisons for instructing the use of the new Enfield rifle. The soldiers had to bit off the cartridge before loading these rifles. Rumours had it that the cartridges were coated with the fat of cows and pigs. People thought that the English aimed to turn them all into Christians since both Muslims and Hindus would be defiled by using it. This infuriated the sepoys and became a major cause of the mutiny.

questions.question:
A few reasons for the failure of the revolt of 1857 are given below. Suggest the one that is not considered as a reason for the failure.

  1. The leaders were neither organized and united nor properly trained.
  2. They used outdated weapons.
  3. The Hindus and Muslims fought together even though the British tried to keep them divided.
  4. The educated class of the Indians were loyal to the British due to their selfish intentions.
questions.explanation: One major feature of the Revolt of 1857 was the remarkable unity between the Hindus and the Muslims. Sepoys and civilian rebels from both the communities fought side by side to take back their country from the alien rule and respected each other's religion. So the unity of both the communities cannot be a cause of failure of the Mutiny.

questions.question:
This was a major turning point in the history of India's freedom struggle. From the following list, which one would be the major turning point with special reference to the impact of the Revolt of 1857?

  1. The rise of a feeling of nationalism and unity among the people of all parts of India.
  2. The army was reorganized and new weapons replaced the outdated weapons.
  3. Governor-General was made the Viceroy.
  4. No interference in the religious practices of India by the British.
questions.explanation: The revolt of 1857 was the first great struggle of the Indians for freedom from British Imperialism. For the first time, people from all parts of the country united to fight for a common cause. It also heightened the sense of Indian nationalism.

questions.question: Who took over the administration in 1858 from the East India Company?
questions.explanation: After the Indian rebellion of 1857, the East India Company's rule was abolished in 1858. On 2nd August 1858, the British government assumed the task of administering India in the new British Raj by passing the Government of India Act. The Bill called for the dissolution of the British East India Company that was ruling India under the patronage of the Parliament and transfer of that power to the British Crown. So Queen Victoria took over the administration of India.

questions.question:
In 1858, Queen Victoria issued an important proclamation. Some of the important points covered in this are given below. Pick out the one that is not related to the Proclamation.

  1. Indian princes were given assurance that no further annexation will be made by the British.
  2. East India Company was abolished and India was directly under the control of the British Crown.
  3. The Proclamation abolished the Doctrine of Lapse.
  4. The proclamation did not pardon the rebels because they attacked the British.
questions.explanation: The Queen's Proclamation of 1858  granted a general amnesty (pardon) to the rebels except those who were directly involved in the killing of the British subjects.

questions.question: What was the new title given to the Governor-General?
questions.explanation: As the British Crown took over the administration a new title of Viceroy was introduced for the supreme representation of the British Government in India.

questions.question:
A few important changes in the Indian army are given below after the revolt of 1857. Choose the one that is not true.

  1. More number of soldiers were recruited from Gurkhas, Sikhs, and Pathans.
  2. The number of Indian soldiers was increased and Europeans soldiers reduced.
  3. Very few soldiers were recruited from Awadh, Bihar, Central, and South India.
  4. The army was reorganised and strengthened.
questions.explanation: The Indian army was carefully reorganised after 1858 to prevent the recurrence of another Revolt. Several steps were taken to minimise if not eliminate the capacity of the Indian soldiers to revolt. The proportion of Europeans to Indians was raised. So it would be incorrect to say that after the Sepoy Mutiny the number of Indian soldiers increased and Europeans decreased.

questions.question: The land and property of ________ was confiscated on a large scale and they were treated with suspicion and hostility.
questions.explanation: The land and property of the Muslims were confiscated on a large scale and they were treated with suspicion and hostility because the British believed that the Muslims were responsible for the rebellion in a big way.

questions.question:
From the outline map of India, identify this place marked in red :
This is situated in the Southern part of India, in the state of Tamil Nadu
There was Mutiny on 10 July 1806 where the mutineers broke into the fort and killed 200 British people.
questions.explanation: In 1806, the Indian soldiers were ordered to wear turbans and leather boots and were forbidden from wearing earrings or use holy ash on their forehead. On July 10, 1806 they revolted from the Fort of Vellore in South India but were brutally forced to subjugation by the British.

questions.question:
Identify the place marked B, from the outline map of India,
Nana Saheb led the revolt from this place
When British captured this place, Nana Saheb fled to Nepal
questions.explanation: When Nana Saheb the adopted son of Baji Rao II was refused the pension of his father after the latter's death he joined the Sepoy battalions at Kanpur in the rebellion in June 1857. He led the rebellion from Kanpur but was defeated and was driven into the Nepal hills.

questions.question: The image given below is an artists impression of an important port town of 17th century in the state of Andhra Pradesh today. But later as new ports were set up by the British in Bombay, Madras, Calcutta etc, this one lost its importance. Identify this port town. This port was also deurbanized during 19th century.
questions.explanation: This is the view of Machlipatnam, 1672 Machlipatnam developed as an important port town in the seventeenth century. Its importance declined by the late eighteenth century as trade shifted to the new British ports of Bombay, Madras and Calcutta. Cities such as Machlipatnam, Surat and Seringapatam were deurbanised during the nineteenth century.

questions.question: In the late 18th century, Calcutta, Bombay, and ________ rose in importance as Presidency cities and were the centres of British power.
questions.explanation: In the late eighteenth century, Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras rose in importance as Presidency cities. They became the centres of British power in the different regions of India. At the same time, a host of smaller cities declined. Many towns manufacturing specialised goods declined due to a drop in the demand for what they produced.

questions.question: Which were the three important Presidency regions set up by the British for administrative purposes?
questions.explanation: In the late eighteenth century, Calcutta, Bombay and Madras rose in importance as Presidency cities. They became the centres of British power in the different regions of India. At the same time, a host of smaller cities declined. Many towns manufacturing specialised goods declined due to a drop in the demand for what they produced. Old trading centres and ports could not survive when the flow of trade moved to new centres.

questions.question: From the following options, which one refers to De-urbanisation?

options.html: Migration of people from an urban area for economic or social reasons
The veto power of the administration in times of emergency
A feeling of unity among the people

questions.explanation: In the late eighteenth century, Calcutta, Bombay and Madras rose in importance as Presidency cities. They became the centres of British power in the different regions of India. Old trading centres and ports could not survive when the flow of trade moved to new centres. Similarly, earlier centres of regional power collapsed when local rulers were defeated by the British and new centers of administration emerged. This process is often described as de-urbanisation. Cities such as Machlipatnam, Surat and Seringapatam were deurbanised during the nineteenth century.

questions.question: What is Urbanisation?

options.html: The process of reorganization of the administrative setup
The process of annexing one state after the other
The process of demolishing a city
The process by which more and more people begin to reside in towns and cities

questions.explanation: Urbanisation is the process by which more and more people begin to reside in towns and cities. By the early twentieth century, only 11 percent of Indians were living in cities.

questions.question: From the list of options given below, which cities were de-urbanised during the 19th century?
questions.explanation: Earlier centres of regional power collapsed when local rulers were defeated by the British and new centres of administration emerged. This process is often described as de-urbanisation. Cities such as Machlipatnam, Surat, and Seringapatam were deurbanised during the nineteenth century.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, identify this place marked in red circle. This city began to grow when the East India Company stared using this place as its main port in Western India
questions.explanation: The Bombay port in the eighteenth century began to grow when the East India Company started using Bombay as its main port in western India. In the late eighteenth century, Calcutta, Bombay and Madras rose in importance as Presidency cities. They became the centres of British power in the different regions of India

questions.question: Name this historic imperial city that became a dusty provincial town in the 19th century before it was rebuilt as the capital of British India after 1912.
questions.explanation: In 1803, the British gained control of Delhi after defeating the Marathas. Since the capital of British India was Calcutta, the Mughal emperor was allowed to continue living in the palace complex in the Red Fort. The modern city as we know it today developed only after 1911 when Delhi became the capital of British India. The historic imperial city of Delhi became a dusty provincial town in the nineteenth century before it was rebuilt as the capital of British India after 1912

questions.question: Long ago, how many capital cities were founded in a small area on the left bank of the river Jamuna?
questions.explanation: Delhi has been the capital for more than 1,000 years, although with some gaps, as many as 14 capital cities were founded in a small area of about 60 square miles on the left bank of the river Jamuna. The remains of all other capitals may be seen on a visit to the modern city-state of Delhi.

questions.question: What is the tomb of a Sufi saint called?
questions.explanation: Delhi during Shah Jahan’s time was also an important centre of Sufi culture. It had several dargahs, Khanqahs, and Idgahs. Open squares, winding lanes, quiet cul-de-sacs, and water channels were the pride of Delhi’s residents. No wonder the poet Mir Taqi Mir said, “The streets of Delhi aren’t mere streets; they are like the album of a painter. A Dargah is the tomb of a Sufi saint.

questions.question: A Sufi lodge often used as a rest house for travellers and a place where people come to discuss spiritual matters, get the blessings of saints, and hear Sufi music. What is this place called?
questions.explanation: Delhi during Shah Jahan’s time was also an important centre of Sufi culture. It had several dargahs, Khanqahs, and Idgahs. Open squares, winding lanes, quiet cul-de-sacs, and water channels were the pride of Delhi’s residents. Khanqah is a Sufi lodge, often used as a rest house for travellers and a place where people come to discuss spiritual matters, get the blessings of saints, and hear Sufi music.

questions.question: What is an open prayer place of Muslims meant for Id prayers called?
questions.explanation: Delhi during Shah Jahan’s time was also an important centre of Sufi culture. It had several dargahs, Khanqahs, and Idgahs. Idgah is an open prayer place of Muslims primarily meant for id prayers.

questions.question: From the following list of options, what is a Street with a dead end called?
questions.explanation: Delhi during Shah Jahan’s time was also an important centre of Sufi culture. It had several dargahs, Khanqahs, and Idgahs. Dargah is the tomb of a Sufi saint. Cul-de-sac is a Street with a dead end.

questions.question: Which was the most splendid capital built by Shah Jahan?
questions.explanation: The most splendid capital of all was built by Shah Jahan. Shahjahanabad, it was begun in 1639 and consisted of a fort-palace complex and the city adjoining it. Lal Qila or the Red Fort, made of red sandstone, contained the palace complex. To its west lay the Walled City with 14 gates. The main streets of Chandni Chowk and Faiz Bazaar were broad enough for royal processions to pass. A canal ran down the centre of Chandni Chowk.

questions.question: Which building was made of red stone near Delhi, which was built as a palace fort in Shahjahanabad, also a residence of Mughal emperors of India?
questions.explanation: Shahjahanabad was begun in 1639 and consisted of a fort-palace complex and the city adjoining it. Lal Qila or the Red Fort, made of red sandstone, contained the palace complex. To its west lay the Walled City with 14 gates. The main streets of Chandni Chowk and Faiz Bazaar were broad enough for royal processions to pass. A canal ran down the centre of Chandni Chowk.

questions.question: What is the importance of Jama Masjid, situated in Delhi?

options.html: One of the important residences of the Mughal emperors of India
The office of archeological survey of India
One of the largest and grandest mosques in India
The office of the electorate of India

questions.explanation: Set amidst densely packed mohallas and several dozen bazaars, the Jama Masjid was among the largest and grandest mosques in India. There was no place higher than this mosque within the city then.

questions.question: The streets of Delhi aren't mere streets, they are the album of a painter. Name the poet who wrote these words?
questions.explanation: Delhi during Shah Jahan’s time was also an important centre of Sufi culture. It had several dargahs, Khanqahs, and Idgahs. Open squares, winding lanes, quiet cul-de-sacs, and water channels were the pride of Delhi’s residents. No wonder the poet Mir Taqi Mir said, “The streets of Delhi aren’t mere streets; they are like the album of a painter.”

questions.question: Which is this building in Delhi, supposed to be one of the finest pieces of architecture with minarets and full domes?
questions.explanation: Set amidst densely packed mohallas and several dozen bazaars, the Jama Masjid was among the largest and grandest mosques in India. There was no place higher than this mosque within the city then. The above shown picture is the eastern gate of the Jama Masjid in Delhi, by Thomas Daniell, 1795. This is also the first mosque in India with minarets and full domes.

questions.question: After defeating the ________, the British gained control of Delhi in 1803.
questions.explanation: In 1803, the British gained control of Delhi after defeating the Marathas. Since the capital of British India was Calcutta, the Mughal emperor was allowed to continue living in the palace complex in the Red Fort. The modern city as we know it today developed only after 1911 when Delhi became the capital of British India.

questions.question: Before Delhi was made the capital of India, which was the other city that was the capital of British India?
questions.explanation: In 1803, the British gained control of Delhi after defeating the Marathas. Since the capital of British India was Calcutta, the Mughal emperor was allowed to continue living in the palace complex in the Red Fort. The modern city as we know it today developed only after 1911 when Delhi became the capital of British India.

questions.question: In which year did Delhi become the capital of British India?
questions.explanation: In 1803, the British gained control of Delhi after defeating the Marathas. Since the capital of British India was Calcutta, the Mughal emperor was allowed to continue living in the palace complex in the Red Fort. The modern city as we know it today developed only after 1911 when Delhi became the capital of British India.

questions.question: What is the festival of flowers called?
questions.explanation: The life of Delhi depends on the Fort, Chandni Chowk, the daily gatherings at the Jamuna Bridge, and the Annual Gulfaroshan. Gulfaroshan is a festival of flowers.

questions.question: What term from the list given bolow can be given to the rebirth of art and living. It is often described as a period of high creativity.
questions.explanation: The establishment of the Delhi College in 1792 led to a great intellectual flowering in the sciences as well as the humanities, largely in the Urdu language. Many refer to the period from 1830 to 1857 as a period of the Delhi renaissance. Renaissance is literally the rebirth of art and learning. It is a term often used to describe a time when there is a great creative activity.

questions.question: What is the period from 1830 to 1857 generally referred to as with respect to Delhi?
questions.explanation: The establishment of the Delhi College in 1792 led to a great intellectual flowering in the sciences as well as the humanities, largely in the Urdu language. Many refer to the period from 1830 to 1857 as a period of the Delhi renaissance.

questions.question: When the angry lions entered the town, they killed helpless ??. And burned houses. Hordes on men and women, commoners and noblemen, poured out of Delhi from the three gates and took shelter in small communities and tombs outside the city ???... Who is the poet Ghalib referring to as angry lions here?
questions.explanation: Mirza Ghalib referred it to the British as he perceived the gradual demolition of Delhi in the hands of blood-hungry the British troops.

questions.question: Where did the British exile Bahadur Shah Zafar?
questions.explanation: To prevent another rebellion, the British exiled Bahadur Shah to Burma (now Myanmar), dismantled his court, razed several of the palaces, closed down gardens and built barracks for troops in their place.

questions.question: Name the mosque converted into a bakery by the British?
questions.explanation: The British wanted a clear ground for security reasons. Mosques in particular were either destroyed or put to other uses. For instance, the Zinatal-Masjid was converted into a bakery. One-third of the city was demolished, and its canals were filled up.

questions.question: For how many years no worship was allowed at Jama Masjid in Delhi?
questions.explanation: The British wanted a clear ground for security reasons. Mosques in particular were either destroyed or put to other uses. For instance, the Zinatal-Masjid was converted into a bakery. No worship was allowed in the Jama Masjid for five years. One-third of the city was demolished.

questions.question: How was the western walls of Shahjahanabad broken?

options.html: The western walls were broken as the Parliament house was to be constructed there
The western wall was broken as the British Governor Genreal wanted to construct his residential suite here
The western wall was broken by a fierce battle between the British and the rebels
The western wall was broken to establish the railway and to allow the city to expand beyond the walls

questions.explanation: In the 1870s, the western walls of Shahjahanabad were broken to establish the railway and to allow the city to expand beyond the walls. The British now began living in the sprawling Civil Lines area that came up in the north, away from the Indians in the Walled City. The Delhi College was turned into a school and shut down in 1877.

questions.question: Over 100,000 Indian princes and British officers gathered at this place. In the image given. What was the occasion for this Durbar?

options.html: Announcement of India's first Prime Minister
Announcement that the East India Company was abolished
The Coronation Durbar of King George V
Announcement of the end of British rule in India

questions.explanation: In 1911, when King George V was crowned in England, a Durbar was held in Delhi to celebrate the occasion. The decision to shift the capital of India from Calcutta to Delhi was announced at this Durbar.In the Coronation Durbar of 12 December, 1911 Over 100,000 Indian princes and British officers and soldiers gathered at the Durbar.

questions.question: What was the important decision announced in 1911 when a durbar was held in Delhi to celebrate the occasion of crowning of King George V?

options.html: Announcement of the end of British Rule in India
Announcement that the Governor-General will be hereafter called Viceroy
Announcement that the East India Company continued its rule in India
Shifting of capital of India from Calcutta to Delhi

questions.explanation: In 1911, when King George V was crowned in England, a Durbar was held in Delhi to celebrate the occasion. The decision to shift the capital of India from Calcutta to Delhi was announced at this Durbar.

questions.question: Which city was constructed as a 10 square mile city on Raisina Hill, south of the existing city?
questions.explanation: New Delhi was constructed as a 10-square-mile city on Raisina Hill, south of the existing city. Two architects, Edward Lutyens and Herbert Baker were called on to design New Delhi and its buildings. The government complex in New Delhi consisted of a two-mile avenue, Kingsway (now Rajpath), that led to the Viceroy’s Palace (now Rashtrapati Bhavan), with the Secretariat buildings on either side of the avenue.

questions.question: Who were the two important architects who were given the responsibility to design New Delhi and its buildings?

options.html: Christopher Charles Benniger and Joseph Allen Stein

questions.explanation: New Delhi was constructed as a 10-square-mile city on Raisina Hill, south of the existing city. Two architects, Edward Lutyens and Herbert Baker were called on to design New Delhi and its buildings. The government complex in New Delhi consisted of a two-mile avenue. Kingsway (now Rajpath), that led to the Viceroy’s Palace (now Rashtrapati Bhavan), with the Secretariat buildings on either side of the avenue.

questions.question: Name this important masterpiece of architecture in Delhi
questions.explanation: New Delhi was constructed as a 10-square-mile city on Raisina Hill, south of the existing city. Two architects, Edward Lutyens and Herbert Baker, were called on to design New Delhi and its buildings. The government complex in New Delhi consisted of a two-mile avenue,Kingsway (now Rajpath), that led to the Viceroy’s Palace (now Rashtrapati Bhavan), with the Secretariat buildings on either sides of the avenue.

questions.question: Even though the features of the important structures were borrowed from the different periods of India's imperial history, the overall look was from ________.
questions.explanation: The features of the government buildings were borrowed from different periods of India’s imperial history, but the overall look was Classical Greece (fifth century BCE). For instance, the central dome of the Viceroy’s Palace was copied from the Buddhist stupa at Sanchi, and the red sandstone and carved screens or jalis were borrowed from Mughal architecture.

questions.question: From where was the central dome of the Viceroy's Palace copied?
questions.explanation: The government complex in New Delhi consisted of a two-mile avenue,Kingsway (now Rajpath), that led to the Viceroy’s Palace (now Rashtrapati Bhavan), with the Secretariat buildings on either sides of the avenue. The features of these government buildings were borrowed from different periods of India’s imperial history, but the overall look was Classical Greece (fifth century BCE). For instance, the central dome of the Viceroy’s Palace was copied from the Buddhist stupa at Sanchi, and the red sandstone and carved screens or jalis were borrowed from Mughal architecture.

questions.question: Why did the architect make sure that the Viceroy's Palace was higher than Shah Jahan's Jama Masjid?

options.html: To assert the beauty of Classical Greece
To show the different periods of India's imperial history

questions.explanation: The features of these government buildings were borrowed from different periods of India’s imperial history, but the overall look was Classical Greece (fifth century BCE). For instance, the central dome of the Viceroy’s Palace was copied from the Buddhist stupa at Sanchi, and the red sandstone and carved screens or jalis were borrowed from Mughal architecture. But the new buildings had to assert British importance, that is why the architect made sure that the Viceroy’s Palace was higher than Shah Jahan’s Jama Masjid.

questions.question: How many years (approximately) did it take to build New Delhi?
questions.explanation: New Delhi took nearly 20 years to build. The idea was to build a city that was a stark contrast to Shahjahanabad. There were to be no crowded mohallas, no mazes of narrow bylanes. In New Delhi, there were to be broad, straight streets lined with sprawling mansions set in the middle of large compounds. The architects wanted New Delhi to represent a sense of law and order, in contrast to the chaos of Old Delhi.

questions.question:
A few facts related to the idea of building the city of New Delhi are given below. Analyse the statements and pick out from the options the correct one.

  1. It took approximately 20 years to build this important city
  2. The idea was to build a city in stark contrast to Shahjahanabad where there were no crowded mohallas, no narrow streets, etc
  3. The architects wanted New Delhi to represent a sense of law and order, in contrast to the chaos of Delhi with greenery all around
  4. New Delhi had a better water supply, sewage disposal, and good hygienic conditions
questions.explanation: It took approximately 20 years to build this important city. The idea was to build a city in stark contrast to Shahjahanabad where there were no crowded mohallas, no narrow streets, etc. The architects wanted New Delhi to represent a sense of law and order, in contrast to the chaos of Delhi with greenery all around. New Delhi had a better water supply, sewage disposal, and good hygienic conditions.

questions.question: Most of the structures designed by Edward Lutyens was in ________.
questions.explanation: New Delhi was constructed as a 10-square-mile city on Raisina Hill, south of the existing city. Two architects, Edward Lutyens and Herbert Baker were called on to design New Delhi and its buildings. Most of the structures designed by Edward Lutyens was in Red Stone.

questions.question: From the following list of options, which one refers to Trellised Windows?
questions.explanation: The Trellised window is a framework split into a number of panels. It is a crossbarred work which looks like a net. So Jalis is correctly explained.

questions.question: In which year did the partition of India happen?
questions.explanation: The Partition of India in 1947 led to a massive transfer of populations on both sides of the new border. As a result, the population of Delhi swelled, the kinds of jobs people did change, and the culture of the city became different. Days after Indian Independence and Partition, fierce rioting began.

questions.question: New colonies like Lajpat Nagar and Tilak Nagar came up in this city. Which city are we talking about?
questions.explanation: At the same time, Delhi became a city of refugees. Nearly 500,000 people were added to Delhi’s population. New colonies such as Lajpat Nagar and Tilak Nagar came up at this time. Shops and stalls were set up to cater to the demands of the migrants; schools and colleges were also opened.

questions.question: Which one of the following refer to the correct meaning of a Baoli?
questions.explanation: In the past, Mughal Delhi’s famed canals had brought not only fresh drinking water to homes but also water for other domestic uses. This excellent system of water supply and drainage was neglected in the nineteenth century. The system of wells (or Baolis) also broke down, and channels to remove household waste (called effluents) were damaged.

questions.question: Name the grand mansions of the Mughal aristocracy in the 17th and 18th century?
questions.explanation: In the 17th and 18t century Havelis were symbolic of Mughal's prestige and power. They were very huge mansions.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Mechanized production of which textiles made Britain the foremost industrial nation in the 19th century?
questions.explanation: The development of cotton industries in Britain affected textile producers in India. Indian textiles had to compete with British textiles in the European and American markets.

questions.question: When its ________ industry started growing from the 1850s, Britain came to be known as the workshop of the world.
questions.explanation: Britain was often referred to as the 'Workshop of the world' as it is iron and steel industry started growing from the 1850's and later had success. So many started buying and selling goods from Britain so it was given the title of the workshop of the world.

questions.question: Around 1750, before the British conquered Bengal, India was by far the world's largest producer of this. Which one of the following options will replace the term this here?
questions.explanation: Indian textiles had long been renowned both for their fine quality and exquisite craftsmanship. They were exclusively traded in Southeast Asia and West and Central Asia.

questions.question: Why were the Indian textiles popular?

options.html: By its fine quality and exquisite craftsmanship

questions.explanation: Indian textiles were extensively traded in Southeast Asia and West and Central Asia. From the sixteenth-century European trading companies began buying Indian textiles for sale in Europe.

questions.question: The image given below is a particular type of weave that was woven in Surat, Ahmedabad and Patan. This became a part of the local weaving tradition later. Name this type of weave
questions.explanation: Patola weave came into existence in mid-nineteenth century. It was woven in Surat, Ahemadabad and Patan. It was highly valued in Indonesia. It became a part of the local weaving tradition there.

questions.question: In which place in present-day Iraq did the European trader's first encountered fine cotton from India carried by Arab merchants?
questions.explanation: European traders first encountered fine cotton cloth from India carried by Arab merchants in Mosul in present-day Iraq. So they began referring to all fine woven textiles as "muslin"-a word that acquired wide currency.

questions.question: Which one of the following does Muslin refer to?
questions.explanation: A cotton fabric made in various degrees of fineness and often printed, woven or embroidered in patterns, especially a cotton fabric of plain weave, used for sheets and for a variety of other purposes.

questions.question: It is believed that the word calico is derived from the name of a particular place in Kerala. Identify the place.
questions.explanation: When the Portuguese first came to India in search of spices they landed in Calicut on the Kerela coast in South-West India. The cotton textiles which they took back to Europe, along with the spices, came to be called calico and subsequently calico became the general name for all cotton textiles.

questions.question: The East India Company sent to its representatives an order book in 1730 with a list. How many varieties of cotton and silk orders were in that order book?
questions.explanation: The order in the year 1730 was for 5,89,000 pieces of cloth. Browsing through the order book we would find a list of 98 varieties of cotton and silk cloths. These were known by their common name in the European trade as piece goods-usually woven cloth pieces that were 20 yards long and 1 yard wide.

questions.question: From where did the English word chintz derive from?
questions.explanation: Chintz is derived from the Hindi word chhint, a cloth with small and colourful flowery designs. From the 1680s there started a craze for printed Indian cotton textiles in England and Europe mainly for their exquisite floral designs, fine texture, and relative cheapness.

questions.question: The Europeans started using the term Chintz, derived from the Hindi word Chhint. What is the meaning of Chhint?

options.html: An order ready to be shipped to Britain
A cloth with small and colourful flowery designs

questions.explanation: Chintz was originally glazed calico textiles, initially specifically those imported from India, printed with designs featuring flowers and other patterns in different colours, typically on a light plain background.

questions.question: Observe the image and identify the name of the special decorative motif.
questions.explanation: Jamdani weave of early twentieth century, was a fine muslinon which decorative motifs are woven on the loom, typically in grey and white. Often a mixture of cotton and gold thread was used.

questions.question: Among the following, which place in Bengal was the important centre of Jamdani weaving?
questions.explanation: The most important centres of jamdani weaving were South Rupshi, Narayanganj, Dacca in Bengal, and Lucknow in the United Provinces.

questions.question: Among the following, which place in Uttar Pradesh was the important centre of Jamdani weaving?
questions.explanation: The most important centres of jamdani weaving were Dacca in Bengal and Lucknow in the United Provinces.

questions.question: Bandanna is derived from the word bandhana. Which one would be the most appropriate meaning of the word Bandanna?

options.html: A bright coloured scarf for the neck or head

questions.explanation: The term bandanna derived from the word bandhana (Hindi for tying) and referred to a variety of brightly coloured cloth produced through a method of tying and dying.

questions.question: By the early 18th century, who were worried about the Indian cotton textiles and popularity in England and started protesting?

options.html: Wool and Silk makers in India
Wool and Silk makers in England

questions.explanation: At this time textile industries had just begun to develop in England. Unable to compete with Indian textiles, English producers wanted to secure the market within the country by preventing the entry of Indian textiles.

questions.question: Name the legislation in 1720 banning the use of printed cotton textiles chintz in England.
questions.explanation: Calico Act was a precursor to the industrial revolution when Britain eventually surpassed India as the worlds leading textile manufacturer in the 19th century.

questions.question: Name the machine that increased the productivity of the traditional spindles in the textile industry.
questions.explanation: In 1764, the spinning jenny was invented by John Kaye which increased the productivity of the traditional spindles. A spinning jenny was a machine by which a single worker could operate several spindles on which thread was spun.

questions.question: Name the person who invented Spinning Jenny?
questions.explanation: John Kaye constructed the first known frame and is known to be the inventor of the spinning frame. His spinning jenny increased the productivity of the traditional spindles.

questions.question: Identify this person who revolutionized cotton textile weaving with the invention of the steam engine?
questions.explanation: Richard Arkwright invented steam engine in 1786 and revolutionised cotton textile weaving. Cloth could now be woven in immense quantities and cheaply too.

questions.question: Apart from the English, who were the other traders who benefitted and traded the Indian textiles?
questions.explanation: Till the end of 18th-century Indian textiles continued to dominate the world trade. European trading companies-the Dutch, the French, and the English-made enormous profits out of this flourishing trade. These companies purchased cotton and silk textiles in India by importing silver.

questions.question: As European trade expanded, trading settlements with fortification were established at various ports like the one shown in the image. This major port city of the West coast is situated in the state of Kerala today. Identify the place.
questions.explanation: The Dutch settlements in Cochin came up in the seventeenth century. Dutch presence in the region started with the capture of Portuguese Quilon in 1661 and ended with the occupation of Malabar by the British in 1795.

questions.question: Name the place in Eastern Bengal, in present-day Bangladesh, famous for its textile centre in the 18th century.
questions.explanation: Dacca in Eastern Bengal (now Bangladesh) was the foremost textile centre in the eighteenth century. It was famous for its mulmul and jamdani weaving.

questions.question: An important cluster of weaving centre existed in the Coromandel coast, in the southern part of India stretching from ________.
questions.explanation: Along the Coromandal coast, we find a clusture of cotton weaving centres from Madras to Northern Andhra Pradesh. Here chintz was produced in Masaulipatnam, Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question:
A few statements pertaining to the weavers of India are given below. Identify the one that is not true with respect to the weavers.

  1. Weaving skills were not passed on from one generation to another.
  2. Weavers often belonged to the communities that specialized in weaving.
  3. The tanti weavers, julahas are some of the important weavers.
  4. Weaving provided a livelihood for many Indians.
questions.explanation: Weavers often belonged to communities that specialised in weaving. Their skills were often passed on from one generation to the next. In most communities weaving was a task done by men.

questions.question: Name this important weavers of Bengal from the image given below.
questions.explanation: The Tanti are said to have originated as the weavers, providers of cloth, back in history as far as ancient Bengal. They were known for great skill in weaving and the ability to produce both fine linens as well as more common everyday fabrics.

questions.question: Name the important weavers of Northern India from the following list.
questions.explanation: The Julaha are an important artisan community who weave cloth on handlooms. They live in Uttar Pradesh (2.9 million), Himachal Pradesh, Delhi, Haryana, Jammu and Kashmir, and Chandigarh. The term Julaha is derived from the Persian word Julah, meaning a ball of thread. The word is commonly associated with the Hindi Ujala or jali, meaning interlaced thread.

questions.question: Name the important weavers of Southern India from the following list.
questions.explanation: Sale and Kaikollar are Indian castes and their traditional occupation was weaving and are mostly found in the regions of Kerela, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu. Devang community can mostly be found in Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Orissa, Pondicherry and Tamil Nadu. Most members of this community are weavers of silk and cotton.

questions.question: Choose the first stage of production, a work done mostly by women.
questions.explanation: The first stage of production was spinning which was done by women. The charkha and takli were household spinning instruments. The thread was spun on the charkha and rolled on takli.

questions.question: Name the household spinning instruments.
questions.explanation: The thread was spun on the charkha and rolled on the takli. When the spinning was over the thread was woven into cloth by the weaver.

questions.question: Among the following terms, which one refers to a Persian term for a warehouse i.e a workshop where goods are collected before being sold:
questions.explanation: Aurangs were abolished, because of which weavers and their families were distressed for want of the means of livelihood.

questions.question: What was the specialist block printers used by weavers known as:
questions.explanation: For coloured textiles, the thread was dyed by the dyer, known as rangrez. For printed cloth, the weavers needed the help of specialist block printers known as chippigars.

questions.question: Name the important place in Western India that emerged as a weaving centre in the late 19th century.
questions.explanation: Sholapur has been a leading centre for cotton mills and power looms in Maharashtra. Sholapur had the world's second-largest and Asia's largest spinning mill.

questions.question: Name the important place in Southern India that emerged as a weaving centre in the late 19th century.
questions.explanation: Madurai was the main centre of Saurashtra enterprise in textiles. Some of the mid-nineteenth century district manuals mentioned local jari manufacture at Madurai and Arcot.

questions.question: Spot from the list given below, the important hand spun material urged by Mahatma Gandhi to use______.
questions.explanation: Mahatama Gandhi urged people to boycott imported textiles and use hand-spun and hand-woven cloth. Khadi gradually became a symbol of nationalism.

questions.question: Which one of the following was at the centre of the tricolor flag of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanation: Khadi gradually became a symbol of nationalism. The charkha came to represent India, and it was put at the centre of the tricolour flag of the Indian National Congress adopted in 1931.

questions.question: At the centre of the tricolor flag of the Indian National Congress, Charkha was adopted and it came to represent India. Which year was this?
questions.explanation: In 1931, the Congress party adopted another flag with the colours saffron, white and green, and featuring the Charkha (spinning wheel) in the centre, as their official flag.

questions.question:
A list of statements pertaining to the weavers and spinners who lost their livelihood during the British reign is given below. Pick out the statements that are true with respect to the weavers and spinners.

  1. Many weavers became agricultural labourers.
  2. A few weavers migrated to cities in search of work
  3. A few others went out of the country to work in plantations in Africa and South America
  4. Some of the workers found work in new cotton mills in Bombay, Ahmedabad, Nagpur, and Kanpur.
questions.explanation: By the beginning of the nineteenth century, English made cotton textiles successfully outsets Indian goods from their traditional markets in Africa, America, and Europe. Thousands of weavers were thrown out of employment. Bengal weavers were hit the worst.

questions.question: Which city in India can boast of the first-ever cotton mill set up as a spinning mill in 1854?
questions.explanation: From the early nineteenth century, Bombay had grown as an important port for the export of raw cotton from India to England and China. It was close to the vast black soil tract of western India where cotton was grown. When the cotton textile mills came up they could get supplies of raw material with ease.

questions.question: Give one good reason as to why Bombay had grown as an important port for the export of raw cotton.

options.html: The presence of a vast number of dying units
The vast number of women weaver workers
The presence of vast black soil tract

questions.explanation: Bombay was close to the vast black soil tract of western India where cotton was grown. When the cotton textile mills came up they could get supplies of raw material with ease.

questions.question: What is the process of obtaining a metal from rock (or soil) by heating it to a very high temperature called? Sometimes to make something new, metals are melted also.
questions.explanation: The process of applying heat to ore in order to melt out a base metal is called smelting. It is a form of extractive metallurgy. It is used to extract many metals from their ores, including silver, iron, copper, and other base metals.

questions.question: It was observed that in 1900, over 84 mills started operating in Bombay established by Parsi and Gujarathi businessmen, who made their money through trade with another nation. Which nation did they trade with successfully?
questions.explanation: The first mill in Ahemdabad was started in 1861. A year later a mill was established in Kanpur in United Provinces. By 1900, over 84 mills started operating in Bombay. Many of these were established by Parsi and Gujarati businessmen who had made their money through trade with China.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, identify the place marked A in blue circle, where was the first mill was established in 1861.
questions.explanation: First mill in Ahmedabad was floated by Shri Ranchodlal Chotalal in 1859. It was a small mill containing 2500 throstle spindles and employed 63 workers. It started its production on 30th May 1861.

questions.question: From the outline map of Indi, identify the city marked B, in red triangle, where a mill was established in the United Provinces in the year 1862.
questions.explanation: The first Cotton Textile Mill, the Elgin Mills, was started in 1862 and Muir Mills in 1882, and many others that followed in the next 40 years, such as Victoria Mills.

questions.question: Identify the sword of this great personality who ruled Mysore till 1799. He died fighting against the British with this sword in his hand. A Tiger head at the bottom, quotations from the Koran were written with gold on the steel handle of this sword.
questions.explanation: The sword had an incredibaly hard and sharp edge that could easily rip through the opponents armour. The quality of the sword came from a special type of high carbon steel called Wootz which was produced all over South India.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: From which holy book were quotations taken and written in gold in Tipu sultan's sword?
questions.explanation: The fine steel blade is inscribed and bears the verses from the Holy Quran together with the name of Tipu Sultan and his capital Srirangapattnam.

questions.question:
Listed below are a few statements about the sword of Tipu Sultan. Pick out the statements that are not true.

  1. Tipu Sultan died fighting with his sword in his hand
  2. Tipu's legendary swords are a part of the valuable collections in the museums in England
  3. The sword had an incredibly hard and sharp edge that could rip through the opponents armour
  4. The quality of the sword came from a special type of high carbon steel called Wootz
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan had lost his sword in a war with the Nairs of Travancore during the Battle of Nedumkotta(1789). All of Tipu's swords are known for their toughness and high carbon content and the project aims to unravel the metallurgical process which led to the manufacture of such high-quality swords made from Wootz steel. The sword was on display at the Wallace Collection, No. 1 Manchester Square, London.

questions.question: What was the type of carbon steel that Tipu's sword was made up of called?
questions.explanation: The sword came from a special type of high carbon steel called ''Wootz'' which was produced all over south India. Wootz steel, when made into swords, produced a very sharp edge with a flowing water pattern. This pattern came from very small carbon crystals embedded in the iron produced in many hundreds of smelting furnaces in Mysore.

questions.question: Identify this personality based on the clues given. He toured Mysore in 1800 and left an impressionable account of the technique by which Wootz steel was produced.
questions.explanation: Several European travellers including Francis Buchanan and Voysey from the 17th century onwards observed the manufacture of steel in south India by a crucible process at several locales including Mysore, Malabar, and Golconda.

questions.question: Wootz is an anglicised version of the Kannada word ukku. Telugu word hukku and Tamil and Malayalam urukku. What does this word actually mean?
questions.explanation: The word ukku is based on the meaning "melt, dissolve" other Dravidian languages have similar-sounding words for steel.

questions.question: Identify this legendary scientist and discoverer of electricity and electromagnetism who spent four years studying the properties of Indian Wootz.
questions.explanation: Micheal Farraday was an English scientist who contributed to the study of electromagnetism and electrochemistry. His main discoveries include the principles underlying electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism and electrolysis.

questions.question: What are Bellows?

options.html: A device that can pump air
A travel account of a traveller

questions.explanation: The smelting was done by men while women worked the bellows, pumping air that kept the charcoal burning. Bellows is a device or equipment that can pump air.

questions.question: With the help of the image given below, pick out the name of the community specialized in iron smelting. These workers were affected by a series of famine and had to desert their village and migrated to other places
questions.explanation: Agarias was a community who lived in Bihar and Central India. Agarias were specialised in the craft of iron smelting. People of this community told Darobji Tata about the deposits of Iron ore in Rajhara hills.

questions.question: Who travelled along with Charles Weld in Chhattisgarh in search of iron-ore deposits in 1904?
questions.explanation: Charles Weld and Dorabji Tata, the eldest son of Jamshedji Tata, were travelling in Chattisgarh in search of iron ore deposits. They had spent many months on a costly venture looking for sources of good iron ore to set up a modern iron and steel plant in India.

questions.question: Choose the name of the hills where the finest ores were discovered by the American geologist Chalres Weld and Jamsetji Tata?
questions.explanation: On exploring the hill the geologist declared that they had at last found what they had been looking for. Rajhara Hills had one of the finest ores in the world.

questions.question: Which unit was set up to produce steel in 1912?

options.html: The Tata Iron and Steel Company

questions.explanation: Tata Iron and Steel Company was founded by Jamshedji Tata and established by Dorabji Tata on 26 August 1907, as part of his father Jamshedji's Tata Groups. It began producing steel in 1912.

questions.question: Pick out the from the different names of rivers, the banks on which a factory and industrial township of TISCO was set up.
questions.explanation: A large area of forest was cleared on the banks of the river Subarnarekha to set up the factory and industrial township-Jamshedpur.

questions.question: Choose the city where the Tata Iron and Steel Company (TISCO) was established in 1912.
questions.explanation: The areas surrounding Jamshedpur are rich in minerals, including iron ore, coal, manganese bauxite, and limestone. The largest factory is that of Tata Iron and steel industry is situated almost at the centre of the city.

questions.question: Which state-owned enterprise turned to TISCO when the imports of British steel into India declined?
questions.explanation: British experts in the Indian Railways were unwilling to believe that good quality steel could be produced in India. In 1914 the First World War broke out. Steel produced in Britain now had to meet the demands of the war in Europe. Imports of British steel into India declined dramatically and the Indian Railways turned to TISCO.

questions.question: Complete the following. By 1919, the colonial government bought ________ percent of steel manufactured by TISCO.
questions.explanation: As the First World war dragged on for several years, TISCO had to produce shells and carriage wheels for the war. By 1919 the colonial government was buying 90% of the steel manufactured by TISCO.

questions.question: What would you call a person who knows several languages?
questions.explanation: For instance, William Jones who had studied Greek and Latin at Oxford and knew French, English, Arabic, and Persian was a linguist.

questions.question: Identify this linguist who arrived in Calcutta in 1783 as a junior judge at the Supreme Court.
questions.explanation: The person in the pic is seen learning Persian and it was William Jones who learnt Persian and also arrived in Calcutta in 1783.

questions.question: From the image given below, name this English scholar who learnt Sanskrit and ancient sacred writings of Hinduism along with William Jones.
questions.explanation: Englishmen like Henry Thomas Colebrooke and Nathaniel Halhed shared the interests of William Jones, discovered the ancient Indian heritage,mastered Indian languages and translated Sanskrit and Persian works into English.

questions.question: Three Englishmen were busy discovering the ancient Indian heritage and mastering Indian languages. One was Willam Jones and another Henry Thomas Colebrooke. Name the third person.
questions.explanation: The three Englishmen William Jones, Henry Thomas Colebrooke, and Nathaniel Halhed together set up the Asiatic Society of Bengal and also started a journal called Asiatic Researches which was based on their researches on Indian heritage.

questions.question: Who started the journal Asiatick Researches?
questions.explanation: Many British officials living in Calcutta at that time like Henry Thomas Colebrooke and Nathanil Halhed shared the interests of William Jones. So together with them William Jones set up the Asiatic Society of Bengal and started the journal.

questions.question: What does the Arabic word Madrasa refer to?

options.html: A place of learning, a school or college

questions.explanation: Madrasa was set up in Calcutta in 1781 to promote the study of Arabic, Persian, and Islamic law.

questions.question: Where in India a Madrasa was set up in 1781 to promote the study of Arabic, Persian, and Islamic Law?
questions.explanation: In order to win a place in the hearts of the “natives” British took steps to make provisions to teach the Indians what they valued and treasured, not subjects that were alien to them. A madrasa was set up in Calcutta in 1781 to promote the study of Arabic, Persian and Islamic law; and the Hindu College was established in Benaras in 1791 to encourage the study of ancient Sanskrit texts.

questions.question: Name the important scholar who studied Sanskrit and found it to be the most scientific language of the world. He also translated the important works Shakuntala and Manusmriti into English.
questions.explanation: William Jones was a linguist and studied the Sanskrit language from pandits. He along with Colebrooke went about discovering ancient texts, understanding their meaning, translating them, and making their findings known to others.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, identify the place marked A in black circle. Situated on the banks of Ganges, in Uttar Pradesh also known as Varanasi or Kasi where the Hindu College was established in 1791.
questions.explanation: The Hindu College was established to encourage the study of ancient Sanskrit texts. Company officials thought setting up institutions would be useful for the administration of the country.

questions.question: From the following which one would best describe an Orientalist?

options.html: Those who study about the birds of Asia
Those who study the animal behavior of Asia
Those who study about the transport and communication of Asia
Those with a scholarly knowledge of culture and language of Asia

questions.explanation: Warren Hastings who was a famous Orientalist took the initiative to set up the Calcutta madrasa and believed that the ancient customs of the country and Oriental learning ought to be the basis of British rule in India.

questions.question: A person who can read, write, and teach Persian is called a ________.
questions.explanation: The majestic figure of Hastings, who was an enthusiastic supporter of Orientalists is placed between the standing pandit on one side and seated munshi on the other side in Victoria Memorial in Calcutta. The figure portrays Hastings' interest in vernacular languages and the two scholars of Sanskrit and Persian languages who taught him the two languages.

questions.question: In countries like India, the British used this term to mark the difference between the local languages and everyday use and English. This term is generally used to refer to a local language or dialect as distinct from what is seen as the standard language.
questions.explanation: British considered English as the language of the imperial masters and said that knowledge of the East was full of errors and unscientific thought. So for local languages, they used the term vernacular.

questions.question: Warren Hastings took the initiative to set up a Madrasa at one of the important cities of India and believed that the ancient customs of the country and Oriental learning ought to be the basis of British rule in India. Which city is being referred to here?
questions.explanation: Warren Hastings was a supporter of the Orientalists, which we can clearly see in the image at Victoria Memorial in Calcutta. Here Warren Hastings figure is placed between the standing figure of a pandit and a seated munshi.

questions.question: The image given below is that of a famous Governor General in the centre, and a Pandit on one side and a Munshi on another side. This monument is now in Victoria Memorial, Calcutta. Guess correctly the name of this Governor General.
questions.explanation: Warren Hastings was a supporter of Orientalists, so his image was placed between a pandit and a munshi because he needed Indian scholars to teach vernacular languages.

questions.question:
Some comments about the Orientalist Vision of Learning by the British are given below. Select the one that is not applicable to this particular style of learning.

  1. British said that knowledge of the east was full of errors and unscientific thoughts.
  2. British observed that Western literature was non-serious and light-hearted.
  3. British argued that it is not advisable to encourage the study of Arabic and Sanskrit language and literature.
  4. British argued that the aim of education ought to be to teach what was useful and practical.
questions.explanation: British officials said that Eastern literature was non-serious and light-hearted.

tests.instructionHtml:

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name this Scottish historian who attacked the Orientalists.
questions.explanation: James Mill believed, the aim of education to be teaching what was useful and practical and did not agree with the views of Orientalists.

questions.question:
Identify this famous historian from the following clues :
He was the most outspoken and influential critic who attacked the Orientalists
He saw India as an uncivilised country that needed to be civilized
He felt that British government in India should stop wasting public money in promoting Oriental learning for it was of no practical use.
questions.explanation: Thomas Babington Macaulay was the most outspoken and influential critics of the time and he declared that a single shelf of a good European library was worth the whole native literature of India and Arabia.

questions.question: A single shelf of a good European library was worth the whole native literature of India and Arabia. Who said these words?
questions.explanation: Thomas Babington Macaulay was the most outspoken and influential critics of Orientalists of the time so only he could say such words.

questions.question: Lord Macaulay emphasized the need to teach this language. Which language did he insist to teach?
questions.explanation: Lord Macaulay felt that knowledge of English would allow Indians to read some of the finest literature the world had produced; it would make them aware of the developments in Western science and philosophy.

questions.question: Which year was the English education Act introduced in India?
questions.explanation: Following the minutes by T.B.Macaulay on 2nd February 1835, the English Education Act of 1835 was introduced.

questions.question: The temples of darkness that were falling of themselves into decay. Who said these words about Calcutta, Madras, and Benares Sanskrit College?
questions.explanation: To stop the promotion of Oriental institutions like Calcutta Madrasa and Benaras Sanskrit College, Lord Macaulay said these words.

questions.question: Which year did the Court of Directors of the East India Company in London send an educational dispatch to the Governor-General in India?
questions.explanation: Issued by Charles Wood, the President of the board of control of the Company, the educational policy that was to be followed in India was sent to the Governor-General in India James Broun Ramsay.

questions.question: Name the President of the Board of Control in England who sent a dispatch.
questions.explanation: Sir Charles Wood had an important effect on spreading education in India. In 1854 he sent a dispatch to Lord Dalhousie, the then Governor-General of India, according to which education departments were established in every province.

questions.question:
The main points covered in the Woods dispatch are given below. Pick out any statement if any not true with respect to the Woods despatch

  1. Provision was made for a systematic method of education from the primary level to the university level.
  2. In Bombay, Madras, and Calcutta, universities were opened
  3. Grants in- aid were to be given to private schools affiliated to the government.
  4. Indians were not allowed to be taught in their mother tongue.
questions.explanation: According to the despatch, Indian natives should be given training in their mother tongue also.

questions.question:
How did European learning improve the moral character of Indians?

  1. It would make them truthful and honest.
  2. It would expand trade and commerce.
  3. It would make the Indians realize the importance of developing resources of the country.
  4. It would make them change their tastes and desires and create a demand for British goods.

options.html: It would make them change their tastes and desires and create a demand for British goods
It would make the Indians realize the importance of developing resources of the country
It would expand the trade and commerce
It would make them truthful and honest

questions.explanation: Wood's Despatch argued that European learning would improve the moral character of Indians. It would make them truthful and honest, and thus supply the Company with civil servants who could be trusted and depended upon.

questions.question: The introduction of ________ brought with it ideas of democracy, liberty, fraternity among the minds of Indians.
questions.explanation: Macaulay believed teaching English could be a way of civilizing people, changing their taste, values, and culture.

questions.question: This picture is of a university that was established as per recommendations of the Woods Dispatch along the lines of London universities. Identify the place where this famous university stands today in the state of Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: According to the recommendations made in Wood's Despatch, universities on the model of London University be established in big cities such as Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras. This is at Bombay because of the three cities Bombay is in Maharashtra.

questions.question: Name the college established on the banks of the river Hoogly near Calcutta by William Carrey.
questions.explanation: In 1818 William Carey who was a missionary founded Serampore College to train indeginous ministers for the growing church and to provide education in arts and sciences to anyone regardless of caste or country.

questions.question: Name the person who was a part of the Scottish missionary who helped to establish the Serampore Mission.
questions.explanation: William Carey was a Christian missionary to India but was forced to leave Calcutta, so he joined the Baptist Missionaries in the Danish colony Fredericksnagar in India(Serampore). Here he opened first theological university offering divinity degrees.

questions.question:
The following image is a pictorial representation of a Dutch painter who came to India in the late 18th century depicting the everyday life of a village pathshala.

Name this Dutch painter
questions.explanation: Francois Solvyn came to India in the late eighteenth century and tried to depict the everyday life of people in his paintings.
