questions.question: Who as a part of the Scottish missionary toured the districts of Bengal and Bihar in the 1830s?
questions.explanation: William Adam had been asked by the Company to report the progress of education in vernacular schools, so he toured the districts of Bengal and Bihar.

questions.question:
A few important aspects of education through a pathshala are given below. Pick out the one that is not true with respect to a pathshala.

  1. Teaching was oral and the guru taught only the children of rich families.
  2. There was no fixed fee, no printed books, no benches, etc.
  3. Classes were held under trees or even at gurus home.
  4. Mostly the shishyas stayed in the guru's house and studied and had flexible timings according to the harvest time.
questions.explanation: Classes were not held during harvest time when rural children often worked in the fields which means that children coming from poor families also studied in the pathshalas.

questions.question: Which year did the East India Company decide to improve the system of vernacular education?
questions.explanation: Up to the mid-nineteenth century, the Company was concerned primarily with higher education, so it allowed the local pathshalas to function but in 1854 it decided to improve the system.

questions.question:
What was the important measure taken by the East India Company to improve vernacular education?

  1. They appointed a number of government pandits in charge of schools and asked them to inspect the schools.
  2. They allowed only higher education neglecting primary education.
  3. They introduced high fees and introduced grants.
  4. They closed down many local schools.
questions.explanation: The task of the pandit was to visit the pathshalas and try and improve the standard of teaching. Each guru was asked to submit periodic reports and take classes according to the timetable.

questions.question: Name the poet who reacted against the introduction of Western education in India?
questions.explanation: Impressed with the developments in Europe, some Indians felt that the Western education would help modernise India, of which Mahatma Gandhi and Rabindranath Tagore were prominent.

questions.question: Who was of the opinion that Colonial education created a sense of inferiority in the minds of Indians?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi felt that colonial education made Indians see Western civilisation as superior, and destroyed the pride they had in their own culture.

questions.question: Compelete the following words of Mahatma Gandhiji. Education in English ________ Indians.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi felt education in English distanced Indians from their own social surroundings and made them strangers in their own lands.

questions.question:
Some of the important points related to the ill effects of Western education in India put forward by Mahatma Gandhi are given below. Pick out if any statement(s) that are not true with respect to the views of Gandhiji

  1. Gandhiji felt there was poison in this education and it was sinful.
  2. Gandhiji wanted an education that could help Indians recover their sense of dignity and self-respect.
  3. Gandhiji urged the students to leave educational institutions in order to show to the British that Indians were no longer willing to be enslaved.
  4. Gandhiji strongly felt that Indian languages ought to be the medium of teaching.
  5. Gandhiji felt education in English crippled Indians, distanced them from their own surroundings.
questions.explanation: According to Mahatma Gandhi, Western education focussed on reading and writing rather than oral knowledge. It valued textbooks rather than lived experience and practical knowledge.

questions.question: The image given below depicts Mahatma Gandhi Rabindranath Tagore and a group of girls at Santiniketan in 1940. Identify the person sitting next to Mahatma Gandhi
questions.explanation: Kasturba Gandhi was a political activist and participated in the Indian independence movement with her husband.

questions.question: Complete the following sentence. Mahatma Gandhi felt that the ________ ought to be the medium of teaching.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi strongly felt education in English crippled Indians, distanced them from their own social surroundings, and made them strangers in their own lands. Speaking a foreign tongue, despising local culture, the English educated did not know how to relate to the masses.

questions.question: Mahatma Gandhi believed that people had to work with their hands, learn a ________ and know how different things operated. This would develop their mind and their capacity to understand.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi argued literacy-or simply learning to read and write-by itself did not count as education.

questions.question: By education, I mean all-round drawing out of the best in child and man-body, mind and spirit. Literacy is neither the end of education nor even the beginning. Whose words are these?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi had written literacy is only one of the means whereby man and women can be educated. Literacy in itself, not education.

questions.question: This image is the prayer hall of an important institution started by Rabindranatha Tagore in 1901. Name the institution.
questions.explanation: Rabindranath Tagore set up Santiniketan in 1901 and saw it as an 'abode of peace'. Here children would cultivate their natural creativity living in harmony with nature.

questions.question: This is the picture of a Class in progess in Santhiniketan in the 1930s. Name the person who started this famous institution in 1901.
questions.explanation: Rabindranath Tagore was of the view that creative learning could be encouraged only within a natural envioronment, so he chose to set up his school 100kms away from Calcutta, in a rural setting.

questions.question:
The experiences of a famous personality are given below. Identify this famous personality based on the clues given :

  1. He hated going to school
  2. The school appeared like a prison, for he could never do what he felt like doing
  3. When the other children listened to the teacher, his mind would wander away.
  4. On growing up, he wanted to set up a school where the child was happy, free and creative - a school of self-learning
questions.explanation: Rabindranath Tagore found school suffocating and oppressive. So he wanted to set up a school where the child was able to explore his own thoughts and desires, for which he set up Santiniketan.

tests.name: Civilising the Native Educating the Nation Test 04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History Civilising the Native Educating the Nation CBSE Online Test 04 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

questions.question:
Some important viewpoints of Gandhiji's and Rabindranath Tagore's thought of education are given below. State the statements that are not true with respect to their thought on education.

  1. Gandhiji believed that creative learning could be encouraged only within a natural environment.
  2. Gandhiji was highly critical of Western civilisation and its worship of machines and technology.
  3. Tagore wanted to combine elements of modern Western civilization with what he saw as the best within the Indian tradition.
  4. Tagore emphasized the need to teach Science and technology at Santiniketan along with art, music, and dance in a natural environment.
questions.explanation: Tagore believed that creative learning could be encouraged only within a natural environment. Gandhiji strongly felt that Indian languages ought to be the medium of teaching.

questions.question: Which of the following university was established by Maharaja Sayajirao III in the state of Gujarat?
questions.explanation: Maharaja Sayajirao III of Baroda took a keen interest in the field of education. He introduced a number of educational and social reforms. He set up many schools and also took steps to establish a university in Baroda and provided personal and state funds. The university was completed by his grandson.

questions.question: Maharaja Sayajirao University is well known for its fine arts, engineering, medicine, etc. Name an important student of this institution who stood for the backward classes and responsible for drafting the Indian constitution.
questions.explanation: When Maharaja Sayaji Rao announced a scholarship for higher studies in abroad to any promising untouchable student, he was sent by his father to Maharaja Sayaji Rao. Maharaja was impressed by Ambedkar and was provided a scholarship of 25 dollars per month.

questions.question: Name the important university in Uttar Pradesh started by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, a visionary among the Muslims in the 19th century.
questions.explanation: The Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College, founded by Sayyed Ahmad Khanin 1875 at Aligarh, later became the Aligarh Muslim University. The institution offered modern education, including Western science, to Muslims.

questions.question: Name the personality who started the Aligarh Muslim University.
questions.explanation: Sayed Ahmed Bin Mushtaq Khan was an Indian Muslim pragmatist, Islamic reformist, and philosopher of nineteenth-century British India.

questions.question: Name the person who along with Sir Syed Ahmed Khan help to establish the Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College in Aligarh in 1875.
questions.explanation:
The Aligarh Muslim University grew out of the dreams and efforts of Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, a visionary among the Muslims of the 19th century. After the Revolt of 1857, the British began their policy of ‘divide and rule’ and began sidelining the Muslims. Sir Syed felt a strong desire to see Muslims also join the mainstream of progress. He felt that Muslims would have no future in India if they were not exposed to Western education and failed to learn English. He, along with Hidayatullah Khan started the Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College in Aligarh in 1875.
The aim of the college was to combine Western thinking with Islamic values. In 1920, it was made into the Aligarh Muslim University, which taught engineering, humanities, and sciences. AMU has produced a generation of Muslim and Hindu men and women, many of whom led India in its fight for freedom.

questions.question: Which among the following was later renamed and the Aligarh Muslim University?
questions.explanation: In 1875 Sir Sayed Ahmad Khan founded Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College at Aligarh, the first Muslim university in South Asia. This MAO College later came to be known as Aligarh Muslim University.

questions.question:
A list of reasons as to why Sir Syed Ahmed Kahn started the Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College are given below. Which among the following are not valid reasons?

  1. His aim was to combine Western thinking with Islamic values.
  2. He wanted to propagate Islam.
  3. He wanted Urdu to be the main language of India.
  4. He wanted the Muslims to be given importance as he felt they were sidelined.
questions.explanation: Mohammedan Anglo-Oriental College was the part of the movement of Muslim awakening.

questions.question: Name the first woman chancellor of the Aligarh Muslim University.
questions.explanation: Sultan Jahan Begum was the first female ruler who vehemently worked for the promotion of education amongst the Muslim community and established a Trust with an endowment of Four Lakh rupees for the educational advancement of females.

questions.question: Name the important system of education started in 1905 on a national scale after discussions with eminent personalities like Raja Ram Mohan Roy and Rabindranath Tagore.
questions.explanation: Raja RamMohan Roy is regarded as the "Father of Bengal renaissance". The Bengal renaissance started with Raja Ram Mohan Roy and ended with Rabindranath Tagore. It was their effort that the new system of education started.

questions.question: Who is the brain behind the important college at Banaras which later grew into a university?
questions.explanation: Pandit Madan Mohan Malviya is remembered as the founder of Banaras Hindu University at Varanasi in 1916. He was the Vice-Chancellor of Banaras Hindu University from 1919 to 1938.

questions.question: The image given is that of an important educationist who founded Gurukul at Haridwar to revive the ancient gurukul system. He was assassinated by a Muslim fanatic in 1926.
questions.explanation: Swami Shraddhananda was an Indian educationalist and an Arya Samaj missionary who propogated the teachings of Swami Dayanand Saraswati. In 1902 he established Gurukul in Kangri near Haridwar which is now recognized as Gurukul Kangri University.

questions.question: What was the new service started to recruit teachers for educational institutions all over the country.
questions.explanation: Indian Education Service was formed based on the recommendations of the 1886 Public Service Commission as part of Superior Education Service which comprised also the Provincial Education Service.

questions.question: In 1920, a team of scientists like P C Roy, J C Bose, C V Raman, Satyan Bose, Birbal Sahni, etc decided to set up an important Science Centre. Name this.
questions.explanation: Indian Science Congress Association is a premier scientific organisation of India with headquarters at Kolkata, West Bengal. The association started in the year 1914 in Kolkata and meets annually in the first week of January.

questions.question: Name the person who put forward a demand for free and compulsory education before the British.
questions.explanation: As a nationalist leader Gokhle visualised the importance of primary education in socio-political awakening in India. As a member of the Legislative Council, he put forward a proposal for introducing compulsory education in 1910.

tests.name: Women Caste and Reform Test 01
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History Women Caste and Reform CBSE Online Test 01 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Painted by a European artist who came to India, this was one of the many pictures of a religious practice, a social evil that existed in ancient India. Identify the name of the practice
questions.explanation: In many parts of the country widows were praised if they chose death by burning themselves on the funeral pyre of their husbands. Women who died in this manner whether willingly or otherwise, were called sati meaning virtuous women. The practice of sati was seen as evidence of the barbarism of the East.

questions.question: Name the uppermost caste in the social ladder that existed in ancient India.
questions.explanation: In most regions, people were divided along the lines of caste. Brahmins and Kshatriyas considered themselves as upper castes of which Brahmins were the uppermost.

questions.question: Among the following, which class belonged to the traders and money lenders
questions.explanation: Traders and money lenders often reffered to as Vaishayas were placed after Brahmins and Kshatriyas in the caste ladder.

questions.question: Name the class that belonged to the lowermost strata in the social ladder of ancient India.
questions.explanation: Peasants and artisans such as weavers and potters were referred to as Shudras and were the lowest rung class. They laboured to keep the villages and cities cleaner worked at jobs that upper caste considered "polluting" that is it could lead to the loss of caste status.

questions.question:
With respect to ancient India, a list of statements related to the untouchables is given below. Point out the one that is not true.

  1. They were not allowed to enter temples.
  2. They were not allowed to draw water from the wells used by the upper castes.
  3. They were not allowed to bathe in ponds where the upper caste bathed.
  4. They were not considered as inferior human beings.
questions.explanation: Shudras were the lowest rung and were those who laboured to keep cities and villages clean or worked at jobs that upper caste considered "polluting", that is it could lead to the loss of caste status.

questions.question: An image of a popular reformer who founded a reform association Brahmo Samaj is given below. Name this personality.
questions.explanation: Raja Rammohan Roy founded a reform association known as the Brahmo Sabha which later came to be known as the Brahmo Samaj. People such as Rammohan Roy are described as reformers because they felt that changes are necessary in the society.

questions.question: What does monotheism mean?
questions.explanation: Monotheism is the belief in the existence of one God that created the world, is all-powerful, and intervenes in the world.

questions.question: Few important points with respect to Raja Ram Mohan Roy are given below. Select the one that is not true.

options.html: Rabindranath Tagore called him as the Father of Indian Renaissance
Rajaram Mohan Roy encouraged the study of local languages and wanted to abolish Western education
He tried to show through his writings that the practice of widow burning had no sanction in ancient texts
Through Brahmo Samaj he attempted to reform Hindu society

questions.explanation: Rammohan Roy was keen to spread the knowledge of Western Education in the country and bring about greater freedom and equality for women.

questions.question: Who has been referred to as the Prophet of Indian Nationalism?
questions.explanation: Raja Rammohan Roy is known as the Father of Indian Nationalism, Father of Indian Renaissance, and Prophet of Indian Nationalism. He founded Brahmo Samaj in 1828. He established schools to impart English education to the Indians. He started the Hindu College which emerged as the Presidency college.

questions.question: Under which Governor-General did Raja Ram Mohan Roy initiate to ban Sati?
questions.explanation: Under sustained campaigning against Sati by Christian Missionaries such as William Carrey and Brahmin Hindu reformers such as Ram Mohan Roy, the provincial government banned Sati in 1829. The ban of Sati was effected by Lord William Bentick the then Governor-General of India.

questions.question: His support for women's upliftment made him pass the Widow Remarriage Act of 1856. Who is being referred to here?
questions.explanation: Introduced the practice of widow remarriages to the mainstream Hindu society. He was a social reformer and worked against polygamy and wanted to change the orthodox society from within.

questions.question: Name the follower of Raja Ram Mohan Roy who opposed the purdah system and supported intercaste marriages.
questions.explanation: Keshab Chandra Sen was an Indian Bengali Hindu philosopher and a social reformer who attempted to incorporate Christian Theology within the framework of Hindu thought. He joined Brahmo Samaj in1856.

questions.question: In the Telugu speaking areas of the Madras Presidency, an association was formed for widow remarriage. Name the personality behind this, who is also called the Vidyasagar of the south.
questions.explanation: Rao Bahadur Kandukuri Veersalingam Pantulu was a social reformer writer of Andhra Pradesh. He is the father of the Renaissance movement in Telegu. He is one of the social reformers who encouraged women's education, remarriage of widows which was not supported by the society at his time and also fought against the dowry system.

questions.question: Who founded Arya Samaj, an organization that attempted to reform Hindusims?
questions.explanation: Swami Dayanand Saraswati founded Arya Samaj in 1875. He had a great sense of pride for Indian culture and philosophy. He condemned idol worship, superstitious practices, and the superiority of Brahmin priests.

questions.question: Name the person who founded the Theosophical Society in India.
questions.explanation: The Theosophical Society was founded in the U.S. in 1875 by Madame H.P. Blavatsky and Colonel H.S.Olcott. Theosophy means Brahmavidya in Sanskrit. The Theosophical movement gained momentum in India with the arrival of Annie Besant in India. Its international headquarters are at Adyar in Chennai.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Identify the personality based on the clues:

  1. Born in a poor Brahmin family, he was a great Sanskrit scholar who became the Principal of a Sanskrit College
  2. He spent his life for the upliftment of women and girls and was instrumental in the passing of Widow Remarriage Act 1856
  3. He opposed the monopoly of the Brahmans on the study of Sanskrit
questions.explanation: Ishwar Chandra received the tittle Vidyasagar from Sanskrit College Calcutta, due to his excellent performance in Sanskrit and philosophy. Noted Bengali mathematician Anil Kumar Gain founded Vidya Sagar University named in his honour.

questions.question: Considered as the Grand Old Man of India, he is one of the co-founders of the Indian National Congress, he also fought for the legal rights of women
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naroji was a Parsi intellectual, educator, cotton trader and early Indian political and social reformer. His book Poverty and Un-Brititish rule in India brought attention to the draining of India's wealth into Britain.

questions.question: Name the social reformer who worked for the upliftment of women in Maharashtra.
questions.explanation: Jyothirao Phule was an Indian social activist, thinker,anti-caste social reformer, and a writer from Maharashtra. He is regarded as an important social reform figure in Maharashtra. He is most known for his efforts to educate women and lower caste people.

questions.question: Followers of Brahmo Samaj started another one in Bombay in 1867. Name this Samaj that fought against social customs like child marriage and remarriage for widows.
questions.explanation: Prarthana Samaj started as a movement for religious and social reform in Maharashtra and can be seen much more like Brahmo Samaj. This comprehensive reform movement has led many impressive projects such as improvement of a lot of women and depressed classes, an end to the caste system, the abolition of child marriages and infanticide, educational opportunities for women, and widow remarriage.

questions.question: Name the personality, from the list given below, who secretly learned to read and write in the flickering light of candles at night.
questions.explanation: Rashsundari hid a page out of her son's book and a sheet of paper and taught herself to read and write after the household slept, scratching letters on the soot-covered walls of kitchen.

questions.question: In which language women of the aristocratic Muslim households of North India learned to read and write the Koran?
questions.explanation: In aristocratic Muslim families in North India women learnt to read the Quran in Arabic. They were taught by women who came home to teach.

questions.question: Who was the important reformer who reinterpreted verses from the Koran to argue for women's education?
questions.explanation: Sayed Mumtaz Ali, a Muslim reformer from Lahore, claimed that Muslim women had more rights under Islam than women from other religious communities. He saw a need of both men and women to be aware of these rights, to affect a transformation in Muslim society.

questions.question: In which century did the first Urdu novels begin to be written?
questions.explanation: Mirat-al-Urus (The Bride's Mirror,1868-1869) by Deputy Nazeer Ahmad is regarded as the first novel in Urdu. Within twenty years of publication 100,000 copies had been printed and it has never been out of print in Urdu.

questions.question: Who from Bhopal, from the early 20th century, played a notable role in promoting education among women and founded a primary school for girls?
questions.explanation: Muslim women like Begums of Bhopal played a notable role in promoting education among women. They founded a primary school for girls at Aligarh. Another woman, Begum Rokeyya Sakhawat Hossain started schools for Muslim girls in Patna and Calcutta.

questions.question: Where did the first primary school for girls start at?
questions.explanation: On 1st Jan. 1848, India’s first school for girls was started at Bhide Wada in Pune by Mahatma Jotiba Phule and Savitribai Phule.

questions.question: Name the person who started schools for Muslim girls in Patna and Calcutta and encouraged education for Muslim girls.
questions.explanation: Begum Rokaiya suggested that the education of women is the foremost requisite of women's liberation: Hence, she established the first school aimed primarily at Bengali Muslim girls at Calcutta.

questions.question: Name the important women personality who wrote and published a book Stripurushtulna, criticising the social differences between men and women.
questions.explanation: Tarabai Shinde was a feminist activist who protested patriarchy and caste in the 19th century in India. She is known for her work Stripurushtulna originally published in Marathi in 1882.

questions.question: Identify this personality who was a great scholar in Sanskrit. She also founded a widow home at Poona to provide shelter to widows who had been treated badly by their husband's relatives.
questions.explanation: Pandita Ramabai felt that Hinduism was oppressive towards women and wrote a book about the miserable lives of upper-caste Hindu women. The widows' home founded by her was a place where women were trained so that they could support themselves economically.

questions.question: Who translated an old Buddhist text that was critical of caste?
questions.explanation: Raja Rammohan Roy learned the Buddhist and Jain religious works to better argue the case for Christianity in the cultural context.

questions.question: Which of the following adhered to the tradition of Bhakti that believed in the spiritual equality of all castes?
questions.explanation: The adherents of Prarthna Samaj were devoted theists, they did not disregard Vedas as divine or infallible. Their ideas traced back to devotional poems of the Vitthals as part of the Bhakti Devotional Movements of the thirteenth century in Maharashtra.

questions.question: Which one among the following was founded in 1840 to work for the abolition of caste?
questions.explanation: Paramhans Mandali was a secret socio-religious group in Bombay. It was started by Mehtaji Durgaram, Dadoba Pandurang, and a group of his friends. They were primarily interested in breaking caste rules.

questions.question: From the following list of options, during the course of the 19th century who started setting up schools for tribal groups and lower caste children so that these children were equipped with some resources to make their way into a changing world.
questions.explanation: The standards of living of the tribals were raised and they were able to carve out living with the help of missionaries. Educational reforms imbibed in the Missionaries a unifying spirit in the Indians and they came together to fight for the cause as a united nation.

questions.question: Name this coolie ship that carried many Indian labourers to Mauritius where they did a variety of forms of hard labour.
questions.explanation: The poor people from low castes went to work in plantations in Assam, Mauritus, Trinidad and Indonesia. They saw it as an opportunity to get away from the oppressive hold that upper caste land owners exercised over their lives and the daily humiliation they suffered. The best ship engaged in the transportation of indentured labourers to Mauritus were of John Allen of London who had ships of between 600 to 1000 tons.

questions.question: Name this important untouchable caste of Andhra Pradesh. These experts made shoes.
questions.explanation: Madigas were experts at cleaning hides, tanning them for use and sewing sandals. Caste prejudice against leather work meant that only the traditional leather workers and shoemakers were ready to supply army shoes.

questions.question: Who was Ghasidas?

options.html: Leader of Madigas, supposedly an untouchable caste
Leader of the Dublas, supposedly a lower caste tribal group
The leader who revolted when labourers were carried to Mauritius

questions.explanation: Guru Ghasidas was the founder of Satnami community in the state of Chattisgarh. Ghasidas preached Satnam particularly for the people of Chattisgarh.

questions.question: Name the personality who worked among the low castes Chandala cultivators in Eastern Bengal.
questions.explanation: Haridas Thakur Matua sect worked in Eastern Bengal. He worked among low caste Chandala cultivators. They tried to create self-esteem amongst lower castes.

questions.question: Name this great social leader from Kerala who proclaimed the ideas of unity of all people within one sect, a single caste and one guru.
questions.explanation: A guru from Ezhava caste, Shri Narayana Guru, proclaimed the ideals of unity for his people. He argued against treating people unequallyon the basis of caste differences. According to him all human kind belonged to the same caste.

questions.question:
Some important points about the great social reformers who fought for equality and justice during the second half of the 19th century are given below. Choose the correct option:

  1. All these reformers came from the so-called low caste.
  2. They tried to change the habits and practices among low castes which provoked upper castes contempt.
  3. They tried to create a sense of self-esteem amongst the lower castes.
questions.explanation: These assertions did not go unchallenged. The forceful speeches, writings, and movements of lower caste leaders did lead to rethinking and some self-criticism among upper-caste nationalist leaders.

questions.question: Name the author who penned the book Gulamgiri, dedicated to all those Americans who had fought to free slaves.
questions.explanation: Jyotirao Phule dedicated his book Gulamgiri to all those Americans who had fought to free slaves, thus establishing a link between the conditions of the "lower" castes in India and black slaves in America.

questions.question: Name this prominent Dalit leader from Western India who fought for caste reforms in the 20th century.
questions.explanation: Ambedkar was born into a Mahar family. He led three movements for temple entry between 1927 and 1935. His aim was to make everyone see the power of caste prejudices within society.

questions.question: From the image given below identify this great personality from the Southern part of India who fought for caste reforms.
questions.explanation: E.V.Ramaswamy Naicker, or Periyar, as he was called, came from a middle-class family. He founded the Self Respect Movement. He argued that untouchables were the true upholders of an original Tamil and Dravidian culture which had been subjugated by Brahmins. Untouchables had to free themselves, therefore from all religions in order to achieve social equality.

questions.question:
Why did Dr. B R Ambedkar lead three temple entry movements between 1927 and 1935?

  1. Dr. B R Ambedkar felt humiliated when he led the first movement.
  2. Dr. Ambedkar himself was from a Dalit.
  3. Dr. B R Ambedkar wanted to make everyone see the power of caste prejudices within society.
  4. Dr. Ambedkar wanted to study about these movements.
questions.explanation: As a child, he experienced what caste prejudice meant in everyday life. In school, he was forced to sit outside the classroom on the ground and was not allowed to drink water from taps that upper caste children used. Brahmin priests were outraged when Dalits used water from the temple tank. He wanted everyone to see these prejudices.

questions.question: Inspired by the Brahmo Samaj, in Madras in 1864 another movement worked to abolish caste distinction and promote widow remarriage and women's education. Name this.
questions.explanation: Members of Veda Samaj believed in one God. They condemned the superstitions and rituals of orthodox Hinduism.

questions.question: A teacher at the Hindu College, Calcutta in the 1820s promoted radical ideas and encouraged his pupils to question all authority. Name the brain behind this Young Bengal Movement.
questions.explanation: Referred to as the Young Bengal Movement, Henry Louis Vivian's students attacked tradition and custom, demanded education for women, and campaigned for the freedom of thought and expression.

questions.question: Where did the reform organization of the Sikhs form first in 1873?
questions.explanation: Reform organisations of the Sikhs, the first Singh Sabhas were formed at Amritsar in 1873 and at Lahore in 1879. The Sabhas sought to rid Sikhism of superstitions, caste distinctions and practice seen by them as non-Sikh.

questions.question: The European artists brought with them new styles and conventions of painting. Which of the following options best describe the term convention?
questions.explanation: A stream of European artists came to India along with British traders and rulers. The artists began producing pictures which became widely popular in Europe and helped shape Western perceptions of India.

questions.question: They began producing pictures which became widely popular in Europe and helped shape Western perceptions of India. Who is being referred to here as they?
questions.explanation: From the eighteenth century, a stream of European artists came to India along with British traders and rulers. These artists brought with them a new style and new conventions of painting.

questions.question: European artists brought with them the idea of realism. From the list of options which one best describes the term realism.

options.html: The artwork had to be done by a real professional artist
The artwork had to project the power of the British
The artwork had to be an engraved one
The artwork was expected to look real and lifelike

questions.explanation: European artists also brought with them the technique of oil painting. Oil painting enabled artists to produce images that looked real.

questions.question: This image is an artist's oil painting on the ruins on the banks of the Ganges at Ghazipur. This painting depicts the ruins of local buildings that were once grand showing reminders of past glory. Name the famous British artist whose nephew was also equally popular, who painted this.
questions.explanation: Thomas Daniel was an English landscape painter. He spent several years in India, accompanied by his nephew William who was also an artist and published several series of aquatints of the country.

questions.question: Which year did the painters Thomas Daniell and his nephew William Daniell come to India?
questions.explanation: Thomas Daniell and his nephew William Daniellcame to India in 1785, and stayed for seven years, journeying from Calcutta to northern and southern India. They produced some of the most evocative picturesque landscapes of Britains' newly conquered territories in India.

questions.question: This image is a painting by Thomas and William Daniell bringing modern civilization to India- the making of a new city is powerfully emphasized in this pictures. Identify the city
questions.explanation: In the drawings by Daniels, we can see the making of new Calcutta, with wide avenues, majestic European-style buildings, and new modes of transport.

questions.question: Which one the following definition best explain a Portrait?

options.html: A picture of a person in which the face and its expression is prominent
A picture of a scenic landscape

questions.explanation: A tradition of art that became immensely popular in colonial India was portrait painting. The rich and the powerful, both British and Indian, wanted to see themselves on canvas. Colonial portraits were life-size images that looked lifelike and real.

questions.question: This new style of Portraiture also served as an ideal means of displaying the lavish lifestyles, wealth, and status that the empire generated. Explain the term Portraiture.

options.html: The art of combining Literature and Photography

questions.explanation: Portraitures were not merely an extension of European styles and conventions into a new environment. The subjects of paintings were often placed in stylised architectural settings and others appeared against identifiable  Indian scenes.

questions.question: A few facts with respect to the portrait painting are given below. Choose the one that is not true.

options.html: The rich and the powerful both wanted to see themselves on the canvas
During the colonial India days, portrait painting was very popular
The portraits often did not project the lavish lifestyles, wealth, and status that the empire generated.
The size of the paintings projected the importance of the patrons who commissioned the portraits

questions.explanation: Portrait paintings have primarily memorialised the rich and the powerful. Societies commissioned paintings of leading patrons and portraits of leading grandees were painted to be presented to the royal court leading aristocrats or major institutions at home.

questions.question: Who introduced the use of water colours and oil paints in India for the first time?
questions.explanation: European artists brought with them the technique of oil painting. Oil painting enabled artists to produce images that looked real.

questions.question: What techniques did the painters of Murshidabad and Patna adapt from the British while making postcards?
questions.explanation: Artists at Murshidabad worked primarily on three techniques: oil on canvas, miniature painting in watercolour on ivory and watercolour on paper and prints made by them in engraving process.

questions.question: To formally choose someone to do a special piece of work against payment is called ________.
questions.explanation: As portrait painting became popular, many European portrait painters came to India in search of profitable commissions. One of the most famous of the visiting European painters was Johann Zoffany.

questions.question: This great piece of work is by a painter from Europe who came to India in the mid - 1780s. Who painted this?
questions.explanation: This is a portrait of Governor-General Hastings with his wife in their Belvedere estate, an oil painting by Johann Zoffany(1784). He came to India in mid-1780's for five years.

questions.question: From the two oil paintings given below by Johann Zoffany, what is the main inference that one can make about the lifestyle and attitude during Colonial India.

options.html: The paintings showed that British always entertained guests
British were shown superior and imperious, with the Indians occupying a shadowy background.
The paintings showed that only the British and Indians lived in grand colonial mansions
The paintings showed that the Indians adapted British live styles.

questions.explanation: The Indians are shown as submissive, as inferior, as serving their white masters, while the British are shown flaunting their clothes, stand regally or sit arrogantly, and live a life of luxury. Indians are never at the centre of such paintings.

questions.question: Name the Nawab who fought against the British Resident controlling the affairs of the state in the 1770s and later became a dependent pensioner of the East India Company?
questions.explanation: Muhammad Ali Khan commissioned two visiting European artists, Tilly Kettle and George Willison, to paint his portraits, and gifted these paintings to the King of England and the Directors of the East India Company. The Nawab had lost political power, but the portraits allowed him to look at himself as a royal figure.

tests.name: The Changing World of Visual Arts Test 02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History The Changing World of Visual Arts CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name the two European artists commissioned by Muhammed Ali Khan.
questions.explanation: Muhammad Ali Khan commissioned two visiting European artists, Tilly Kettle and George Willison, to paint his portraits, and gifted these paintings to the King of England and the Directors of the East India Company.

questions.question: Name the artist who painted this beautiful portrait of Muhammed Ali Khan of Arcot in 1775.
questions.explanation: This is a portrait of nawab Muhammad Ali Khan of Arcot, painted by George Willison in 1775. The portrait allowed him to look at himself as a royal figure.

questions.question: Name the third category of imperial art that existed during colonial India.
questions.explanation: The tradition of "history painting" sought to dramatise and recreate various episodes of British imperial history, and enjoyed great prestige and popularity during the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries.

questions.question: Name the artist who created this painting of Lord Clive meeting Mir Jafar after the Battle of Plassey. Considered to be first of the series of history paintings, this was placed on public display in the Vauzhall Gardens in London.
questions.explanation: In the painting by Hayman act of agression and conquest is not depicted which is usually seen after a battle. It shows Lord Clive being welcomed by Mir Jafar and his troops after the battle of Plassey.

questions.question: The painting The Storming of Seringapatnam portrayed the celebration of the British military triumph over Tipu Sultan. Name the painter of this important painting full of action and energy.
questions.explanation: Rober Kerr Porter shows the British troops storming the fort from all sides, cutting Tipu's soilders to pieces, climbing the walls, raising the British flag aloft on the ramparts of Tipu's fort. The painting dramatises the event and glorifies the British triumph.

questions.question: This oil painting by David Wilkie in 1839 glorifies the British triumph over something. Name the occasion where General Sir David Baird seems to announce this is the fate of those who dare to oppose the British.

options.html: After defeating and discovering the body of Tipu Sultan
After defeating Nawab Muhammed Ali Khan
After the failure of the revolt of 1857.

questions.explanation: General Baird, who led the British army that stormed Tipu's fort is shown standing triumphantly in the middle. It was the occasion of the discovery of the body of Sultan Tipu by General Sir David Baird, 4th May 1799.

questions.question: Tipu Sultan encouraged a special type of wall painting it is called ________.
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan encouraged local traditions, and had the walls of his palace, at Seringapatanam covered with mural paintings done by local artists.

questions.question: The following mural painting depicts the celebration by Tipu Sultan and Haidar Ali after defeating the English troops. Name the battle that was fought in 1780.
questions.explanation: It is a mural painting commissioned by Tipu Sultan at the Dariya Daulat palace at Seringapatanam, commemorating Haider Ali's victory over the English army at the battle of Polilur of 1780.

questions.question: The following image is a miniature copy by a local court painter of an oil painting by the visiting British artist G Farrington depicting a procession on the occasion of an important festival celebrated by the Muslims. Name the festival.
questions.explanation: This is a picture of an Id procession painted by a local court painter in the late eighteenth century. In this painting we can see Nawab Mubarakuddaulah of Murshidabad.

questions.question: From the following options available which one can be called a collection of an Indo-European style of paintings made in India by Indian artists?
questions.explanation: Company painting is the term used for a hybrid Indo-European style of paintings made in India by Indian artists, many of whom worked for European patrons in the British East India Company or other foreign companies in the 18th and 19th centuries.

questions.question: Name the pilgrimage centre of the temple village around which the scroll painters and potters began developing a new style of art in Bengal.
questions.explanation: Kalighat painting originated in the 19th century, Bengal in the vicinity of Kalighat Kali Temple, Kalighat, Kolkata, India. The artistes were villagers who travelled from place to place with their scroll paintings and sang the scenes from the epics depicted in the paintings during village gatherings and festivals.

questions.question: What is the specialty of the early Kalighat paintings?

options.html: Use of a bold, deliberately non-realistic style, where the figures emerge large and powerful, with a minimum of lines detail and colours
Use of wall as the canvas
Use of Perspective-the way that objects appear smaller when they are further away and the way parallel lines appear to meet each other at a point in the distance

questions.explanation: Kalighat painters began to use shading to give them a rounded form, to make the images look three dimensional. Yet the images were not realistic and lifelike. Kalighat artists produced paintings on social and political themes.

questions.question: Identify this style of painting. This painting is a typical example of how Patuas depicted English life in India where hunting was considered as a sport that demonstrated courage and manliness
questions.explanation: In this Kalighat painting we can see Englishman on an elephant hunting a tiger. Kalighat paintings depict social life under British rule

questions.question: Name the important printing press set up at Calcutta in the late 19th century started by the middle-class Indians, producing life-like images of eminent Bengali personalities as well as mythological pictures.
questions.explanation: One of the most successful presses that were set up in late-nineteenth-century Calcutta was the Calcutta Art Studio. Unlike Kalighat paintings the mythological pictures produced here were realistic.

questions.question: Following image is an advertisement of a popular Indian brand of a product /item that was banned by the British in 1905. This image was used to express nationalist ideas and inspire people against the British rule by the Calcutta art Studio. Guess the product/item.
questions.explanation: This is an advertisement of an Indian brand of cigarette that was banned by the British in 1905. We can see the heads of British soilders amongst the demons killed by the godess.

This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History The Changing World of Visual Arts CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name the Principal of the Calcutta Art Studio in 1896 who redirected Indians towards their indigenous traditions.
questions.explanation: E.B.Havell was an influential English arts administrator, art historian, and author of numerous books about Indian art and architecture. He was the principal of Government School of Art Calcutta from 1896-1905 where he developed a style of art and art education based on Indian rather than Western models which led to the foundation of Bengal School of Art.

questions.question: Name the brain behind the concept of Mother India which later became a popular painting depicting India as a woman.
questions.explanation: Bharat Mata is a work painted by Abanindranath Tagore in 1905. It depicts Bharat Mata or Mother India in the style of a Hindu goddess. The painting holds historical significance as is the earliest visualisations of Bharat Mata or Mother India.

questions.question: Name the important Indian painter who used oil paints as the medium of her paintings and chose villagers as her subject. The picture given below is painted by this great artist.
questions.explanation: Three girls is a painting by Amrita Sher Gill, an Indian artist.It was painted in 1935 and won a gold medal at the annual exhibition at Bombay Art Society in 1937.

questions.question: He started using the Western style of painting, turned to miniature paintings of rural Bengal. Identify the artist. Who is being referred to here as he?
questions.explanation: Jamini Roy changed style from his academic Western training and featured a new style based on Bengali Folk traditions. His techniques as well as the subject matter was influenced by traditional art of Bengal.

questions.question: Specialised in painting scenes from Hindi mythology, his paintings defined Indian feminine beauty for several generations of Indians. Identify the artist based on the painting given here
questions.explanation: This is a painting of Damayanti from Mahabharata painted by Raja Ravi Verma.His mythological paintings became a rage among Indian princes and art collectors,who filled their palace galleries with his works.

questions.question: A great poet from Bengal took up painting in his sixties. He chose the unconscious as the source and his paintings were modern and Indian in style. Who is he?
questions.explanation: At sixty, Tagore took up drawing and painting, successful exhibitions of his many works-which made a debut appearance in Paris upon encouragement by artists he met in the South of France-were held throughout Europe.

questions.question: Name the photographer who came to India in the 1860s and set up one of the famous photographic studios in Calcutta, known as Bourne and Shepard.
questions.explanation: Samuel Bourne was a British photographer known for his prolific seven years' work in India, from 1863 to 1870. Together with Charles Shepherd, he set up Bourne and Shepherd first in Shimla in 1863 and later in Kolkata.

questions.question: The British wanted their buildings to express their power and glory and their cultural achievements. Name this building with rounded arches and pillars, the style borrowed from the Classical Greece and Rome.
questions.explanation: The General Post Office is the Central Post Office of this metropolitan city. The site where GPO is located was actually the site of first Fort William. The GPO is notable for its imposing high domed roof (rising over 220 feet) and tall Ionic-Corinthian pillars.

questions.question: The image is a pictorial representation of My Mother, painted by a very famous artist. He is the nephew of a great nobel prize winning poet. Identify the painter.
questions.explanation: In this painting by Abanindranath Tagore we can see the influence of Rajput miniatures. Abanindranath Tagore broke away from the convention of oil painting and the realistic style, and turned to medieval Indian traditions of miniature painting.

questions.question: Jatugriha Daha, is a popular painting by a famous artist. The artist is a student of Abanindranath Tagore, who simply did not follow any earlier style. Who is he/she?
questions.explanation: Abanindranath and Nandalal modified the earlier style and made it their own. In this painting you can see how Nandalal uses shading to give three dimensional effect to the figures.

questions.question: The painting by Abanindranath Tagore, in 1904 is based on a famous poem Meghaduta by a great Classical Sanskrit poet. Name the poet.
questions.explanation: In this painting, the misty background, the soft colours and the absence of any hard linesare the stylistic elements which are found in Japanese water colour paintings.

questions.question: Name the book published in Japan by Okakura Kakuzo where he believed that Asia is one. Through the book, he argued that Asia had been humiliated by the West and Asian nations had to collectively resist Western domination.
questions.explanation: The book "The Ideals of the East"is famous for its opening lines:"Asia is one."Okakura researched on Japanese art and emphasised the need to save traditional techniques of traditional Japanese art at a time they were being replaced by Western-style painting.

questions.question: Okakura Kakuzo visited an important place/ university in India. Name the place/university.
questions.explanation: Okakura visited Shantiniketan and had a powerful influence on Rabindranath Tagore and Abanindranath Tagore.

questions.question: Okakura Kakuzo had a powerful influence on two important people of India. One was Abanindranath Tagore, name the other person.
questions.explanation: Okakura visited Shantiniketan and had a powerful influence on Rabindranath Tagore and Abanindranath Tagore.

questions.question: This important painting of the Pine Trees is painted by a very popular Japanese painter. Name the painter.
questions.explanation: Hasegawa Tohaku was a Japanese painter and the founder of Hasegawa school.He is best known for his byobu folding screens such as Pine Trees ,Pine Trees and Flowering Plants

questions.question: Which one of the following is the correct description of the term sovereign?

options.html: The capacity to rule a nation
The power of ruling nation A by nation B
The capacity to act independently without outside interference
The right to control a group of nations

questions.explanation: Here the term sovereign implies that the people should be modern and conscious and was a key feature of nationalism.

questions.question: In which year was the Arms Act passed disallowing Indians from processing arms?
questions.explanation: The Arms Act was passed in 1878, disallowing Indians from possessing arms. In the same year, the Vernacular Press Act was also enacted in an effort to silence those who were critical of the government.

questions.question: In which year was the Vernacular Press Act enacted to silence those who were critical to the Government?
questions.explanation: The dissatisfaction with British rule intensified in the 1870s and 1880s. Hence the Arms Act and the Vernacular Press act were passed in 1878.

questions.question:
Why there was a furore over the attempt by the Government to introduce Ilbert Bill in 1883?

  1. The bill provided for the trial of British or European person by Indians and sought equality between British and Indian judges in the country.
  2. The bill disallowed Indians from possessing arms.
  3. The bill allowed the government to confiscate the assets of newspapers published anything that was found objectionable.
  4. The bill allowed the British to exercise control over the resources of India.
questions.explanation: The dissatisfaction with the British rule intensified in the 1870s and 1880s. In 1883 when Ilbert Bill was introduced to seek equality between British and Indian judges in the country. But the bill was withdrawn which enraged the Indians and highlighted the racial attitudes of the British in India.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, name this city where 72 delegates from all over the country met in December 1885.
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress was established when 72 delegates from all over the country met at Bombay in December 1885. The early leadership-Dadabhai Naroji, Pherozeshah Mehta, Badruddin Tyabji, W.C. Bonnerji, Surendranath Banerji, Romesh Chandra Dutt, S.Subramania Iyer, among others was largely from Bombay and Calcutta.

questions.question:
From the image given below, identify this great personality based on the clue :
He was a great businessman and publicist settled in London
He was also known as the Grand Old Man, a Parsi and the first Asian to be a British MP.
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji guided the younger nationalists and his book Poverty and Un-British Rule in India offered a scathing criticism of the economic impact of British rule.

questions.question: Identify this political reformer, who was one of the important founders of the Indian National Congress, also considered to be a great ornithologist. He also played a part in bringing Indians from the various regions together.
questions.explanation: Allan Octavian Hume was a retired British official. He was the founder of Indian National Congress a political party that was later to lead in Indian independence movement.

questions.question: Name the important book written by Dadabhai Naoroji that offered scathing criticism of the economic impact of British rule.

options.html: Poverty and Un-British Rule in India

questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji publicised his ideas by circulating information, writing reports and speaking at meetings, and also guided the young nationalists. Poverty and Un -British rule was one of his works.

questions.question: A few reasons as to why the British generally preferred not to give Indians good positions of responsibility are given below. Which one is not true?

options.html: This meant that the Civil Service examinations could not be conducted in India
This meant a movement against racism since most of the important jobs was monopolized by white officials
They felt that this meant that Indians will have a greater voice in the administration
Since British officers were sending the major part of their salary to England, they felt that it affects the drain of wealth to England

questions.explanation: Congress demanded civil service examinations be held in India as well, not just in London.

questions.question: Which one of the following is closest in meaning to Repeal?

options.html: To officially end the validity of something such as law
To start a revolt against any foreign power
To change the power from one country to another

questions.explanation: The word repeal means to undo a law, here repeal of the Arms Act and freedom of speech and expression are being talked of.

questions.question:
Which one among the following is closest in meaning to the word Nationalism?

  1. Raising your voice against someone to transfer power.
  2. A feeling of pride and love of one's country and willingness to sacrifice anything for it.
  3. Use of violence as a means of agitation.
  4. Creating political awareness by giving lectures, writing articles, etc.
questions.explanation: A growing sense of unity and looking beyond the regional boundaries and thinking of India as a single nation with oneself as part of the nation.

questions.question:
A few points related to the Moderate leaders are given below. Pick out the one that is not true with respect to the Moderates.

  1. The leaders were well versed with Western thoughts and ideas.
  2. Most of the leaders were teachers, lawyers, journalists, industrialists, etc.
  3. Most of the moderate leaders were neither educated nor well informed.
  4. The moderate leaders believed that the British could be persuaded to introduce reforms for the benefit of the Indians.
questions.explanation: Congress which was moderate in action was a body of the educated elite class. The early congress raised a number of economic issues. It demanded reduction of revenue, cut in military expenditure, and more funds for irrigation. The Moderate leaders published newspapers, wrote articles and showed how British rule was leading to the economic ruin of the country.

questions.question:
Some important approaches in the line of thought of the Moderate leaders are given below. Which of the following statements are true.

  1. The Moderates did not believe in the use of violence and only promoted constitutional means of agitation.
  2. The leaders had full faith in the British sense of fair play and justice and adopted Prayer, Petition, and Protest.
  3. To spread the message of unity and political awareness they distributed pamphlets, gave lectures, and wrote articles in the press.
  4. They organized processions and protest marches and also sent delegations to England for negotiations.
questions.explanation: The Moderate leaders wanted to develop public awareness about the unjust nature of British rule. They felt what was necessary was to express their demands and make the government aware of the feelings of Indians.

questions.question: From the following demands of Moderates, which one is not true?

options.html: Change in British economic policies to benefit Indians
Indians to be appointed in high posts in the administration

questions.explanation: The Moderates declared that the British rule had led to poverty and famines: an increase in the land revenue had impoverished peasants and zamindars, and exports of grains to Europe had created food shortages. They demanded reduction of revenue, cut in military expenditure, and more funds for irrigation.

questions.question:
Which of the following achievements of the Moderates cannot be considered true?

  1. This microscopic minority of leaders could change the policies and made a huge impact on the British.
  2. The Moderates helped in removing social injustices like the rigid caste structure that divided Indian society.
  3. The Moderates created a sense of national consciousness.
  4. Due to the persistent efforts of the Moderates, Indians understood the concepts of freedom, secularism and democracy.
questions.explanation: The British turned hostile towards the moderates and began to openly criticise the Congress. They realised that instead of becoming a means to contain the anger of the masses, the Congress was becoming the focus of Indian nationalism.

questions.question: Identify this great personality who coined the slogan Freedom is my birth right and I shall have it
questions.explanation: In Bengal Maharashtra and Punjab, leaders such as Bipin Chandra Pal, Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Lala Lajpat Raiwere begining to explore more radical objectives and methods. They argued that people must rely on their own strength, not on the good intentions of the government; people must fight for swaraj. Tilak raised the slogan ,"Freedom is my birthright and I shall have it!"

questions.question: From the following list of the names of the leaders, who did not belong to the trio- Lal-Bal-Pal?
questions.explanation: Radical nationalists were led by Lala Lajpat Rai, Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Bipin Chandra Pal (popularly known as Lal-Bal-Pal)

questions.question: Name the Viceroy who was responsible for partitioning Bengal in 1905?
questions.explanation: On 19 July 1905, the Viceroy Lord Curzon issued an order dividing the province of Bengal into two parts. At that time Bengal was the biggest province of British India and British argued for dividing Bengal for reasons of administrative convinience.

questions.question: A list of leaders are given below, identify the leader who is not considered as a Radical but a Moderate.
questions.explanation: Surendra Nath Banerjee was one of the senior-most leaders of moderate Congress and favoured accommodation and dialogue with the British. He became the patron of rising Indian leaders such as Gopal Krishna Gokhle and Sarojini Naidu.

questions.question: A list of leaders are given below, identify the leader who is not considered as a Moderate but a Radical.
questions.explanation: In 1907 at the Surat session of Congress where extremists and moderates had a major showdown he led with extremists along with Bal Gangadhar Tilak.

questions.question: Name the Marathi newspaper edited by Bal Gangadhar Tilak criticizing the British rule.
questions.explanation: Kesari, a Marathi newspaper edited by Tilak, became one of the strongest critics of British rule. The newspaper was used as the spokes piece of the Indian National Freedom Movement.

questions.question: On 16 October 1905, how was Bengal partitioned as:
questions.explanation: East Bengal in which the majority of the population was Muslim, was separated from the rest of Bengal, which had a high percentage of Hindus.

questions.question: Name the movement launched following the Partition of Bengal.
questions.explanation: The partition of Bengal infuriated people all over India. All sections of the Congress-the Moderates and the Radicals, as they may be called-opposed it. Large public meetings and demonstrations were organised and novel methods of mass protest developed. The struggle that unfolded came to be known as the Swadeshi Movement.

questions.question:
Some important features of the Swadeshi Movement are given below: Pick out the one that is not applicable to the Swadeshi Movement.

  1. Swadeshi movement stressed on the use of the English language with minimal use of Indian languages.
  2. The Swadeshi movement sought to oppose British rule.
  3. They encouraged the ideas of self-help.
  4. They boycotted British institutions and goods.
questions.explanation: The Swadeshi Movement encouraged swadeshi enterprise, national education, and the use of Indian languages.

questions.question: He is a nationalist from Punjab and one of the leading members of the Radical group which was critical of the politics of petitions. He was also an active member of the Arya Samaj. Identify this great personality who fought for the freedom of India.
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai was an Indian freedom fighter to play an important role in Indian independence movement. He was popularly known as Punjab Kesari and was the third of the Bal -Lal-Pal triumvirate.

questions.question: The Moderates opposed to the usage of a specific word/term because they felt that it involved the use of force. Which word/term did they oppose?
questions.explanation: To fight for swaraj, the radicals advocated mass mobilisation and boycott of British institutions and goods. The Moderates were opposed to the use of boycott. Hence the Congress split in 1907. After the split, the Congress came to be dominated by the Moderates.

questions.question: After a major split between the Moderates and Radicals, in which year did they reunite again?
questions.explanation: After the split, the Congress came to be dominated by the Moderates with Tilak's followers functioning from outside. The two groups reunited in December 1915.

questions.question: Who is this great Indian Muslim philosopher who was also considered as the Father of Muslim Nationalism?
questions.explanation: Sir Sayed Ahmad Khan was a teacher and a politician. He played a vital role in improving muslim status. He brought about the Two-Nation Theory and was known as the Father of the Pakistani Movement.

questions.question: In December 1906, under whose leadership a Muslim deputation met the Viceroy Lord Minto and requested for a separate representation and an organisation for Muslims.
questions.explanation: Agha Khan was the founding member and the first president of the All India Muslim League. Under his leadership, a group of Muslim landlords and nawabs met in Dacca and supported the partition of Bengal.

questions.question: Identify the city marked B, in the given map of India where the session of Congress in 1906 demanded self-rule or Swaraj.
questions.explanation: In Calcutta session of 1906 under the leadership of Dadabhai Naroji Congress adopted swaraj as the goal of Indian people.But there was a bit of politics played by Moderates. They by no means wanted to be tagged as radicals.

questions.question: Under whose initiative, the All India Muslim League was formally founded on 30 December 1906?
questions.explanation: The annual meeting of All India Muhammadan Educational Conference was held at Dacca that continued from 27th December until 30th December 1906. Here the objectives were explained and unity of Muslims was stressed under the banner of an association. It was formerly proposed by Nawab Salimullah Khan.

questions.question: Among the following group of Muslim leaders, who was in favour of unity with the Congress?
questions.explanation: Abul Kalam Azad was the youngest person to serve as the president of the Indian National Congress. He worked for Hindu Muslim unity and rejected All India Muslim League's communal separatism.

questions.question: Identify this personality who was a great Muslim leader who is considered as the founder of Pakistan and became the first Governor General of Pakistan on August 15, 1947. Officially he is known in Pakistan as Quaid-e-Azam.
questions.explanation: Mohammad Ali Jinnah was an ambassador of Hindu Muslim unity until 1920. He played an important role in making of Lucknow pact. He reorganised the Muslim League after 1934 and became the major spokesperson for the demand of Pakistan.

questions.question: What is the literal meaning of the term Revolutionary violence?

options.html: The use of violence to make a radical change within society
Distributing favours to own religious groups

questions.explanation: To fight for swaraj, the radicals advocated mass mobilisation and boycott of British institutions and goods. Some of them also suggested that revolutionary violence would be necessary to overthrow British rule.

questions.question: In which year did the British Government announce the Indian Council Act, popularly known as Minto Morley Reforms?
questions.explanation: To pacify the moderate leaders the government announced the Indian Councils Act popularly known as Minto Morley reforms. According to it, more members were introduced into the Central Legislative Council, and also the system of separate electorates was introduced.

questions.question: From among the following list of terms, which one can be described as an appointed or elected body of people with an administrative, advisory, or representative function?
questions.explanation: Some seats in the councils were now reserved for Muslims who would be elected by Muslim voters. The Muslims were grouped into separate constituencies from where only Muslim candidates would contest elections.

questions.question:
Why did Lord Minto grant a system of the separate electorate and what was the implication?

  1. Hindus and Muslims were divided and Hindus will vote only for Hindus and Muslims will vote only for Muslims.
  2. To protect the good-will of all Indians.
  3. To increase the number of elected members for Hindus and Muslims.
  4. Forcing the British to make administrative and political changes.
questions.explanation: To divide Hindu Muslim unity, with the help of some Pro-British Muslim leaders, the British conceded the so-called demand of Muslim leaders for separate electorates.

questions.question: Who were the important people behind the organization of the Home Rule Movement in 1916?

options.html: Annie Besant and Bal Gangadhar Tilak
Mohammed Ali Jinnah and Maulana Abul Kalam Azad
Lala Lajpat Rai and Surendranath Banerjee
Bipin Chandra Pal and Aurobindo Ghosh

questions.explanation: Home Rule League, either of two short-lived organisations of the same name in India established in April and September 1916, respectively by an Indian militant nationalist, Bal Gangadhar Tilak, and British social reformer and Indian independence leader Annie Besant.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, name the place, A where the Congress and Muslim League decided to work together and signed a Congress? League Pact in December 1916.
questions.explanation: The Congress and Muslim League signed the historic Lucknow Pact and decided to work together for representative government in the country. After the signing of this pact the rivalry between the moderates and extremists reduced to some extent.

questions.question: When were the Montague-Chelmsford Reforms announced?
questions.explanation: After the First World War, the government passed the Government of India Act popularly known as Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms. According to this act, the Central Legislative Council was made bicameral and the system of dyarchy or dual government was introduced.

questions.question: This year was a landmark in the Indian freedom struggle in many ways. It was this year the British Government passed the Rowlatt Act. Which year is it?
questions.explanation: The Montagu-Chelmsford Act disappointed the Indians. Protests broke out all over the country. In March 1919 the government passed the Rowlatt Act.

questions.question: In which year did Mahatma Gandhi arrive in India from South Africa after having led the Indians in non-violent marches against racist restrictions?
questions.explanation: Gandhiji aged 46, arrived in India in1915 from South Africa. He was already a respected leader, known internationally.

questions.question: Identify this great personality marked A, who fought against injustice, who believed in ahimsa and satyagraha and worked tirelessly for the freedom of India. In the image given below, he along with other Indians established the Natal Congress in Durban, South Africa in 1895 to fight against racial indiscrimination.
questions.explanation: In South Africa Gandhiji led a campaign opposing a bill which denied Indians in South Africa the right to vote.His campaign was sucessful in drawing attention to the grievances of Indians in South Africa. He also helped in founding Natal Indian Congress in 1894 and moulded the Indian community of South Africa into a unified political force.

questions.question: Indian culture is neither Hindu, Islamic, nor any other wholly.  It is a fusion of all. Whose words are echoed here?
questions.explanation: During the Rowlatt Satyagraha, the participants tried to ensure Hindus and Muslims were united in the fight against British rule. This was also a call of Mahatma Gandhi who always saw India as a land of all the people who lived in the country-Hindus, Muslims, and those of other religions.

questions.question: According to Gandhiji, who was Harijans or children of God?

options.html: People who worked in the fields

questions.explanation: The term Harijan was popularised by Indian revolutionary leader Mahatma Gandhi referring to Dalits traditionally considered to be untouchables.

tests.name: National Movement from 1870 to 1947 Test 04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History National Movement from 1870 to 1947 CBSE Online Test 04 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
