If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
From the map of India, identify the place based on the clues given below :
Gandhiji's first experiment with Satyagraha where he took up a cause of the indigo cultivators against the planters.
questions.explanation: The Champaran Satyagrah of 1917, in the Champaran district of Bihar, India during the period of British Raj was the first satyagraha movement led by Mohan Das Karamchand Gandhi and major revolt in Indian independence movement.

questions.question: Which place in Gujarat did Gandhiji take up the cause of the peasants where he successfully suspended tax collection because of crop failure?
questions.explanation: The Kheda Satyagraha of 1918, in the district of Kheda, Gujrat was the third satyagraha movement inspired by Mahatma Gandhi. Gandhiji organised this movement to support the peasants of Kheda district. People of Kheda were unable to pay high taxes levied by the British due to crop failure and plague epidemic.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, identify the city marked A in Gujarat, where Gandhiji successfully raised the wages of the textile mill workers.
questions.explanation: In February March 1918, there was a conflict between Gujarat Mill owners and the workerson the question of plague bonus of 1917. Under the leadership of Gandhiji there was a strike in the cotton mills where Gandhiji used the weapon of Hunger strike.

questions.question: Inspired by the non-violent methods of Gandhiji, in Bihar who tried to employ methods of peaceful demonstrations?
questions.explanation: In the Satyagraha of Champaran Gandhiji was joined by many young nationalists from all over India, including Brijkishore Prasaad, Rajendra Prasad, Anurag Sinha, Jawaharlal Nehru, and others.

questions.question: Fired with enthusiasm by the non-violent methods of Gandhiji, in Gujarat who tried to employ methods of peaceful demonstrations?
questions.explanation: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was Gandhiji's chief lieutenant in Gujarat. Patel and his colleagues organised a major tax revolt and all the different ethnic and caste communities of Kheda rallied around it.

questions.question: Choose the year in which the Rowlatt Act was passed?
questions.explanation: In March 1919, the government passed the Rowlatt Act. According to the Act, the government could arrest anyone without a warrant and imprison him without a trial.

questions.question: Why was the Rowlatt Act in March 1919 called the Black Act?

options.html: It led to the boycott of legislative councils and law courts
It curtailed the freedom of Indians
It led to the defiance of laws
It led to the massacre of many innocent people

questions.explanation: The Act curbed fundamental rights such as freedom of expression and strengthened police powers. Mahatma Gandhi, Mohammad Ali Jinnah, and others felt that the government had no right to restrict people's basic freedoms. They criticised the act as devilish and tyrannical.

questions.question: From the list of dates given below, which day did Gandhiji call upon all Indians to observe as the Satyagraha Day or national Humiliation Day in protest against the Rowlatt Act.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi, Mohammad Ali Jinnah, and others felt that the government had no right to restrict people's basic freedoms. They criticised the Act as tyrannical and devilish. Gandhiji asked the Indian people to observe 6 April 1919 as a day of non-violent opposition to this Act, as a day of "humiliation and prayer" and hartal(strike).

questions.question: Which one, among the following can be considered as the first all India struggle in cities against the British government?
questions.explanation: Gandhiji asked the Indian people to observe 6 April 1919 as a day of non-violent opposition to the Rowlatt Act, as a day of "humiliation and prayer" and hartal. Satyagraha Sabhas were set up to launch the movement. The Rowlatt Satyagraha turned out to be the first all-India struggle against the British government although it was largely restricted to cities.

questions.question: Name the important General who blocked the only exit and ordered to fire at the people assembled in Jalianwala Bagh.
questions.explanation: On April 13, 1919, people gathered in a parkin Amritsar, called the Jallianwala Bagh, to protest against the arrest of national leaders in Punjab. The peaceful gathering was attended by men, women, and children. General Dyer, the military commander of Amritsar, came to the park with some solders and blocked the only entrance of the park. He then ordered his men to open fire.

questions.question: On April 1919, the auspicious day of a festival, Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlew and Dr. SatyaPal were arrested in Amritsar, Punjab. Name the festival.
questions.explanation: On Sunday 13th April 1919 when the festival of Baisakhi was being celebrated people had gathered in a park in Amritsar, called the Jallianwala Bagh, to protest against the arrest of Dr. Saifiddin Kitchlew and Dr. Satya Pal.

questions.question: Name the important personality who renounced his knighthood expressing pain and anger of the country over the Jalianwallah massacre.
questions.explanation: On learning about the Jalianwala Bagh massacre, Rabindranath Tagore wrote a public letter to Lord Chelmsford and expressed the pain and anger of the country and also renounced the knighthood that had been conferred on him in 1915.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, identify this place where many were killed and wounded in park called Jalianwala Bagh
questions.explanation: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre also known as the Amritsar massacre took place on 13th April 1919 when troops of British army under the command of General Dyer fired rifles into the crowd of pilgrims who had gathered in Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar, Punjab.

questions.question: The Khilafat Movement was led by the Ali brothers to protest against the abolition of the office of the Caliph. One of the brothers was Mohammed Ali, name the other.
questions.explanation: The Khilafat movement was organised by Ali brothers-Mohammad Ali and Shaukat Ali in protest against the injustices done to Turkey after the First World War. Turkey was important to the Indian Muslims as the Sultan of Turkey also known as the Caliph and was the head of Muslims throughout the world.

questions.question: From the list given below, pick out the important lawyer who gave up his practice as a support to the Non-Cooperation Movement.
questions.explanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement gained momentum through 1921-22. Thousands of students left government-controlled schools and colleges and many lawyers such as Motilal Nehru, C.R.Das, C.Rajagopalachari, and Asaf Ali gave up their practices.

questions.question: Among the different goals of the Non-Cooperation Movement, which one of these can be considered as the main and important goal?

options.html: Attainment of Swaraj through peaceful and legitimate means
Protest against the Jalianwala Bagh massacre

questions.explanation: Non-Cooperation Movement led by Mahatma Gandhi after the Jallianwala Bagh massacre aimed at resisting British rule in India through nonviolent means Ahimsa.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, name the village in present day Uttar Pradesh, where a procession of about 3000 peasants led by the Congress workers were fired upon by the Police on 5 February 1922.
questions.explanation: On 5th February1922, in Chauri Chaura a small village in Uttar Pradesh a crowd of peasants was leading a march through the village. The police fired on their peaceful demonstration. This agitated the peasants and they set fire to the police station.

questions.question: From the image given, name this great lawyer who was also known as Deshabandhu. He was also a leader of the Swaraj Party in Bengal against British rule.
questions.explanation: Chittranjan Das popularly known as Deshbandhu was a leading Indian politician a prominent lawyer, an activist of Indian National Movement and founder leader of swaraj party in Bengal.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, name the place B, in Gujarat where the peasants organised non- violent campaigns against the high land revenue demand of the British.
questions.explanation: Kheda Satyagraha of 1918, in Kheda district of Gujarat during British Raj was the third Satyagraha movement inspired by Mohan Das Karam Chand Gandhi and was a major revolt in Indian independence movement.

questions.question: From the following list, pick out the symbol of the nationalist movement and later was placed in the centre of the national flag.
questions.explanation: The flag then called the Swaraj flag became the official flag of Congress at the 1931 meeting. The tricolour flag then adopted was designed by Pingali Venkayya. It features three horizontal stripes of saffron, white and green with a charkha in the centre.

questions.question:
Cite one good reason as to why Mahatma Gandhi called off the Non-Co-operation Movement in February 1922.

  1. Gandhiji thought that public participation was not happening.
  2. British asked Gandhiji to withdraw the Movement.
  3. Gandhiji and many others were imprisoned.
  4. Gandhiji realised that it was turning violent and was no longer peaceful.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi was against violent movements. He abruptly called off the Non-Cooperation Movement when in February 1922 a crowd of peasants set fire to a police station in Chauri Chaura.

questions.question: From the image given, name this great personality who was tried and executed at the age of 23 for standing up against the British and killing the british officer Saunders.
questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh was an Indian socialist revolutionary  whose two acts of dramatic violence against British in India and execution at the age of 23 made him a folk hero of Indian independence movement.

questions.question: The Civil Disobedience Movement was launched with an historic event by Mahatma Gandhiji in 1930. Name it with the help of the image given :
questions.explanation: Gandhiji and his followers marched for over 240 miles from Sabarmati to the coastal town of Dandi where they broke the government law by gathering natural salt  found on the seashore.

questions.question: Name the place in Gujarat from where Mahatma Gandhiji walked 400 km to Dandi.
questions.explanation: On 12th March 1930, Gandhiji along with 78 followers left the Sabarmati Ashram at Ahemdabad and travelled towards Dandi, a small village on the west coast of India. After walking for 25 days and covering a distance of 385km, the group reached Dandi.

questions.question:
What was the important event that happened on 6th April 1930 under the leadership of Mahatma Gandhi?

  1. Gandhiji picked up a handful of salt that had been formed along the shore by the evaporation of seawater.
  2. Gandhiji called off the Non-Co-operation Movement.
  3. Gandhiji Gandhi-Irwin Pact.
  4. Gandhiji asked people to wear Khadi and also requested people to spin Khadi.
questions.explanation: On 6th April 1930, Gandhiji picked up a lump of Natural salt found on the seashore and boiling seawater to produce salt. Hence, he broke the salt law.

questions.question: From Karnataka, she had been married at the age of twelve. She was arrested for picketing foreign cloth and liquor shops in Udupi. She taught spinning and organised Prabhat Pheris. Name this lady who supported the national movement.
questions.explanation: Participation in the freedom movement brought women out of their homes. Ambabai, widowed at sixteen, was arrested, served a sentence, and was rearrested. Between the prison,  terms she made speeches, taught spinning, and organised Prabhat Pheris.

questions.question:
A few points related to the Government of India Act, 1935 are given below.
Pick out the one that is not true:

  1. According to the Act, India was to become a federation based on the union of the provinces of British India and the princely states.
  2. The voting right was given to all.
  3. The Act introduced provincial autonomy.
  4. The governors and Governor-General were appointed by the British parliament and were given veto powers.
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act of 1935 prescribed provincial autonomy which meant provinces could make relatively independent decisions while remaining within a federation and the government announced elections to the provincial legislatures in 1937.

questions.question: Choose the correct year in which the Cripps Mission was sent to India to secure India's cooperation in the Second World war.
questions.explanation: The Cripps Mission was an attempt in late March 1942 by the British government to secure full Indian cooperation and support for their efforts in World War II. The mission was headed by Sir Stafford Cripps, Lord Privy Seal which held the rank of a senior minister and leader of the House of Commons.

questions.question: Name the important and historic resolution passed by All india Congress Committee on 8th August 1942 with the help of the picture given below:
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement or the India August Movement was a movement launched at the Bombay session of the All India Congress committee by Mahatma Gandhi on 8th August 1942 during World War II demanding an end to the British rule in India.

questions.question:
Identify this great statesman and radical nationlist with the help of the clues given :
He did not believe in ahimsa. He secretly left his home, went to Germany, Singapore and raised Azad Hind Fauj or Indian National Army to free India from British. He died in a plane crash on his way to Tokyo.
questions.explanation: Subhash Chandra Bose was one of the Indian freedom fighters and a cause for India's freedom. He was known as Netaji and was the president of Indian National Congress from1938-39. He attempted to get rid of British rule in India in the World War II with the help of Nazi Germany and imperial Japan.

questions.question:
Name the person speaking to Gandhiji in the image given, based on the following clues:
A veteran nationalist, and leader of the salt Satyagraha in the South, he is popularly known as Rajaji. He also served as a member of the Interim Government of 1946 and as free India?s first Indian Governor General.
questions.explanation: C. Rajagopalachari informaly called Rajaji was an Indian politician, independence activist,lawyer, writer and statesman. He was the first and the last Indian Governor-General of India after Lord Mountbatten left India in 1948, as soon India became republic in 1950.

questions.question: Recognise and name this great leader from a peasant family of Gujarat. He was the foremost organiser of the freedom movement from 1918 onwards and also served as the Presdient of the Congress in 1931. Also known as the Iron man of India.
questions.explanation: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel popularly known as Sardar Patel was the first deputy Prime Minister of India. He was an Indian barrister and statesman. He was also a senior leader of Indian National Congress and the founding father of the Republic of India.

questions.question: This famous personality with Mahatma Gandhiji, was the ambassador of Hindu-Muslim unity until 1920. He also played an important role in the Lucknow Pact. He later became a major spokesperson for Pakistan
questions.explanation: Mohammad Ali Jinnah was the founder of Pakistan. He served as a leader of All India Muslim League from 1913 until Pakistan's independence on 14th August 1947 and then as Pakistan's Governor General until his death.

questions.question:
Recognise the personality in dark glasses, sitting along with the members of the Congress Working Committee, Sevagram, 1942. Based on the clues given :
He was born in Mecca to a Bengali mother and an Arab father.
Well-versed in many languages and a scholar of Islam, he was an active participant in Gandhian movements and a staunch advocated of Hindu-Muslim unity.
He opposed Jinnah's two-nation theory
questions.explanation: Maulana Azad was a scholar and a senior Muslim leader in Indian National Congress during the Indian independence movement. Following India's independence he became the first Minister of Education in the Indian government.

questions.question: Pick out the date on which Clement Atlee made an important announcement that the British would completely transfer power to the Indians by June 1948.
questions.explanation: Attlee took a special interest in India. He supervised the process by which India was partitioned into India and Pakistan and arranged for India's independence.

questions.question: Clement Atlee, send a Cabinet Mission to India, to negotiate the terms of the transfer of power with the Indian leaders and general elections were held in India. After the general elections, who was invited to form the Interim Government in 1946?
questions.explanation: The newly elected government of Clement Attley dispatched the 1946 Cabinet mission to formulate the proposals for the formation of the government that would lead independent India. The power of Prime Minister in the interim government was bestowed on the vice-president of the Executive Council, a position held by the Congress leader Jawaharlal Nehru.

questions.question: Pick out from the following dates on which Mohammed Ali Jinnah decided to launch Direct Action Day.
questions.explanation: After the failure of the Cabinet Mission, the Muslim League decided on mass agitation for winning its Pakistan demand. It announced 16th August 1946 as Direct Action Day. On this day riots broke out in Calcutta lasting several days and resulting in the death of thousands of people.

questions.question: Recognise and name this great Pashtun leader from the North West Froniter Province, standing along with Mahatma Gandhiji .
questions.explanation: Khan Abdul Gaffar Khan also known as Badshah Khan was the founder of Khudai Khidmatgars,a powerful non-violent movement among the Pathans of his province.He was strongly opposed to the partition of India and also criticised his Congress coleagues for agreeing to 1947 division.

questions.question:
Some of the important points of Lord Mountbatten's plan are given below. Pick out the statements that are not true, if any, with respect to the Lord Mountbatten plan.

  1. Two states India and Pakistan were to be set up with a different constitution
  2. The provinces of Punjab and Bengal were to be divided on the basis of Muslim and non-Muslim majority areas
  3. India and Pakistan were free to leave or remain in the British Commonwealth
  4. A boundary commission was appointed to determine the boundaries between what was to be Pakistan and the rest of India
questions.explanation: The Indian independence Act of 1947 also known as Mountbatten plan was announced on 3rd June 1947. According to this plan principle of partition of British India was accepted by the British government and successor governments would be given domain status.

questions.question: Identify the person sitting in the centre with Jawaharalal Nehru and Mohammed Ali Jinnah . He was also the last Viceroy .
questions.explanation: The legislation was formulated by the government of Prime Minister Clement Attlee and governor-general of India Lord Mountbatten,after representatives of Indian National Congress,the Muslim League and Sikh community came to an agreement with Lord Mountbatten on what came to be known as Mountbatten Plan

questions.question: After the discussions of the Mountbatten plan, on ________ the Congress accepted the Mountbatten Plan.
questions.explanation: The primary reason behind the Congress acceptance of the Mountbatten plan was to end the communal fury between the Congress leaders and Muslim League leaders. So the Congress leaders decided by June 1947 to accept the Mountbatten Plan because only an immediate transfer of power could prevent the spread of direct action and communal disturbances.

questions.question: On which date given below did the British Parliament pass the Indian Independence Act so as to end the British rule and transfer power to the two new dominions of India and Pakistan.
questions.explanation: The Act received the Royal assent on 18th July 1947 and Pakistan came into being on 14th August and India came into being on 15th August 1947.

questions.question:
Pick out the important event that happened on 14-15 August 1947 at Red Fort, New Delhi.

  1. The National Flag was hoisted at Red Fort by Jawahar Lal Nehru.
  2. Mahatma Gandhi ji was declared the Father of Nation.
  3. Mahatma Gandhiji introduced ahimsa and non-violence to fight against the British.
  4. The Indian Constitution was framed by Dr. B R Ambedkar.
questions.explanation: On 15th August 1947, Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru raised the Indian National Flag above the Lahori Gate of the Red Fort in Delhi.

questions.question: Tryst with Destiny was an important speech made by the first Prime Minsiter of independent India on the eve of Independence . Based on the image attached, recognise the great leader who gave this awe- inspiring speech?
questions.explanation: 'Tryst with Destiny' was the speech delivered by Jawaharlal Nehru the Prime Minister of independent India to the Indian Constituent Assembly in the parliament on the eve of India's independence, towards midnight on 15th August 1947.

questions.question: During the first round of discussions of framing the Constitution, who was appointed as the Chairman of the Drafting Committee?
questions.explanation: On 29th August 1947, the Constituent Assembly set up a Drafting Committee under the chairmanship of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar to prepare a draft of the Constitution for India.

questions.question: Name the occasion/event where thousands of Indians gathered on 15 August 1947 based on the image given below:

options.html: Flag hoisting ceremony at the Red Fort

questions.explanation: When India became independent in August 1947, it faced a series of very great challenges. The given picture is The place where thousands gathered on the occasion of the flag hoisting ceremony at the Red Fort on 15 August 1947.

questions.question: Who was the first Prime Minister of independent India who hoisted the Indian flag on 15 August 1947?
questions.explanation: The first was Jawaharlal Nehru of the Indian National Congress party, who was sworn-in on 15 August 1947, when India gained independence from the British.

questions.question: Which one of the following words/terms refer to The Right to Vote.
questions.explanation: One feature of the Constitution was its adoption of universal adult franchise. Franchise means the right to vote.

questions.question: A series of meetings of the Constituent Assembly was held in New Delhi after Indian's independence with different political parties for an important discussion. What was the outcome of these series of meetings?

options.html: Stress importance to be given for vernacular languages

questions.explanation: Between December 1946 and November 1949, some three hundred Indians had a series of meetings on the country’s political future. The meetings of this “Constituent Assembly” were held in New Delhi, but the participants came from all over India, and from different political parties. These discussions resulted in the framing of the Indian Constitution, which was adopted on 26 January 1950.

questions.question: One important feature of the Constitution was its adoption of universal adult franchise. Which of the following statements best describe this term.

options.html: All Indians above the age of 19 would be allowed to vote
All Indians above the age of 16 would be allowed to vote
All Indians above the age of 17 would be allowed to vote
All Indians above the age of 21 would be allowed to vote

questions.explanation: One feature of the Constitution was its adoption of universal adult franchise. All Indians above the age of 21 would be allowed to vote in state and national elections. This was a revolutionary step.

questions.question:
Some important features of the Indian Constitution are given below: Pick if any, a statement not true.

  1. The Constitution stressed on Hindu Pakistan ideals.
  2. All Indians above the age of 21 would be allowed to vote in state and national elections.
  3. It guaranteed equality before the law to all citizens, regardless of their caste or religious affiliation.
  4. It offered special privileges for the poorest and most disadvantaged Indians.
questions.explanation: There were some Indians who wished that the political system of the new nation is based on Hindu ideals and that India itself be run as a Hindu state. They pointed to the example of Pakistan, a country created explicitly to protect and further the interests of a particular religious community – the Muslims. However, the Indian Prime Minister, Jawaharlal Nehru, was of the opinion that India could not and must not become a “Hindu Pakistan.

questions.question: Pick out the date on which the new Indian Constitution came into force.
questions.explanation: Discussions of “Constituent Assembly” resulted in the framing of the Indian Constitution, which was adopted on 26 January 1950.

questions.question: Based on the image given below who is the important leader who introduced the resolution that outlined the objectives of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: In the given picture Jawaharlal Nehru is introducing the resolution that outlined the objectives of the Constitution.

questions.question: From the following options, along with the Untouchables, who were granted reservation in seats and jobs as per the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: A third feature of the Constitution was that it offered special privileges for the poorest and most disadvantaged Indians. Along with the Untouchables, the Adivasis or Scheduled Tribes were also granted reservation in seats and jobs.

questions.question:
Identify this personality based on the clues given :
He was respectfully referred to as Babasaheb
He belonged to a Marathi speaking Dalit family and a revered leader of the Dalits.
He was a lawyer and economist
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar (1891-1956),respectfully referred to as Babasaheb, belonged to a Marathi-speaking dalit family.A lawyer and economist, he is best known as a revered leader of the Dalits and the father of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: While debating about the language used in the Constituent Assembly, who said these words a warning on behalf of people of the South some of whom threatened to separate from India if Hindi was imposed on them.
questions.explanation: There was a major debate in the Constituent Assembly concerned language. Speaking in the Assembly, T.T. Krishnamachari conveyed “a warning on behalf of people of the South”, some of whom threatened to separate from India if Hindi was imposed on them.

questions.question: Why was the date 26 January 1950 chosen for the new Constitution to come into force?

options.html: To coincide the Birthday of the Father of Indian Constitution , B R Ambedkar
To coincide the day on which Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated
To coincide the integration of the princely states
To coincide with the 20th anniversary of the Independence Day celebration at the Lahore session

questions.explanation: India's constitution came into effect on Republic Day, January 26, 1950. This date was chosen as it was the 20th anniversary of Poorna Swaraj Day, which was held on January 26, 1930.

questions.question: The image given below is that of a funeral procession that shook the entire Indian nation on 30 January 1948. Identify the event

options.html: The assassination of Partap Singh Kairon, the Chief Minister of Punjab

questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated on January 30, 1948, by Nathuram Godse.

questions.question: Name the Hindu fanatic, who shot and killed Mahatma Gandhiji.
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated on January 30, 1948, by Nathuram Godse.

questions.question: The person who said, democracy is centered in Delhi and it is not allowed to work in the same sense and spirit in the rest of the country belonged to ________.
questions.explanation: A member from Mysore feared that under the present system “democracy is centred in Delhi and it is not allowed to work in the same sense and spirit in the rest of the country”.

tests.name: India after Independence Test 02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science History India after Independence CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.

  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: From the image given below, identify the lady (extreme right), Her original name was Madeleine Slade and was a British woman who left Britain, to live and work with Gandhiji and devoted her life on implementing Gandhiji's principles.
questions.explanation: Merabehn popularly known as Mahatama Gandhi's English daughter also made significant contribution to the cause of freedom.

questions.question: Who was the Deputy Prime Minister in the cabinet of the Prime Minister Jawahar Lal Nehru?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister Nehru and Deputy Prime Minister Vallabhbhai Patel were against the creation of linguistic states.

questions.question: With the help of the attached image, name the important leader from Andhra who went on a hunger strike and died later demanding the formation of Andhra State to protect the interests of Telugu speakers, when Nehru went to campaign during the elections of 1952.
questions.explanation: The given image is of Potti Sriramulu, the Gandhian leader who died fasting for a separate state for Telugu speakers.

questions.question: When the new state of Andhra Pradesh did come into being?
questions.explanation: On 15 December 1952, fifty-eight days into his fast, Potti Sriramulu died. As a newspaper put it, “the news of the passing away of Sriramulu engulfed entire Andhra in chaos”. The protests were so widespread and intense that the central government was forced to give in to the demand. Thus, on 1 October 1953, the new state of Andhra Pradesh came into being.

questions.question: In which year was the State Reorganisation Committee set up to recommend the redrawing of the district and provincial boundaries of many provinces?
questions.explanation: A States Reorganisation Commission was set up, which submitted its report in 1956, recommending the redrawing of the district and provincial boundaries to form compact provinces of Assamese, Bengali, Oriya, Tamil, Malayalam, Kannada and Telugu speakers respectively.

questions.question: Based on which of the following did India, after the independence, reorganize the states?

options.html: On the basis of the language spoken
On the basis of the area of the state
On the basis of the status/employment of the people
On the basis of the population of the state

questions.explanation: After the independence, reorganization of the states was done on the basis of Linguistic States.

questions.question: Pick out the year in which the bilingual state of Bombay was divided into separate states for Marathi and Gujarathi.
questions.explanation: A little later, in 1960, the bilingual state of Bombay was divided into separate states for Marathi and Gujarati speakers.

questions.question: Choose the year in which the state of Punjab was divided into Punjab and Haryana.
questions.explanation: In 1966, the state of Punjab was also divided into Punjab and Haryana, the former for the Punjabi speakers (who were also mostly Sikhs), the latter for the rest (who spoke not Punjabi but versions of Haryanvi or Hindi).

questions.question: Name the place in India where the Hindu ruler Maharaja Hari Singh ruled a majority of Muslim people.
questions.explanation: Hari Singh (September 1895 - 26 April 1961) was the last ruling Maharaja of the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir in India.

questions.question: In which year did Jawahar Lal Nehru establish the Planning Commission?
questions.explanation: In 1950, the government set up a Planning Commission to help design and execute suitable policies for economic development.

questions.question: On which country's model did Jawahar Lal Nehru develop the Five Year Plans?
questions.explanation: The first Five-year Plan was launched in 1951. Pandit Nehru was greatly influenced by the achievements of Soviet Planning.

questions.question: Name the first President of the Frist Planning Commission established in 1950?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru was a great supporter of the planning process and was the first President of the Frist Planning Commission established in 1950.

questions.question: The Planning Commission designed and executed suitable policies for economic development based on a ________ model.
questions.explanation: In 1950, the government set up a Planning Commission to help design and execute suitable policies for economic development. There was a broad agreement on what was called a “mixed economy” model.

questions.question: Select the year in which the Second five Year Plan was formulated.
questions.explanation: Focused strongly on the development of heavy industries the Second Five Year Plan was formulated in 1956.

questions.question: Apart from building dams, name the important sector that was focused strongly on the Second Five Year Plan.
questions.explanation: In 1956, the Second Five Year Plan was formulated. This focused strongly on the development of heavy industries such as steel, and on the building of large dams.

tests.instructionHtml:

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name this great statistician, based on whose model the Second Five Year Plan of India was formulated.
questions.explanation: The 2nd year five year plan, functioned on the basis of Mahalanobis model.

questions.question: From the outline map of India, identify the place in Chhattisgarh marked A, where a Steel plant was set up with the help of Soviet Union in 1959, which was seen as an important step of the development of modern India after independence
questions.explanation:
The Bhilai Steel Plant location is shown the given picture.
The Bhilai steel plant was set up with the help of the former Soviet Union in 1959. Located in the backward rural area of Chhattisgarh,it came to be seen as an important sign of the development of modern India after Independence.

questions.question: This image shows a few gentlemen arriving to attend the United Nations .Name this important Indian nationalist and diplomat, standing next to Jawahar Lal Nehru ( with walking stick) who led the Indian delegation between 1952 and 1962 and argued for a policy of non-alignment.
questions.explanation: The given picture is of Jawaharlal Nehru and Krishna Menon arriving at the United Nations.

questions.question: When did India celebrate her sixty years of independence?
questions.explanation: On 15 August 2007, India celebrated sixty years of its existence as a free nation.

questions.question: In which year was the Shimla accord signed between Indian and Pakistan?
questions.explanation: The Simla Agreement signed by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi and President Zulfikar Ali Bhutto of Pakistan on 2nd July 1972.

questions.question: Mention the year in which Sheikh Mujibar Rehman rose to power and declared Bangladesh with India's support.
questions.explanation: The independence of Bangladesh was declared on 26 March 1971 at the onset of the Bangladesh Liberation War. The declaration was made by Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujibur Rahman.

questions.question: When was the People's Republic of China formed?
questions.explanation: On 21 September 1949, Communist Party Chairman Mao Zedong proclaimed the establishment of the People's Republic of China.

questions.question: India and China signed the important Panch Sheel agreement and both the countries followed these 5 principles. In which year was this agreement signed?
questions.explanation: The Panchsheel Agreement. The “Agreement on Trade and Intercourse between the Tibet region of China and India” was signed on 29th April 1954.

questions.question: Which practice was described as a Slur and a bolt-on the fair name of India?

options.html: Discrimination on the basis of Religion

questions.explanation: The practice of untouchability, described as a “slur and a blot” on the “fair name of India”, was abolished. Hindu temples, previously open to only the higher castes, were thrown open to all, including the former untouchables.

questions.question: Bombay was divided into two states in which year?
questions.explanation: In 1960, the bilingual state of Bombay was divided into separate states for Marathi and Gujarati speakers.

questions.question: Name the leader who died fasting for a separate state for Telugu Speakers.
questions.explanation: On 15 December 1952, fifty-eight days into his fast, Potti Sriramulu died.

questions.question: Like India, this neighbouring country was also under British rule till 1948. India shares a long border on the east with this neigbour. Name this neighbouring country of India.
questions.explanation: India shares a long land border of over 1600 Km with Myanmar.

questions.question: The satellite image of an island nation, earlier known as Ceylon is given below. This country shares a historic and cherished relationship with India. Identify this country
questions.explanation: Sri Lanka (formerly Ceylon) is an island nation south of India in the Indian Ocean. This country shares a historic and cherished relationship with India.

questions.question: Name the official language spoken in Sri Lanka.
questions.explanation: In 1956, the year the states of India were reorganised on the basis of language, the Parliament of Sri Lanka (then Ceylon) introduced an Act recognising Sinhala as the sole official language of the country. After much protest, Tamil, too, was given official status.

questions.question:
Identify the author of these lines - 
science and machinery he [mankind] may get huge returns for a time, but ultimately will come desolation. We have got to study Nature’s balance and develop our lives within her laws if we are to survive as a physically healthy and morally decent species.
questions.explanation: As Mahatma Gandhi’s follower Mira Behn, "science and machinery he [mankind] may get huge returns for a time, but ultimately will come desolation. We have got to study Nature’s balance, and develop our lives within her laws if we are to survive as a physically healthy and morally decent species."

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name the term that can collectively refer to consist of all living and nonliving things that surround an organism.
questions.explanation: Environment refers to the sum total of all surroundings of a living organism, including natural forces and other living things, which provide conditions for development and growth.

questions.question: ________ is anything that can be used to satisfy human wants and has a value.
questions.explanation: Things become resources only when they have value. “Its use or utility gives it a value. All resources have some value.”

questions.question: Resources include:

options.html: Things present in an environment and which humans directly use and things which humans transform into usable materials to satisfy their needs
Only things which humans transform into usable materials to satisfy their needs
Only Things present in an environment and which humans directly use
Only things that add economic value to human beings

questions.explanation: Resources include things which are present in the environment and which humans use directly and things which humans transform into usable materials to satisfy their needs. Resources like land, rivers, plants, animals, and minerals gain meaning and value with reference to the wants of people.

questions.question: Water, Electricity, Rickshaw, etc have all something in common, they have been used by people and hence they are all examples of:
questions.explanation: Utility or usability is what makes an object or substance a resource. Water, electricity, rickshaw, vegetable, and textbook have something in common. They have all been used by you, so they have utility.

questions.question: What makes an object or substance a resource is called ________.
questions.explanation: Water, electricity, rickshaw, vegetable, and textbook have something in common. They have all been used by you, so they have utility. Utility or usability is what makes an object or substance a resource.

questions.question: Some resources have economic value. From the given list of options, with respect to the given context, which one is closest in meaning to the word Value?
questions.explanation: The use or utility of resources gives it a value. All resources have some value. Value means worth.

questions.question: Grandmother's home remedies have no commercial value, but can be patented and sold to become economically valuable. What exactly is the meaning of the word Patent from the following list of options?

options.html: The exclusive right over an idea or invention
The usability or utility of a substance

questions.explanation: Patent: It means the exclusive right over any idea or invention.

questions.question: From the following which one can be described as the best example of having an economic value.
questions.explanation: Value means worth. Some resources have economic value, some do not. For example, metals may have an economic value, a beautiful landscape may not.

questions.question:
With regard to the Resources, a few statements are given, which one among the following is not true?

  1. Time and technology are two important factors that can change substances into resources.
  2. Time and technology are related to the needs of the people.
  3. People's ideas, knowledge, inventions, and discoveries lead to the creation of more resources.
  4. People are not considered as an important resource.
questions.explanation: People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are a special or important resource.

questions.question: Name the term given to the resources that are drawn from Nature and used without much modification.
questions.explanation: Resources that are drawn from Nature and used without much modification are called natural resources. The air we breathe, the water in our rivers and lakes, the soils, minerals are all-natural resources.

questions.question: Some of the principles of Sustainable Development are given. Select the incorrect one.

options.html: Minimize the depletion of natural resources
Conserve the earth's vitality and diversity
The government should only take care
Respect and care for all forms of life

questions.explanation: Along with the government, citizens should also conserve resources. Enable communities to care for their own environment.

questions.question: On the basis of origin, Natural resources can be classified as ________ and ________ resources.
questions.explanation: Based on their origin, resources can be abiotic or biotic. Abiotic resources are non-living while biotic resources are living. Soils, rocks, and minerals are abiotic but plants and animals are biotic resources.

questions.question: On the basis of the availability of supply, Natural resources can be classified as____________ and ________.
questions.explanation: Natural resources can be broadly categorised into renewable and non-renewable resources on the basis of supply.

questions.question: On the basis of their use and development, Natural resources can be classified as ________ and ________ resources.
questions.explanation: On the basis of their development and use, resources can be classified into two groups, actual resources, and potential resources.

questions.question: On the basis of their distribution, Natural resources can be classified as ________ and ________ resources.
questions.explanation: On the basis of their distribution resources can be ubiquitous or localised. Resources that are found everywhere like the air we breathe, are ubiquitous. But those which are found only in certain places are localised, like copper and iron ore.

questions.question: From the given list of options, which one can be considered as an example of a Natural Resource?
questions.explanation: Resources that are drawn from Nature and used without much modification are called natural resources. Hence, Sunlight is a natural resource.

questions.question: Identify from the list given, which one is not a Renewable Resource?
questions.explanation: Coal is a non-renewable source of energy.

questions.question: Analyse the following and fill up the blank from the options given
questions.explanation: Natural resources are classified into different groups depending upon their level of development and use;origin; stock and distribution.

questions.question: All living organisms including the flora and fauna are classified under:
questions.explanation: Biotic Resources are the resources which are obtained from the biosphere and have life are called Biotic Resources. Eg: Flora and Fauna.

questions.question: On the basis of their development and Use, resources can be classified as actual and ________ resources.
questions.explanation: On the basis of their development and use, resources can be classified into two groups, actual resources and potential resources.

questions.question: Name the Resources where the quantity is known.
questions.explanation: Actual resources are those resources whose quantity is known. These resources are being used in the present.

questions.question: The rich deposits of coal in the Ruhr region of Germany is the best example for ________.
questions.explanation: Actual resources are being used in the present. Eg.: The rich deposits of coal in the Ruhr region of Germany.

questions.question: Petroleum found in the West Asian areas is a good example of ________.
questions.explanation: Actual resources are being used in the present. Eg.: Petroleum in West Asia.

questions.question: Dark soils of the Deccan plateau in Maharashtra is a good example of ________.
questions.explanation: Actual resources are being used in the present. Eg.: The dark soils of the Deccan plateau in Maharashtra.

questions.question: Name the type of resource whose entire quantity may not be known and these are not being used at present.
questions.explanation: Potential resources are those whose entire quantity may not be known and these are not being used at present. These resources could be used in the future.

questions.question: High-speed winds, solar energy, bio waste, etc were all considered as potential sources of energy in the past, but today they are all examples of ________.
questions.explanation: The level of technology we have at present may not be advanced enough to easily utilise these resources. High-speed winds, solar energy, bio waste were a potential resource two hundred years ago. Today they are actual resources.

questions.question: On the basis of ________, natural resources can be classified as renewable and non-renewable.
questions.explanation: On the basis of deposit or stock, natural resources can be classified as renewable and non-renewable.

questions.question: Name the type of resources which have the capacity or ability to reproduce or renew quickly.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are those which get renewed or replenished quickly.

questions.question: Solar Energy is an example of ________.
questions.explanation: Some of the renewable resources are unlimited and are not affected by human activities, such as solar energy.

questions.question: What would you call resources of limited stock?
questions.explanation: Non-renewable resources are those which have limited stock. Once the stocks are exhausted it may take thousands of years to be renewed or replenished.

questions.question:
From the following, which one would be the appropriate reason to explain why resources like Coal, Petroleum, Natural gas, etc are considered as Non-renewable.

  1. As the resources are found everywhere.
  2. As it may take millions of years to produce them again.
  3. As the distribution of resources is confined only to certain places.
  4. As the resources can be destroyed due to the careless type.
questions.explanation: Non-renewable resources are those which have limited stock. Once the stocks are exhausted it may take thousands of years to be renewed or replenished. Since this period is much more than human life spans, such resources are considered non-renewable. Coal, petroleum, and natural gas are some examples.

questions.question: The distribution of resources is unequal because these factors differ so much over the earth. A list of factors are given below. Which one among them is not considered as an important factor.
questions.explanation: The distribution of natural resources depends upon a number of physical factors like terrain, climate, and altitude. It doesn't include Water.

questions.question: Name the resources that are found everywhere like the air we breathe.
questions.explanation: On the basis of their distribution, Resources that are found everywhere like the air we breathe, are ubiquitous.

questions.question: From the following list, which one cannot be considered as a Localised resource.
questions.explanation: Resources that are found everywhere are ubiquitous. But those which are found only in certain places are localised. Hence, Sunlight is a ubiquitous resource.

questions.question: Which term from the following best describes those Resources created by human beings using technology and skills.
questions.explanation: People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are a special resource. People are human resources.

questions.question: Choose the ultimate resource on the earth from the following:
questions.explanation: People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are the ultimate resource.

questions.question:
Which one of the following reasons is best suitable as to why human beings are called the ultimate(best) resource on the earth?

  1. Human beings can explore, develop and convert the natural resources into useful goods as per the abilities and demands
  2. Human beings are found everywhere on earth
  3. Human beings are Renewable resources
  4. The population of human beings in the world is ever-increasing
questions.explanation: Humans have the ability to transferring physical material into a valuable resource. People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are the ultimate resource.

questions.question:
Pick out the option that best describes the term human resource development?

  1. Increasing the population of human beings.
  2. Increasing the natural resources so that there is a proportionate increase in human resources as well.
  3. Improving the quality of people's skills so that they are able to create more resources.
  4. Increase in technological development so as to increase the lifestyle of human beings.
questions.explanation: Education and health help in making people a valuable resource. Improving the quality of people’s skills so that they are able to create more resources is known as human resource development.

questions.question: Using resources carefully and giving them time to get renewed can be termed as ________.
questions.explanation: If we are not careful then even renewable resources can become very scarce and the non-renewable ones can definitely get exhausted. Hence, using resources carefully and giving them time to get renewed is called resource conservation.

questions.question: Name the term that can refer to balancing the need to use resources and also conserve them for the future.
questions.explanation: Sustainable development means carefully utilising resources so that besides meeting the requirements of the present, also takes care of future generations. In short, Balancing the need to use resources and also conserve them for the future is called sustainable development.

questions.question: Which one of the following is considered as the Golden R's of conservation?
questions.explanation: Reduce, Re-use, Recycle, Recover. These are the 4 'R's to help conserve resources.

questions.question:
Atul, David, Giridhar gives tips to a group of children on the methods of conservation of resources. Which one among the following cannot be put into practice by the children?

  1. Using Plastic bags that cannot be recycled, for shopping
  2. Watering plants by making use of the water used to wash clothes
  3. Switching off the lights and fan when not in use
  4. Ensuring that the water tap is closed when not in use
questions.explanation: Plastic bags cannot be recycled instead of use paper bags that can be recycled, for shopping.

questions.question:
Listed below are some important principles of sustainable development. Which one among them is not true with respect to the principles of sustainable development.

  1. Respect and care for all forms of life.
  2. Maximise the depletion of natural resources.
  3. Conserve the earth's vitality and diversity.
  4. Change personal attitude and practices toward the environment.
questions.explanation: Minimise the depletion of natural resources.

questions.question:
The future of our planet and its people is linked with our ability to maintain and preserve the life support system that nature provides. Some of the important points related to this are given below, which of these statements are not true with respect to sustainable development.

  1. Ensuring minimum benefits with maximum wastage.
  2. All uses of renewable resources are sustainable
  3. The diversity of life on the earth is conserved
  4. The damage to the natural environmental system is minimized
questions.explanation: Ensuring maximum benefits with minimum wastage.

questions.question: Identify the best example to conserve resources from the given list.

options.html: We should focus on Optimal use of resources
We should focus on the indiscriminate use of resources
We should maximize the depletion of resources

questions.explanation: For conserving resources we should not waste it and focus on Optimal use of resources.

chapters.name: Land Soil Water Natural Vegetation and Wildlife Resources

General Instructions:

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name the most important natural resource.
questions.explanation: The land is among the most important natural resources. It covers only about thirty percent of the total area of the earth’s surface and all parts of this small percentage are not habitable.

questions.question: What percentage of the total area of the earth's surface is covered by the most important natural resource Land?
questions.explanation: The land is among the most important natural resources. It covers only about thirty percent of the total area of the earth’s surface and all parts of this small percentage are not habitable.

questions.question: What percentage of the surface of the earth is occupied by Water as ice sheets and glaciers?
questions.explanation: 70 percent of this freshwater occurs as ice sheets and glaciers in Antarctica, Greenland, and the mountainous regions of the world.

questions.question:
A few reasons as to why there is an uneven distribution of the population in different parts of the world are given below. Pick out the most appropriate reason.

  1. Due to varied characteristics of land and climate.
  2. Due to the fact that land covers only about 30% of the total area.
  3. Due to the fact that water covers only about 70% of the total area.
  4. Due to the fact that earth is the only heavenly body in the universe.
questions.explanation: The uneven distribution of the population in different parts of the world is mainly due to varied characteristics of land and climate. The rugged topography, steep slopes of the mountains, low-lying areas susceptible to waterlogging, desert areas, thickly forested areas are normally sparsely populated or uninhabited.

questions.question:
Give the most appropriate reason as to why plains and river valleys are densely populated.

  1. Plains and rivers occupy 70% of the total inhabitable area.
  2. Presence of rivers is responsible for moderating temperature conditions.
  3. River water contains a large quantity of dissolved salts and hence suitable for consumption.
  4. Plains and rivers offer suitable land for agriculture.
questions.explanation: Plains and river valleys offer suitable land for agriculture. Hence, these are the densely populated areas of the world.

questions.question: Given below are some reasons as to why the rocky slopes of the mountains and low-lying areas are sparsely populated. Pick out the correct reason from the following:

options.html: The proportion of these areas vary.
These areas are not suitable for cultivation.
These areas are prone to flooding during rainy seasons.
Clearing of the forest land in these areas has resulted in a reduction in the forest areas.

questions.explanation: Rocky slopes of the mountains and low-lying areas are sparsely populated because during the rainy season accumulate to the low levels and these areas become prone to floods.

questions.question: The fresh water is continuously being renewed and recharged through the ________.
questions.explanation: Water can neither be added nor subtracted from the earth. Its total volume remains constant. Its abundance only seems to vary because it is in constant motion, cycling through the oceans, the air, the land, and back again, through the processes of evaporation, precipitation, and run-off. This is referred to as the ‘water cycle’.

questions.question: Classification of the land, based on soil fertility:
questions.explanation: Classification of the land based on soil fertility: Fertile Land and Barren Land

questions.question: On the basis of the development of that area, land can be classified as ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: Human settlements are classified as rural or urban depending on the basis of the development of that area.

questions.question: Identify from the list given below, the different type of land-based on the ownership of the land:
questions.explanation:
Land can also be divided on the basis of ownership.

  1. Private land
  2. Community land

questions.question: Name the term that can be given to Land that is suitable for crop production.
questions.explanation: Arable land is the land capable of being ploughed and used to grow crops.

questions.question: Out of the given options, which one refers to the land used for grazing cattle?
questions.explanation: Pasture Land is the land covered with grass and other low plants suitable for grazing animals, especially cattle or sheep.

questions.question: From the given options, pick out the meaning of the term Fallow Land.

options.html: Land which can be cultivated, but has been left unused for the season
Land not having good fertility soil

questions.explanation: Fallow land is the land ploughed and harrowed but left for a period without being sown in order to restore its fertility or to avoid surplus production.

questions.question:
Reading the following statement:
Land is used for various purposes like agriculture, forestry, mining, building houses, roads, setting up of industries etc.
Connect from the given list, the commonly used term with respect to the above sentence
questions.explanation: Land is used for different purposes such as agriculture, forestry, mining, building houses, roads and setting up of industries. This is commonly termed as Land use.

questions.question: A few physical factors that determine the use of land are given below. Pick out the one that is not a physical factor.
questions.explanation: The use of land is determined by physical factors such as topography, soil, climate, minerals, and availability of water.

questions.question:
The Land Use in a few selected countries according to their percentage area is given below. Analyse the table and answer the questions. Name the country with the highest land used for grazing cattle?

Countries Cropland Pasture Forest Other Use
Australia 6 56 14 24
India 57 4 22 17
Japan 12 2 67 19
France 35 21 27 17
UK 29 46 10 16
questions.explanation: The Land Use in Australia in pasture or grazing is 56% which is highest amongst other countries.

questions.question:
The Land Use in a few selected countries according to their percentage area is given below. Analyse the table and answer the questions. Name the country that uses the maximum land for cultivation

Countries Cropland Pasture Forest Other Use
Australia 6 56 14 24
India 57 4 22 17
Japan 12 2 67 19
France 35 21 27 17
UK 29 46 10 16
questions.explanation: The Land Use in India in cropland is 57% which is highest amongst other countries.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is true regarding community lands:

options.html: Community lands are owned by the community.
Community land is an encroached land.
Community lands are called private land.
It is not called common property resources.

questions.explanation: Community land is owned by the community for common uses like a collection of fodder, fruits, nuts, or medicinal herbs. These community lands are also called common property resources.

questions.question:
Some important threats to the environment due to over-exploitation of land resources are given below.

  1. Land degradation
  2. Landslides
  3. Soil erosion
  4. Desertification

Which one of the following statements is true and form important threats?
questions.explanation: Land degradation, landslides, soil erosion, desertification are the major threats to the environment because of the expansion of agriculture and constructional activities.

questions.question:
Cite the most important reason as to why Land degradation, landslides, soil erosion, desertification, etc are major threats to the environment, from the following options.

  1. Because of the expansion of agriculture and construction activities.
  2. Because people and their demands are growing because the availability of land is not limited.
  3. Because of suitable, planned and scientific methods of cultivation.
  4. Because of the superior quality of land used for cultivation.
questions.explanation: Land degradation, landslides, soil erosion, desertification are the major threats to the environment because of the expansion of agriculture and constructional activities.

questions.question: What does the term Land degradation refer to?

options.html: Decline in the productivity of cultivated land
Decline in the use of fertilizers
Presence of rocks and minerals in the soil

questions.explanation: Land degradation is the process of deterioration of soil or loss of fertility of the soil.

questions.question: Select the term that refers to the slashing and burning of the trees for agriculture.
questions.explanation: Slash and burn agriculture also knows as Jhum cultivation is a method in which forests are cut down and burnt to clear land for cultivation. The land obtained is fertile and cultivation is practiced on it for a few years before it is left for the regeneration of vegetation and people move to other places to cultivate.

questions.question: Identify the method that cannot be considered to ensure that the natural resources are not depleted, from among the common methods.

options.html: Uncontrolled use of fertilizers and pesticides

questions.explanation: The present rate of degradation of land resources must be checked. Afforestation, land reclamation, regulated use of chemical pesticide and fertilisers and checks on overgrazing are some of the common methods used to conserve land.

questions.question:
Identify this major threat to the environment from the image attached based on the following clues :
A mass movement of rock, debris or earth down a slope.
Often takes place in conjunction with earth quakes, floods and volcanoes.
A major disaster in the hilly terrains and is a major area of concern
questions.explanation: Landslides are simply defined as the mass movement of rock, debris or earth down a slope. They often take place in conjunction with earthquakes, floods and volcanoes. A prolonged spell of rainfall can cause heavy landslide that can block the flow of river for quite some time.

questions.question: Some important techniques to cope with Landslide are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

options.html: Implementation of the surface drainage control
Hazard mapping locate areas prone to landslides
Construction of retention wall to stop the land from slipping

questions.explanation: An increase in the vegetation cover is an effective way to arrest landslide.

questions.question: From the attached image, identify the profile of Soil marked B
questions.explanation: Layer B is Sub soil with sand, silt and clay.

questions.question: From the attached image of the profile of Soil, identify the one that has a fine texture, a high content of organic matter ie humus and is vital for plant growth
questions.explanation: A horizon: Top layer of soil is called A-horizon or top-soil. A-horizon is generally dark in colour and rich in humus. A horizon is generally soft and fertile vital for growth of plant.

questions.question: Name the term that can be given to the various layers in the soil after it is cut out like a slice of cake.
questions.explanation: A soil horizon is a layer parallel to the soil surface. It is the various layers in the soil after it is cut out like a slice of cake.

questions.question: The major factors of soil formation are the nature of parent rock and ________.
questions.explanation: The major factors of soil formation are the nature of the parent rock and climatic factors. Other factors are the topography, role of organic material, and time taken for the composition of soil formation.

questions.question: Analyse the given representation and complete the blank by filling it up an important factor of soil formation.
questions.explanation:
Factors of soil Formation

  1. Relief: Altitude and slope determines the accumulation of soil.
  2. Parent Rock: It determines, colour, texture, chemical properties, mineral contents, etc.
  3. Climate: Rainfall and temperature influence rate of humus and weathering.
  4. Flora, Fauna and Micro organisms.
  5. Time: It determines thickness of soil profile.

tests.name: Natural and Wildlife Resources Test 03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Geography Natural and Wildlife Resources CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.

  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Soil erosion and ________ are the major threats to the soil as a resource.
questions.explanation: Soil erosion and depletion are the major threats to the soil as a resource. Both human and natural factors can lead to degradation of soils.

questions.question: A few factors which lead to soil degradation are given below, pick out the one that is not a factor for soil degradation.
questions.explanation: Mulching is the methods of soil conservation.

questions.question: The bare ground between plants is covered with a layer of organic matter like straw to help retain soil moisture. Name this important method of soil conservation.
questions.explanation: Mulching: Bare ground between plants is covered with a layer of organic matter like straw. It helps to retain soil moisture.

questions.question: Trenches are made in front of the barriers developed with the help of stones or grass along the contour lines in the field. From the following list, choose the name given to this method of soil conservation.
questions.explanation: Contour Barriers: Stones, grass, soil are used to make barriers. Trenches are made in front of the barriers to collect water.

questions.question:
What does the term Rock Dams refer to?

  1. Farming on rocky terrains.
  2. Rocks are piled up to slow down the flow of water to prevent gullies and soil loss.
  3. Rows of trees planted across the rocky areas for soil conservation.
  4. Natural barriers are created on rocky areas to check the wind movement to protect the soil cover.
questions.explanation: Rock dam is one of the methods of soil conservation. Under this method, Rocks are piled up to slow down the flow of water. This prevents gullies and further soil loss.

questions.question: Identify this method of soil conservation from the picture attached:
questions.explanation: Terrace Farming is the method in which Steps can be cut out on the slopes making terraces.

questions.question: Ploughing parallel to the contours of a hill slope to form a natural barrier for water to flow down the slope. With the help of the picture attached, name this important method of soil conservation.
questions.explanation: The given method is Contour Ploughing: Ploughing along contours can decrease the flow of water down the slopes.

questions.question: Given one important reason as to why in the Intercropping method of soil conservation, different crops are sown at different times.

options.html: To prevent soil from rain wash

questions.explanation: Under Intercropping method different crops are grown in alternate rows and are sown at different times to protect the soil from rain wash.

questions.question: Name this important method of soil conservation used in the coastal and dry regions.
questions.explanation: Shelter Belts: Rows of trees are planted to create shelter. Thus, the speed of wind is reduced.Such practice is done in dry and coastal region.

questions.question: Expand ICAR:

options.html: The Indian Council of Agrarian Research
The Indian Committee of Agricultural Research
The Indian Council of Agronomic Research
The Indian Council of Agricultural Research

questions.explanation: The Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) is an autonomous body responsible for co-ordinating agricultural education and research in India.

questions.question: According to ICAR, how many group classifications of soils are found in India?
questions.explanation: According to ICAR(Indian Council of Agricultural Research) soils are divided into 8 categories. They are alluvial soils, Black cotton soils, Red soils, Laterite soils, Mountainous or forest soils, Arid or desert soils, Saline and alkaline soils, Peaty and marshy soils.

questions.question: Name the rich and fertile soil formed by the depositional action of rivers, found in the Indo-Gangetic Plains.
questions.explanation: Alluvial soil is very fertile and it is found near the river or plains formed by rivers. Alluvial soil is deposited by the river system. It is found near the Indo Gangetic Plains.

questions.question: What is the other name for Cotton Soil?
questions.explanation: Black soil is suitable for the growth of cotton and hence it is also called as Black Cotton Soil.

questions.question:
Cite one important reason as to why the Red Soil called so?

  1. Due to the presence of iron oxides in the soil.
  2. Due to the fact that is found only in dry regions.
  3. Due to the presence of the rich humus.
  4. Due to the fact that red eroded material deposits are transported by the rivers.
questions.explanation: Red soil develops a reddish colour due to diffusion of iron in crystalline and metamorphic rocks and the presence of iron oxide.

questions.question: Identify this type of soil present in Maharashtra and West Bengal regions of India, used to grow millets, jute etc .
questions.explanation: The laterite soil develops in areas with high temperature and heavy rainfall. This type of soil is present in Maharashtra and West Bengal regions of India, used to grow millets, jute etc.
