questions.question: Name the basis on which the textile industry can be divided?
questions.explanation: The textile industry can be divided on the basis of raw materials used in them. Fibres are the raw material of the textile industry. Fibres can be natural or man-made. Natural fibres are obtained from wool, silk, cotton, linen and jute. Man-made fibres include nylon, polyester, acrylic and rayon.

questions.question: From where are the natural fibres obtained for the textile industry?
questions.explanation: Fibres can be natural or man-made. Natural fibres are obtained from wool, silk, cotton, linen and jute. Man-made fibres include nylon, polyester, acrylic and rayon.

questions.question: From where are the manmade fibres obtained?
questions.explanation: Fibres can be natural or man-made. Natural fibres are obtained from wool, silk, cotton, linen and jute. Man-made fibres include nylon, polyester, acrylic and rayon.

questions.question: Name the major industry which provides the primary needs of humans like clothing.
questions.explanation: Cotton, wool, silk, jute, flax have been used for making cloth. The textile industry provides the primary need of human clothing. It can be divided on the basis of raw materials used in them. Fibres are the raw material of the textile industry. Fibres can be natural or man-made. Natural fibres are obtained from wool, silk, cotton, linen and jute. Man-made fibres include nylon, polyester etc.,

questions.question: Name the place where the power looms were first used?
questions.explanation: The cotton textile industry is one of the oldest industries in the world. Till the industrial revolution in the 18th century, cotton cloth was made using hand-spinning techniques (wheels) and looms. In 18th century power looms facilitated the development of the cotton textile industry, first in Britain and later in other parts of the world. Today India, China, Japan, and the USA are important producers of cotton textiles.

questions.question: India has been a producer of high-quality cloth from old times. These were handmade and hence production was less but cost very high. Name the cloth item for which Dhaka was famous?
questions.explanation: Muslins of Dhaka hand-spun and hand-woven cloth already had a wide market. It is known worldwide for its quality and design.

questions.question: Name the place in India where the cloth material Calicos was famous for?
questions.explanation: Calicos were hand-spun and hand-woven cloth found in Calicut was known worldwide for its quality and design.

questions.question: Chintzes was a very popular cloth material in the olden days. In which place of India was it produced?
questions.explanation: Chintzes were hand-spun and hand-woven cloth of Masulipatnam was known worldwide for its quality and design.

questions.question:
Identify this place, marked A, based on the clues given :
This place is located along the bank of river Sabarmati in Gujarat
This place is the second largest centre of textile industry in India
This place is also called the Manchester of India
questions.explanation: It is located in Gujarat on the banks of the Sabarmati river. The first mill was established in 1859. It soon became the second largest textile city of India, after Mumbai. Ahmedabad was therefore often referred to as the ‘Manchester of India’. Favourable locational factors were responsible for the development of the textile industry in Ahmedabad.

questions.question: Initially, the textile industry flourished only in the states of Gujarat and Maharashtra because of favourable humid climate. But today this industry has spread to other parts of India and hence occupies an important position in the world. Choose the appropriate reason for it.

options.html: Today humidity and frost-free climate can be created artificially anywhere
Lack of a proper transport network in other parts of India
Skilled labourers were available only in Gujarat and Maharashtra
The other states of India were densely populated

questions.explanation: Initially, this industry flourished in the states of Maharashtra and Gujarat because of favourable humid climate. But today, humidity can be created artificially, and raw cotton is a pure and not weight losing raw material, so this industry has spread to other parts.

questions.question: Name the important textile manufacturing centre, which is also called the Manchester of Japan.
questions.explanation: It is an important textile centre of Japan, also known as the ‘Manchester of Japan’. The textile industry developed in Osaka due to several geographical factors. The extensive plain around Osaka ensured that land was easily available for the growth of cotton mills. The warm humid climate is well suited to spinning and weaving. The river Yodo provides sufficient water for the mills. Labour is easily available. The location of port facilitates import of raw cotton and for exporting textiles.

questions.question: In India, which city is considered to be the main hub of the IT industry?
questions.explanation: Because, Bengaluru has always had a unique advantage, as a city with the highest availability of middle and top management talent.

questions.question:
From where did the Silicon Valley get its name?

  1. Got its name from a valley by the same name that existed in California, US.
  2. Got its name from the meaning of the word silicon.
  3. Got its name from the particular computer software.
  4. Got its name from the silicon that was used to make chips for computers.
questions.explanation: Bengaluru is located on the Deccan Plateau from where it gets the name ‘Silicon Plateau’. The city is known for its mild climate throughout the year. Silicon Valley is a part of Santa Clara Valley, located next to the Rocky Mountains of North America.

questions.question: In 1980, the Government of Karnataka developed an electronic city in Bangalore, 18 km from the core city. Find out the name of that city.
questions.explanation: Because the city is known for its mild climate throughout the year. Silicon Valley is a part of Santa Clara Valley, located next to the Rocky Mountains of North America. The area has a temperate climate with the temperatures rarely dropping below 0 degrees centigrade.

questions.question: From the map of India, identify this place also known as the Silicon Valley of India:
questions.explanation:
Bengaluru is located on the Deccan Plateau from
where it gets the name ‘Silicon Plateau’

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Name the ultimate resource on the earth.
questions.explanation: People are a nation’s greatest resource. It is people with their demands and abilities that turn them into ‘resources’. Hence, human resources are the ultimate resource.

questions.question:
A few important statements with respect to Human Resources are given below. Pick out the one that is not true.

  1. Human resource is the ultimate resource.
  2. The numbers and characteristics of human resources do not change.
  3. Human resources like other resources are not equally distributed.
  4. Human resources differ in educational levels, age, and sex.
questions.explanation: Human resources like other resources are not equally distributed over the world. They differ in their educational levels, age, and sex. Their numbers and characteristics also keep changing.

questions.question: On the basis of skill, labour employed in the process of production can be classified as:
questions.explanation:
On the basis of skill, we can classify labour (people) employed in the process of production into three groups:

  1. unskilled (no specific skill),
  2. skilled (has the basic skills required for the job)
  3. highly skilled (has expertise in the skills required for the job).

questions.question: Choose the option which is not the factor on which the productivity of a nation's human resources depends.
questions.explanation: People vary greatly in their age, sex, literacy level, health condition, occupation, and income level. The productivity of nation's human resources depends on these factors.

questions.question:
Which one from the following refers to Census?

  1. The process of taking a count of the total number of people in a country.
  2. The process of counting the number of states in a country.
  3. The process of counting the land area in a country.
  4. The process of counting the mountains and hilly terrains in a country.
questions.explanation: Census is the process of taking count of the total population of the country. In India, census is held every ten years.

questions.question: Census is conducted after how many years in India?
questions.explanation: Census is the process of taking count of the total population of the country. In India, it is held for every ten years.

questions.question:
Pick out the appropriate definition for population distribution.

  1. The way in which people live in urban areas.
  2. The way in which people live in the villages.
  3. The way in which people are spread across the earth surface.
  4. The way in which skilled human resources are utilized in a country.
questions.explanation: The way in which people are spread across the earth's surface is known as the pattern of population distribution.

questions.question: Choose the percentage of the total population that lives in Asia.
questions.explanation: Of every 100 people in the world, 60 people live in Asia. Hence 60% of the world's population is found in Asia.

questions.question: The areas which usually enjoy an equable climate, adequate rainfall, where the land is flat and the soil is fertile are referred to as:
questions.explanation: The four major geographical factors which affect the distribution of population are a relief (shape and height of land), climate, availability of water, and resources like soil and minerals.

questions.question:
Identify the wrong statement with respect to thinly populated areas.

  1. The climate here is inhospitable.
  2. Usually, the high altitude areas and extremely cold zones are sparsely populated.
  3. Very few people live in the equatorial forests.
  4. These regions enjoy an equable climate where there is adequate rainfall.
questions.explanation: Thickly populated regions enjoy an equable climate where there is adequate rainfall. Generally, People usually avoid extreme climates that are very hot or very cold like Sahara desert, polar regions of Russia, Canada and Antarctica.

questions.question:
Population density refers to:

  1. the number of people living per square unit of area.
  2. the number of skilled people in a country.
  3. the way in which people are spread across the earth's surface.
  4. the number of unskilled people in a country.
questions.explanation: Population density is the number of people living in a unit area of the earth’s surface. It is normally expressed as per square km. The average density of population in the whole world is 45 persons per square km. South-Central Asia has the highest density of population followed by East and South East Asia. Average density of the population in India is 324 persons per square km.

questions.question: ________ percent of the world's people stay in just 10 countries.
questions.explanation: Sixty percent of the world’s people stay in just 10 countries. All of them have more than 100 million people.

questions.question: What is the average density of population in the whole world?
questions.explanation: The average density of population in the whole world is 51 persons per square km. Population density is the number of people living in a unit area of the earth’s surface. It is normally expressed as per square km.

questions.question: Pick out the incorrect statement pertaining to the density of population.

options.html: Availability of mineral resources, industrial development, good transport network contribute to the high density of population in the North Eastern and South Western coasts of America
The coastal parts of Africa, Europe, and Asia have a moderate density of population
Deserts like Sahara, Kalahari, etc have a high density of population
In China, the population is concentrated along the banks of the Yangtze Yang and Hwang Ho rivers

questions.explanation:
Topography: People always prefer to live on plains rather than mountains and plateaus because these areas are suitable for farming, manufacturing, and service activities. The Ganga plains are the most densely populated areas of the world while mountains like Andes, Alps, and the Himalayas are sparsely populated.
Climate: People usually avoid extreme climates that are very hot or very cold like Sahara desert, polar regions of Russia, Canada, and Antarctica.

questions.question: Choose the non-physical factor that affects the distribution of population in a country.
questions.explanation:
Factors affecting the distribution of population
Geographical Factors

  1. Topography
  2. Climate
  3. Soil
  4. Water
  5. Minerals

Social, Cultural and Economic Factors

  1. Social
  2. Cultural
  3. Economic

questions.question: Give one example where countries with stable governments tend to have denser populations.
questions.explanation: Countries with stable governments tend to have a high population density e.g. Singapore. As people feel more secured and protected.

questions.question: This is an example of a place where religious or cultural activities tend to attract more people.
questions.explanation: Places with religion or cultural significance attract people. Varanasi, Jerusalem and Vatican city are some examples.

questions.question: Cite an example from the following which shows that people usually avoid extreme climates.
questions.explanation: People usually avoid extreme climates that are very cold like polar regions of Russia, Canada, and Antarctica.

questions.question: Give reasons as to why Alaska in North America is more populated.

options.html: Due to the fertile soils suitable for agriculture
Due to the excellent transport facilities
Due to the availability of mineral resources
Due to the availability of water

questions.explanation: Alaska is densely populated because of the availability of mineral resources and rapid industrial development, along with a suitable climate for settlement.

questions.question: Choose an appropriate reason from the following why Osaka in Japan and Mumbai in India are two densely populated areas.

options.html: These are areas of religious significance.
Industrial belts providing large employment opportunities.
These are areas with excellent climatic conditions.

questions.explanation: Industrial areas provide employment opportunities. A large number of people are attracted to these areas. Osaka in Japan and Mumbai in India are two densely populated areas.

questions.question:
Population change refers to:

  1. the change in the number of people living in a particular area.
  2. the number of people living per square unit of area.
  3. the way in which people are spread across the earth's surface.
  4. the age-sex composition of a country.
questions.explanation: Population change refers to change in the number of people during a specific time.

questions.question:
Pick out the appropriate definition for natural growth rate from the following options:

  1. The number of people living per square unit of area.
  2. The difference between the birth rate and the death rate of a country.
  3. The way in which people are spread across the earth's surface.
  4. The age-sex composition of a country.
questions.explanation: The difference between the birth rate and the death rate of a country is called the natural growth rate. The population increase in the world is mainly due to a rapid increase in the natural growth rate.

questions.question:
Life expectancy refers to:

  1. the number of years that an average person can expect to live.
  2. the number of literate people in the country.
  3. the number of working people in the country.
  4. the number of illiterate and uneducated people in the country.
questions.explanation: Life expectancy: It is the number of years that an average person can expect to live.

questions.question:
Birth rate refers to:

  1. the number of deaths per 1,000 people.
  2. the number of infants died per 1,000 people.
  3. the number of people moving from one place to another.
  4. the number of live births per 1,000 people.
questions.explanation: Birth rate refers to the number of live births per 1,000 people. Births are usually measured using the birth rate.

questions.question: The number of deaths per 1,000 people refers to:
questions.explanation: Deaths are usually measured using the death rate i.e. the number of deaths per 1,000 people.

questions.question: The movement of people in and out of an area is referred to as:
questions.explanation: Migration is referred to as the movement of the people within a country or between countries. Migration is another way by which population size changes.

questions.question: Analyse the image given below and infer
questions.explanation: We infer from the image that birth rate is more than death rate which inturn increases the population.

questions.question: What does the following image infer?
questions.explanation: From the image, we infer that : Death rate is more than birth rate which inturn decreases the population.

questions.question:
What is the population pyramid?

  1. A graphical presentation of the age, sex composition of a population.
  2. When the population density of an area is so high that people live in tall buildings.
  3. The pattern of population distribution in large urban areas.
questions.explanation: A graphical presentation of the age, sex composition of a population.

questions.question: If the birth rate is high and the death rate is low in a country then the population pyramid will:

options.html: not narrow rapidly towards the top

questions.explanation: In the population pyramid of a country, in which birth rate is high has a broader base and if the death rate is low it will not rapidly narrow towards the top but will be a little broader.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Set of fundamental rules and laws according to that a nation/country is governed:
questions.explanation: A constitution is a set of rules that guides how a country, state, or other political organization works. The constitution may tell what the branches of the government are, what powers they have, and how they work. It may also state the rights of citizens.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is not applicable with respect to the constitution of a country?

  1. Constitution grants the citizen to get right to equality with discrimination.
  2. Constitution provides laws to protect the minority.
  3. The rules and laws are specially created to suit the needs of a country.
  4. Constitution lays down certain principles that help in the decision making of a country.
questions.explanation: Because the Constitution of India guarantees Right to Equality: All persons are equal before the law. This means that all persons shall be equally protected by the laws of the country. It also states that no citizen can be discriminated against on the basis of their religion, caste, or sex. Every person has access to all public places including playgrounds, hotels, shops, etc. The State cannot discriminate against anyone in matters of employment. The practice of untouchability has also been abolished. Discrimination is not supported/encouraged in India.

questions.question:
Which of the following is an inappropriate statement regarding the importance of the Constitution in a country?

  1. A constitution denotes the nature of the political system of a country.
  2. A constitution is framed to protect the interests of the higher and influential section of the society.
  3. To have unity in a country as people belonging to different ethnic groups, language, religions, etc coexist in a country.
  4. A constitution gives guidelines to the government as well as to the citizens.
questions.explanation: Because the Constitution guarantees the rights of minorities against the majority.

questions.question: Name the President of the Constituent Assembly who appointed a drafting committee to frame the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad, President of the Constituent Assembly appointed a drafting committee to frame the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Name the Chairman of the Drafting Committee who was responsible for framing the Constitution with the help of the image given below:
questions.explanation: Dr. Ambedkar was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee he was responsible for framing the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: On which date was the Indian Constitution passed by the Constituent Assembly?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26th November 1949 and came into force on 26th January 1950.

questions.question: On which date the Indian Constitution came into effect?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26th November 1949 and came into force on 26th January 1950.

questions.question:
A few key features of the Indian Constitution are given below. Choose the inappropriate one.

  1. Indian Constitution is one of the lengthiest constitutions of the world.
  2. Indian Constitution defines the powers of the Indian legislature, executive and judiciary.
  3. It also contains special provisions to safeguard the interests of the weaker sections of backward classes of the society.
  4. Indian constitution cannot be amended.
questions.explanation: Indian Constitution is both flexible and rigid in nature. Therefore, there are certain provisions in the constitution which can be changed by a simple majority of the parliament. It does not allow us to change its basic structure.

questions.question: The Indian Constitution begins with a ________.
questions.explanation: A Preamble means the introduction to the constitution. The Preamble is a part of the Indian Constitution. It states that: "WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a SOVEREIGN SOCIALIST SECULAR DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC and to secure to all its citizens.

questions.question: Name the procedure through which changes can be brought out in the Constitution.
questions.explanation: A constitutional amendment is a modification of the constitution of a nation or state. Amendments can add, remove, or update any part of the constitution except its basic structure. It is often used when it is better to change the document than to write a new one.

questions.question: What does the word Preamble mean?
questions.explanation: Preamble to the Constitution of India is a brief introductory statement.

questions.question: Identify which of the following statement is from the Preamble of the Indian Constitution.

options.html: India is a sovereign democratic republic
India is a sovereign socialist secular democratic republic
India is a sovereign, socialist, secular republic

questions.explanation: The Preamble of the Indian Constitution states that: "WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a SOVEREIGN SOCIALIST SECULAR DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC and to secure to all its citizens.

questions.question: How many articles and schedules were present in the Indian Constitution when it was written?
questions.explanation: At present, there are 448 articles in 25 parts, 12 schedules in the Indian Constitution. As compared to when it was written it had, 395 articles in 22 parts and 8 schedules at the time of commencement.

questions.question: A constitution not only includes the type of government, but also an agreement on certain ________ that they all believe the country should uphold.
questions.explanation: A Constitution helps serve as a set of rules and principles that all persons in a country can agree upon as the basis of the way in which they want the country to be governed. This includes not only the type of government but also an agreement on certain ideals that they all believe the country should uphold.

questions.question: A sequence of events that happened in Nepal proves people's struggle for democracy. When did this struggle start?
questions.explanation: There was a people’s struggle in Nepal in 1990 that established democracy that lasted for 12 years until 2002.

questions.question: During which year did Nepal adopt an interim constitution?
questions.explanation: In 2007, Nepal adopted an interim Constitution.

questions.question: A classroom situation is given below in the form of a picture. Analyse the image and pick out the option that clearly tells us about the picture with respect to the need for a proper class room management.

options.html: Shows that Suresh and Anuil are good friends and well behaved in class
Shows that the teacher is not strict with the children
Shows that the misuse of authority can result in gross injustice .
Shows that Suresh is a good leader

questions.explanation: As Suresh is the class monitor and knows that the teacher will believe what he says, he misuses the authority given to him by the teacher as class monitor and reports wrong allegation about Anil. Thus, misusing his power.

questions.question:
What was the huge task that the members of the Constituent Assembly had before them?

  1. India was made up of different communities with different languages, religions, cultures, etc.
  2. The population of India was quite high.
  3. There were no proper architects to frame the Constitution.
  4. The members of the Constituent Assembly always fought each other.
questions.explanation:
The members of the Constituent Assembly had a huge task before them

  1. As the country was made up of different communities who spoke different languages, belonged to different religions, and had distinct cultures.
  2. Also, when the Constitution was being written, India was going through considerable turmoil. The partition of the country into India and Pakistan.

questions.question:
One of the key features of the India Constitution is Federalism. Which one among the following best describes Federalism.

  1. Free from external control.
  2. All religions are treated equally.
  3. The existence of more than one level of government in the country.
  4. The aims and objectives are enforceable in a court of law.
questions.explanation: This refers to the existence of more than one level of government in the country. In India, we have governments at the state level and at the centre. Panchayati Raj is the third tier of government.

questions.question:
Define the term Sovereign as referred to in the Indian Constitution.

  1. Equal opportunities to everyone in the country.
  2. No discrimination on the basis of religion.
  3. People of India govern themselves through their elected representatives.
  4. An independent India, no longer governed by any external authority.
questions.explanation: Sovereign: It refers to independent people and no longer governed by any external authority.

questions.question: What does the term Socialist refer to as per Indian Constitution?

options.html: No discrimination on the basis of religion
An independent India, nol longer governed by any external authority
People of India govern themselves throught heir elected representatives
Equal opportunities to every one  to make use of the resources of the country

questions.explanation: The term ‘Socialism’ in Indian Constitution seeks to establish a welfare State.It gives qual opportunities to every one to make use of the resources of the country

questions.question: With respect to the Indian Constitution, there is no discrimination on the basis of religion i.e. all religions are treated equally, Which one of the following terms refers to the above statement?
questions.explanation: A secular state is one in which the state does not officially promote any one religion as the state religion.

questions.question: Which term refers to All citizens of India have political rights which means that they can elect their representatives in the government with special reference to the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: A democratic country, government, or political system is governed by representatives who are elected by the people.

questions.question:
India is a Republic country. Choose the exact meaning of this statement from the options given below:

  1. The Indian Constitution proclaims that the head of the state will be elected and not be a hereditary ruler.
  2. Equal opportunities to everyone in the country.
  3. No discrimination on the basis of religion.
  4. An independent India, no longer governed by any external authority.
questions.explanation:
1. A state in which the supreme power rests in the body of citizens entitled to vote and is exercised by representatives chosen directly or indirectly by them.
  2. A state in which the head of government is not a monarch or other hereditary head of state.

questions.question: Reservations are given to those who belong to scheduled castes and tribes in educational institutions, government jobs, etc. This shows that the Indian Constitution has:

options.html: The Indian Constitution proclaims that the head of the state will be elected and not be a hereditary ruler
Strived to ensure an equitable and just society by reducing economic and social injustice
Citizens have the freedom to express their opinion
An independent India, no longer governed by any external authority

questions.explanation: The societal inequality in India is represented by the grossly inadequate representation of Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and Other Backward Castes in employment and education due to historic, societal, and cultural reasons. The reservation is undertaken to address the historic oppression, inequality, and discrimination faced by those communities and to give these communities a place.

questions.question:
Fundamental rights refer to:

  1. The list of subjects of the central government.
  2. The list of subjects of the state government.
  3. The basic rights which are granted to citizens for the holistic growth of the individuals.
  4. The list of subjects of both the state and central governments.
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Rights, embodied in Part III of the Constitution, guarantee civil rights to all Indians and prevent the State from encroaching on individual liberty while simultaneously placing upon it an obligation to protect the citizens' rights from encroachment by society.

questions.question: The important fundamental rights to Equality is mentioned in which article of the constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to Equality is the first fundamental right assured to the people of India. Article 14-18 of the Constitution guarantees this right to every citizen of India.

questions.question: Mention the article in which the Cultural and Educational Rights of the Indian Constitution are specified.
questions.explanation: The Indian constitution guarantees Cultural and Educational Rights under articles 29 and 30.

questions.question: Name the fundamental right that guarantees the citizens the right to practise and propagate the religion they desire
questions.explanation: The right to choose a religion (or no religion) without interference by the government.

questions.question: The Constitution states that all minorities, religious or linguistic, can set up their own educational institutions in order to preserve and develop their own culture. Choose the right which explains this.

options.html: Right to Cultural and Educational Rights

questions.explanation: Cultural and Educational Rights:The Constitution states that all minorities, religious or linguistic, can set up their own educational institutions in order to preserve and develop their own culture.

questions.question: The following images shows it is important to spread awareness to stop child labour. Name the Fundamental right that these children should be protected.
questions.explanation: Right against Exploitation: The Constitution prohibits human trafficking, forced labour, and employment of children under 14 years of age.

questions.question: The directives to the government which ensure social and economic reforms are called ________.
questions.explanation: Directive Principles of State Policy: This section was designed by the members of the Constituent Assembly to ensure greater social and economic reforms, and to serve as a guide to the independent Indian State to institute laws and policies that help reduce the poverty of the masses.

questions.question:
A few statements regarding the significance of fundamental rights are given below. Pick out the one that is inappropriate.

  1. They do not check the authority of the government.
  2. They provide the necessary conditions for the moral and material protection of human beings.
  3. They form the foundation of an equitable society based on social injustice and social equality.
  4. They strengthen the secular status of the nation.
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights, protect citizens against the arbitrary and absolute exercise of power by the State. The Constitution, thus, guarantees the rights of individuals against the State as well as against other individuals.

questions.question: The federal structure of the Indian government is governed at ________ levels.
questions.explanation: India has a quasi-federal form of government, called "union" or "central" government, with elected officials at the union, state, and local levels.

questions.question: The three-tier federal structure of the Indian government is composed of:

options.html: One government at centre, another at state, another at local level
Two at the state level and one at the local level
One government at the centre and two at the state level
One government at the centre and two at the local level

questions.explanation: In India, we have three levels of government federal, state, and local. The power is shared between these three levels.

questions.question:
A few statements with respect to the secular state as specified in the Indian constitution are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

  1. That one religious community does not dominate another.
  2. That some members do not dominate other members of the same religious community.
  3. That the State power and religion go hand in hand.
  4. That the State does not enforce any particular religion nor take away the religious freedom of individuals.
questions.explanation: Secularism: A secular state is one in which the state does not officially promote any one religion as the state religion.

questions.question: Why is it important to separate religion from the State in democratic societies? Choose an appropriate answer for it.

options.html: We need to ensure an equitable and just society by reducing social and economic inequalities .
We need to protect the freedom of individuals to exit from their religion , embrace another religion or have the freedom to interpret religions teachings differently
We need to allow people to choose an occupation of their choice.
We need to protect people from being exploited

questions.explanation:
To protect religious freedom of an individual.
 To stop domination of one religious community over other.

questions.question:
The following statements consolidate the fact that only a secular state can realise its objectives. However, one of these statements is not true. Pick out the incorrect one.

  1. That the state is no longer governed by any external authority.
  2. That one religious community does not dominate another.
  3. That some members do not dominate other members of the same religious community.
  4. That the state does not enforce any particular religion nor take away the religious freedom of individuals.
questions.explanation: Secularism: A secular state is one in which the state does not officially promote any one religion as the state religion.

questions.question: A country that passed a law in February 2004, banning students from wearing any conspicuous religious or political signs or symbols such as the Islamic headscarf, the Jewish skullcap, or the large Christian crosses.
questions.explanation: The French law on secularity and conspicuous religious symbols in schools bans wearing conspicuous religious symbols in French public.

questions.question: To force someone to do something or the force used by a legal authority like the state refers to:
questions.explanation: Coercion refers to the action or practice of persuading someone to do something by using force or threats.

questions.question: Name the country where most children in government schools begin their school day reciting the Pledge of Allegiance.
questions.explanation: The Pledge of Allegiance of the United States is an expression of allegiance to the Flag of the United States and the Republic of the United States of America.

questions.question: Analyse the image given and identify, which country's flag is shown here.
questions.explanation: It is the flag of United States of America.

questions.question: The constitution of India also lays down a list of fundamental duties. Pick out the one that is not applicable to the Fundamental Duties.

options.html: If anyone finds his rights being compromised, he does not have the right to appeal in the court of law
According to the 42nd amendment of our constitution enacted in 1946, a set of duties is prescribed for the citizens
All citizens of India are expected to faithfully carry out the fundamental duties
Fundamental duties check the absolute authority of the government

questions.explanation: This statement is not applicable to the Fundamental Duties because the fundamental duties however are non-enforceable and non-justiciable in character. This means that no citizen can be punished by a court for violation of a fundamental duty.

questions.question: The following image is an artwork by Brazilian artist Octavio Roth. What does this imply?

options.html: Article 20 of the Universal Declaration Rights
Article 19 of the Universal Declaration Rights
Article 10 of the Universal Declaration Rights
Article 59 of the Universal Declaration Rights

questions.explanation: Article 10 - Universal Declaration of Human Rights. Everyone is entitled in full equality to a fair and public hearing by an independent and impartial tribunal, in the determination of his rights and obligations and of any criminal charge against him.

questions.question: A few differences between the Directive Principles and Fundamental rights are given below. Pick out the one incorrect one.

options.html: Directive principles are meant to protect and create certain privileges for the people while Fundamental rights are privileges enjoyed by the people
Directive Principles cover wider subjects like international peace and security while the scope of these fundamental rights is limited for the citizens of India
Directive Principles are guidelines for the government to frame policies for the welfare of the people while Fundamental rights are basic rights of the people
Directive Principles are justiciable and citizens can go to the courts to secure them while Fundamental rights are justiciable and cannot be enforced

questions.explanation: The Fundamental Rights must be binding upon every authority that has got the power to make laws. These are justiciable and citizens can go to the courts to secure them. Directive Principles of State Policy are non-justiciable and cannot be enforced in the court of law.

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

questions.question: Originally, the Indian Constitution provides a ________ tier system of government called the federal system.
questions.explanation: The Constitution originally provided for a two-tier system of government, the Union Government (also known as the Central Government), representing the Union of India, and the State governments. Later, a third tier was added in the form of Panchayats and Municipalities.

questions.question: The ________, which is made up of all representatives together, controls, and guides the government.
questions.explanation: The Parliament, which is made up of all representatives together, controls and guides the government. In this sense people, through their chosen representatives, form the government and also control it.

questions.question: The constitution of India establishes a Parliamentary Democracy where people directly elect the ________.
questions.explanation: In a parliamentary form of democracy, the elected members of the Lok Sabha, the Lower House of the Parliament, together elect the Prime Minister. These members of the Lok Sabha are directly elected by universal adult suffrage.

questions.question: The constitution of India establishes a Parliamentary Democracy where people elect the ________ indirectly who is the Ceremonial head of the State.
questions.explanation: The President is not elected directly by the people. The elected Members of Parliament (MPs) and the elected Members of the Legislative Assemblies (MLAs) elect a president.

questions.question: Who is the nominal head of the state in the Indian Parliamentary form of government?
questions.explanation: While the Prime Minister is the head of the government, the President is the nominal head of the State.

questions.question: The members of Loksabha are known as ________.
questions.explanation: The members of Loksabha are known as Member of Parliament (MP).

questions.question: From the following examples, pick out the best example for a Presidential form of Government.
questions.explanation: In many countries of the world, the President is both the head of the state and the head of the government. The President of the United States of America is the most well-known example of this kind of President.

questions.question: How many subjects are included in the union list?
questions.explanation: Union List includes 97 subjects of national importance such as the defence of the country, foreign affairs, banking, communications and currency etc.

questions.question: Pick out the number of subjects included in the State list.
questions.explanation: State List contains 66 subjects of State and local importance such as police, trade, commerce, agriculture, and irrigation, etc.

questions.question: Select the number of subjects included in the concurrent list.
questions.explanation: The Concurrent List or List-III (Seventh Schedule) is a list of 52 items (though the last item is numbered 47) given in the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution of India.

questions.question:
Which type of subjects is included in the Concurrent List?

  1. Subjects on which both the state and central government can make laws.
  2. Subjects on which both the state and central government cannot make any laws.
  3. Subjects on which the state government can make laws.
  4. Subjects on which the central government can make laws.
questions.explanation: Concurrent List includes subjects of common interest to both the Union Government as well as the State Governments, such as education, forest, trade unions, marriage, adoption, and succession.

questions.question: Name the important arms of the government or the organs of the state according to the Indian constitution?
questions.explanation: According to the Constitution, there are three organs of the State. These are the legislature, the executive, and the judiciary.

questions.question: It is a permanent house. Two-third of members retire after every two years. What is it?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is a permanent house. Two-third of members retire after every two years. So every member has a tenure of 6 years.

questions.question: In Lok Sabha, to have a majority a party should have at least ________ the number of members or more.
questions.explanation: In Lok Sabha, to have a majority a party should have at least half the number i.e. 272 members or more.

questions.question: The bill is required to pass through the ________ in order to become a law.

options.html: Both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha

questions.explanation: A bill is required to pass through the Rajya Sabha and Lok Sabha and assented by President in order to become a law.

questions.question: The Indian Parliament consists:

options.html: house of Elders and Council of Nation
Council of States and House of the People

questions.explanation: The Indian Parliament consists of the Council of States(Rajya Sabha) and House of the People(Lok Sabha).

questions.question: Name the city in India where the Parliament House/Sansad Bhavan is located.
questions.explanation: Parliament House or Sansad Bhavan is located in Delhi.

questions.question:
Identify this image based on the following clues :

  1. Designed by the British architects Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker in 1912-13
  2. Located in Delhi, the opening ceremony of this was performed by Lord Irwin on 18 January 1927
  3. Surrounded by large gardens, the shape of this is circular based on the Asokha Chakra
questions.explanation: The given image is of Sansad or the parliament house.

questions.question: Name the lower house of the Indian Parliament.
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha is the lower house of the parliament.

questions.question: Pick out the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha as laid down in the Constitution.
questions.explanation: The House of People known as Lok Sabha is the lower chamber of Parliament comprises of 552 members.

questions.question: The ________ is a group of persons who work together to implement the laws.
questions.explanation: An executive is a group of persons who work together to implement the laws made by the parliament.

questions.question: A few statements regarding the qualification to become a member of Rajya Sabha are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

options.html: He/She must not hold any office or post of profit
He/She must not be a bankrupt or mentally unsound
He/she must be a citizen of India
He/she should be at least 35 years old

questions.explanation: He/she should be at least 30 years old.

questions.question: Select the term period of Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: The term of the Lok Sabha, unless dissolved, is five years from the date appointed for its first meeting.

questions.question: The image given below shows a man reading instructions on how to use an Electronic Voting Machine. During the elections of which year did the Indian citizens start using this machine?
questions.explanation: The above photo shows a man reading instructions on how to use an Electronic Voting Machine (EVM). EVMs were used throughout the country for the first time in the 2004 general elections. The use of EVMs in 2004 saved around 1,50,000 trees which would have been cut to produce about 8,000 tons of paper for printing the ballot papers.

questions.question: The use of this in 2004, saved around 1,50,000 trees which would have been cut to produce 8,000 tons of paper for printing ballot papers. What is referred to here like this?
questions.explanation: EVMs were used throughout the country for the first time in the 2004 general elections. The use of EVMs in 2004 saved around 1,50,000 trees which would have been cut to produce about 8,000 tons of paper for printing the ballot papers.

questions.question:
Pick out the wrong statement from the following statements with respect to the Parliament of India.

  1. Elections to the Parliament are held in a similar manner as they are for the state legislature.
  2. The Parliament in India consists of the President and Lok Sabha.
  3. The elected candidates are called the Members of Parliament.
  4. The Parliament has immense powers because it is the representative of the people.
questions.explanation: The Parliament in India consists of the President, Rajyasabha, and Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Who is the leader of the ruling party in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister of India is the leader of the ruling party in the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Quite often, a single political party does not have the majority to form a government. Then the different political parties with similar concerns form a government. Name such a type of government.
questions.explanation: Often times in the recent past it has been difficult for a single political party to get the majority that is required to form the government. They then join together with different political parties who are interested in similar concerns to form what is known as a coalition government.

questions.question: From the qualifications given, for a person to stand for election to the Lok Sabha. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

options.html: Should not be bankrupt or mentally unstable
Should be an Indian citizen above 35 years of age
Should not hold any salaried job
Should be an Indian citizen above 25 years of age

questions.explanation: Should be an Indian citizen above 25 years of age.

questions.question: The ________ hour is an important mechanism through which MPs can elicit information about the working of the government.
questions.explanation: The Parliament, while in session, begins with a question hour. The question hour is an important mechanism through which MPs can elicit information about the working of the government.

tests.name: Parliament and the Making of Laws Test 03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Civics Parliament and the Making of Laws CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The ________ parties play a critical role in the healthy functioning of a democracy.
questions.explanation: Opposition parties play a critical role in the healthy functioning of a democracy. They highlight drawbacks in various policies and programmes of the government.

questions.question: They highlight drawbacks in various policies and programmes of the government and mobilise popular support for their own policies. Who are they?
questions.explanation: Opposition parties play a critical role in the healthy functioning of a democracy. They highlight drawbacks in various policies and programmes of the government and mobilise popular support for their own policies.

questions.question: Name the presiding officer of Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha (House of the People) is presided over by the Speaker.

questions.question: India became independent on ________.
questions.explanation: There was a long and difficult struggle in which many sections of society participated and finally, India became independent on 15th August 1947.

questions.question: Which one among the following list is not a function of the Speaker of Lok Sabha?

options.html: Casts a vote to resolve a deadlock if there is a tie after voting
Maintains discipline in the Lok Sabha

questions.explanation: Lok Sabha is presided over by the speaker.

questions.question: What is the Upper house or Rajya Sabha otherwise called?
questions.explanation: Rajyasabha is also called the Council of States and is indirectly elected by the elected representatives of the legislative assemblies of various States.

questions.question: Select the maximum number of members that Rajya Sabha can have?
questions.explanation: Article 80 of the Constitution lays down the maximum strength of Rajya Sabha as 250, out of which 12 members are nominated by the President and 238 are representatives of the States and of the two Union Territories.

questions.question: Mention the number of Nominated members of the Rajya Sabha.
questions.explanation: Article 80 of the Constitution lays down the maximum strength of Rajya Sabha as 250, out of which 12 members are nominated by the President and 238 are representatives of the States and of the two Union Territories.

questions.question: Mention the number of Elected members of the Rajya Sabha.
questions.explanation: Article 80 of the Constitution lays down the maximum strength of Rajya Sabha as 250, out of which 12 members are nominated by the President and 238 are representatives of the States and of the two Union Territories.

questions.question: What is the significant function of parliament?
questions.explanation: Law making is the significant function of parliament.

questions.question: Name the ex-officio Chairperson of the Rajya Sabha.
questions.explanation: The Vice-President of India (currently, Venkaiah Naidu) is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, who presides over its sessions.

questions.question: The elections are conducted by the Election Commission of India. Who heads this Commission?
questions.explanation: Election Commission of India is headed by Election Commissioner.

questions.question: A few functions of the Election Commission of India are given below. List out the one that is not applicable.

options.html: Prepares electoral rolls and sets up the polling booths
Fixes the date for an election
Cannot declare the election results after the counting is over

questions.explanation: Can declare the election results after the counting is over.

questions.question: Money bills of the Parliament originate in the ________.

options.html: Cannot be presented in the Indian Parliament

questions.explanation: Lok Sabha has more power over financial matters. Money bills of the Parliament originate in the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Who introduces Ordinary Bills in the Parliament?

options.html: Any member, either of the ruling party or opposition

questions.explanation: An ordinary bill can be introduced in either house - Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha (unlike finance bill). It then has to be approved by both houses and signed by the President.

tests.name: Parliament and the Making of Laws Test 04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Civics Parliament and the Making of Laws CBSE Online Test 04 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

questions.question:
A sequence of passing the ordinary bills is given below: Pick out the correct sequence from the following

  1. Any member either of the ruling party or opposition may introduce the bill in any one of the houses? Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha
  2. The Bill is sent to the President for his assent
  3. Voting on bill takes place after the debate and it goes to the Other house.
  4. When President signs the Bill it becomes an Act.
questions.explanation: Firstly the bill is introduced in both the houses. After the debate, the voting is done and it goes to other house. It is then sent to the president for his assent. The bill becomes law when the president signs it.

questions.question: What did the universal adult franchise mean?
questions.explanation: Universal Adult Franchise means that the right to vote should be given to all adult citizens without the discrimination of caste, class, colour, religion, or gender.

questions.question: Name the financial bill which is introduced in Lok Sabha.
questions.explanation: Money Bill originates in Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Name the term given to a bill that is introduced by a member of Parliament.
questions.explanation: Any MP can introduce a bill in parliament. Private member bills are bills introduced in parliament by MPs who are not ministers.

questions.question: How many buildings are there for central secretariat?
questions.explanation: There are 2 buildings for central secretariat.

questions.question: To whom are the questions asked in the Lok Sabha addressed to?
questions.explanation: The questions asked in the Lok Sabha are addressed to the Speaker.

questions.question: One of the most important function of the Lok Sabha is to select the ________.
questions.explanation: One of the most important functions of the LokSabha is to select the executive. An executive is a group of persons who work together to implement the laws made by Parliament.

questions.question: What is the period after the question hour and before the beginning of the rest of the day's work called?
questions.explanation: Zero hours denotes the time immediately following the Question Hour in both Houses of Parliament. This is about 12 noon which is why it is called Zero Hour.

questions.question: Normally members raise questions of ________ during zero hours.
questions.explanation: During Zero Hour the members raise the questions of earnest necessity and the need for urgent action regarding public interest.

questions.question: A temporary alliance of groups or parties is called ________.
questions.explanation: Temporary alliance of groups or parties. The alliance formed by political parties after elections when no party has been able to get adequate seats to form a clear majority.

questions.question: What do you mean by situations in which there are no easy solutions to problems?
questions.explanation: Unresolved is the situation in which there are no easy solutions to problems.

questions.question: Which one of the following refers to - a negative vote which can be passed against one minister or the entire ministry?
questions.explanation: No-Confidence Motion: A motion moved by the opposition to prove that it has no confidence in the Council of Ministers. If such a motion is passed by the majority of members of the Lok Sabha the ministry has resigned.

questions.question: Loksabha has more power over ________ matters as compare to RajyaSabha.
questions.explanation: Loksabha has more power over financial matters. Money Bill originates in Loksabha.

questions.question:
Impeachment refers to:

  1. A process to legislate on the matters in the state list during an emergency.
  2. A process to conduct smooth and fair elections in a country.
  3. A process in which a bill becomes a law.
  4. A process in which the charges are levelled against the President.
questions.explanation: The President may also be removed before the expiry of the term through impeachment for violating the Constitution of India by the Parliament of India. The process may start in either of the two houses of the Parliament. The house initiates the process by levelling the charges against the President.

questions.question: For how many years does the President of India remains in office?
questions.explanation: The tenure of the president of India is 5 years.

questions.question: Who is the supreme commander of the defence forces?
questions.explanation: The President of India is the Supreme Commander of the Indian Armed Forces.

questions.question: Who is considered as the first citizen of the country?
questions.explanation: Our president is called as the first citizen of India because he/she represents our country and he/she is the head of the Executive, Legislature, Judiciary, the three arms of our government.

questions.question:
Which one among the following is not an important power of the President of India?

  1. The President is the Chairperson of the Planning Commission.
  2. The President invites the leader of the majority party to form a government.
  3. The President as ahead of the state can declare war or sue for peace and conclude treaties with other countries.
  4. The President is the supreme commander of the armed forces and appoints the three chiefs of the army, air force, and navy.
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister is the Chairperson of the Planning Commission.

questions.question: Judges of the Supreme Court and High court are appointed by the ________.
questions.explanation: The judges of the Supreme Court and High Court are appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister and in consultation with the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.

questions.question:
Some of the important Legislative powers of the President are given below. List out the one that is not a power of the President of India.

  1. The President summons and prorogues Parliament and can dissolve the Lok Sabha.
  2. The President is the ex-officio Chairperson of Rajya Sabha.
  3. The President addresses the joint session when there is a deadlock over an important Ordinary bill.
  4. A bill becomes a law only after President assent.
questions.explanation: The Vice-President of India is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: When there is a threat to the State by war or external aggression or any internal rebellion, then a state called emergency is declared. Who has the power to declare this emergency on the advice of the Union Cabinet?
questions.explanation: Under Article 356, the President may issue a proclamation to impose emergency in a state if he is satisfied on receipt of a report from the Governor of the concerned State, or otherwise, that a situation has arisen under which the administration of the State cannot be carried on according to the provisions of the constitution.

questions.question: Under which Article can President's Rule be imposed if administrative machinery of a State breaks down?
questions.explanation: Under Article 356, the President may issue a proclamation to impose emergency in a state if he is satisfied on receipt of a report from the Governor of the concerned State, or otherwise, that a situation has arisen under which the administration of the State cannot be carried on according to the provisions of the constitution.

questions.question: Under which Article can Financial emergency be declared by the President of the financial stability of a country is threatened?
questions.explanation: The Financial Emergency is provided under Article 360. It provides that if the President is satisfied that the financial stability or credit of India or any of its part is threatened; he may declare a state of Financial Emergency.

questions.question: How many years maximum can a Vice President serve the country?
questions.explanation: The Vice-President of India is the second-highest constitutional office in the country. He serves for a five-year term but can continue to be in the office, irrespective of the expiry of the term.

questions.question:
A few statements related to the Election and Powers of the Vice President of India are given below. Pick out the one that is not true.

  1. The Vice President acts on behalf of President when the President resigns.
  2. The Vice President acts on behalf of President when the President dies in office.
  3. The Vice President acts on behalf of the President when the President is indisposed owing to illness or absence or any other reason.
  4. The Vice President is elected only by the members of the Rajya Sabha.
questions.explanation: The Vice-President is elected by the members of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: The leader of the majority party that wins the elections is invited to become the ________.
questions.explanation: The leader of the ruling party in the Lok Sabha is Prime Minister.

questions.question: The parliament is made up with ________.

options.html: President, Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha

questions.explanation: Parliament consist of President, Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha.

questions.question: How many years normally do the Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers enjoy their term?
questions.explanation: The tenure of Minister and the Council of Ministers is 5 years.

questions.question: Who acts as a link between the President and the Council of Ministers?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister acts as a link between the President and the Council of Ministers.

questions.question: From the given list, choose the one that is not a category of the ministers.
questions.explanation: The associate minister doesn't come under the category of ministers.

questions.question: Who holds the important portfolios like defense, finance, home affairs, etc, and are generally the senior-most ministers?
questions.explanation: Cabinet Ministers are the senior-most ministers. They hold important portfolios like defence, finance, home affairs, etc.

questions.question: The Prime Minister and the Ministers are collectively known as the ________.
questions.explanation: The Cabinet of India is the collective decision-making body of the Government of India, consists of the Prime Minister and Ministers.

questions.question: Parliament enables citizens of India to participate in ________ and control the government.
questions.explanation: Parliament enables citizens of India to participate in decision making and control the government, thus making it the most important symbol of Indian democracy.

questions.question: What is/are the major duty/duties of Parliament?

options.html: To Control, Guide and Inform the Government

questions.explanation:
The three major duties of the Parliament are

  1. To Select the National Government
  2. To Control, Guide and Inform the Government
  3. Law-Making

questions.question:
Why do we need a Parliament? Pick out the incorrect one.

  1. For making new laws in the country and changing and abolishing existing laws.
  2. It controls the executive organ of the government.
  3. To ensure that citizens have freedom of speech, movements, and association.
  4. To ensure that there is a misuse of authority by elected leaders.
questions.explanation: To ensure that there is no misuse of authority by elected leaders as a constitution describes their powers and limitations.

questions.question: Which house dissolves after 5 years?
questions.explanation: In Lok Sabha, Members are elected for a period of 5 years. It is a temporary house and it dissolves after 5 years.

questions.question: Who appoints the Governors of various States after consultations with the Chief Minister of the State?
questions.explanation: Governor is appointed on the advice of the Union Council of Ministers, or in reality on the advice of the Prime Minister. For this President need to consult the Chief Minister of the State.

questions.question: Rajya Sabha is chaired by the ________ of India.
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha or the council of states is chaired by the vice president of India.

questions.question: Who is the real executive head of the state of India?
questions.explanation: Governor is the Head of a State.

questions.question: The Parliament of India is the _______ law-making institution.
questions.explanation: The Parliament of India (Sansad) is the supreme law-making institution. It has two Houses, the Rajya Sabha and the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: The Political party which has the majority of MPs will form the ________ government.
questions.explanation: After the Lok Sabha elections, a list is prepared to show how many MPs belonging to each political party have won the elections. The Political party which has the majority of MPs will form the National government.

questions.question: ________ Hour helps the Parliament to control the Executive (Government).
questions.explanation: The Questions asked during ‘Question Hour’ highlights the shortcomings of the Government and also bring to light the opinion of the citizen through their representatives, namely the MPs. This Question Hour helps the Parliament to control the Executive (Government).

questions.question: ________ is called House of People.

options.html: Both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha

questions.explanation: As it is directly elected by people, Loksabha is called the House of People.

questions.question: The Parliament enables citizens of India to participate in decision making and control the ________.
questions.explanation: The purpose of a Parliament is to enable citizens of India to participate in decision making and control the government.

questions.question: What was the principle of the Indian constitution?
questions.explanation: The main principle of the Indian constitution was the Universal adult franchise. All adults in free India could vote and participate in decision-making.

questions.question: Where is the Supreme Court of India located?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the apex court of India. It was established on 26 January 1950. It is located in New Delhi.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the work of the judiciary?
questions.explanation: The legislature is the law-making organ of the Government.

questions.question: Judiciary also has the power to strike down particular laws passed by the Parliament if it believes that these are a violation of the basic structure of the Constitution. This is called:
questions.explanation: As the final interpreter of the Constitution, the judiciary also has the power to strike down particular laws passed by the Parliament if it believes that these are a violation of the basic structure of the Constitution. This is called judicial review.

questions.question: There are ________ different levels of courts in our country.
questions.explanation:
There are three different levels of courts in our country. There are several courts at the lower level while there is only one at the apex level.

  1. District Court
  2. High Court
  3. Supreme Court

questions.question: At the top is the ________ court, that is located in New Delhi.
questions.explanation: At the top is the Supreme Court that is located in New Delhi and is presided over by the Chief Justice of India.

questions.question: ________ are equal before law.
questions.explanation: All people are equal before the law.

questions.question: Which right was included in article 21 of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to health was included in article 21 of the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: ________ deals with any harm or injury to the rights of individual.
questions.explanation: Civil law deals with any harm or injury to the rights of individuals. For example, disputes relating to the sale of land, purchase of goods, rent matters, divorce cases.

questions.question: The subordinate court is more commonly known by many different names except which of the following:

options.html: Trial Court or the Court of the District Judge

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the apex (highest) court of India. The subordinate court is more commonly known by many different names. These include the Trial Court or the Court of the District Judge, the Additional Sessions Judge, Chief Judicial Magistrate, Metropolitan Magistrate, Civil Judge.

questions.question: PIL stands for:
questions.explanation: The idea of going to court to get justice often seems remote. to many ordinary people, In response to this, the Supreme Court in the early 1980s devised a mechanism of Public Interest Litigation or PILto increase access to justice. It allowed any individual or organisation to file a PIL in the High Court or the Supreme Court on behalf of those whose rights were being violated.

questions.question: If any citizen believes that their rights are being violated, then they can approach the ________ for justice to be done.
questions.explanation: We have a judicial system that consists of the mechanism of courts that a citizen can approach when a law is violated.

questions.question: Function like examine the witnesses in court is done by:
questions.explanation: A witness is a person who saw a crime or was a victim of a crime. Witnesses in court are examined by the judges of the respective court.

questions.question: Hashimpura is in_______.
questions.explanation: The Hashimpura massacre is an alleged incident of mass murder which took place on or around 22 May 1987 near Meerut in Uttar Pradesh state, India.

questions.question: High Courts were first established in which of the following three presidency cities?
questions.explanation: High Courts were first established in the three Presidency cities of Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras in 1862. The High Court of Delhi came up in 1966.

questions.question: A mechanism of PIL was devised in which year:
questions.explanation: In 1980, devised a mechanism of Public Interest Litigation or PIL to increase access to justice. It allowed any individual or organisation to file a PIL in the High Court or the Supreme Court on behalf of those whose rights were being violated.
