questions.question: Right to food include:

options.html: rich have the right to food
all people of the country distributed food free

questions.explanation: The Right to Food includes that all should have equal access to the food.

questions.question: Judiciary plays an important role because it is________.
questions.explanation: It is the independence of the judiciary that allows the courts to play a central role in ensuring that there is no misuse of power by the legislature and the executive. The lack of independence would force the judge to make all judgments in favour of the politician.

questions.question: This refers to money given to make amends for an injury or a loss.
questions.explanation: Compensation: In the context of this chapter this refers to money given to make amends for an injury or a loss.

questions.question: The case between a landlord and a tenant, the court can order the flat to be vacated and pending rent to be paid comes under ________.
questions.explanation: Deals with any harm or injury to the rights of individuals. For example, disputes relating to the sale of land, purchase of goods, rent matters, divorce cases. In a rent matter, either a landlord or tenant can file a case.

questions.question: All persons are equal before ________.
questions.explanation: It is one of the fundamental rights. It ensures the guarantees to every person the right to equality before law & equal protection of the laws.

questions.question: FIR stands for:
questions.explanation: First Information Report (FIR). The first information report means information recorded by a police officer on duty given either by the aggrieved person or any other person to the commission of an alleged offence. On the basis of the first information report, the police commence its investigation.

questions.question: Four key players in the criminal justice system are:

options.html: Police, Politician, Defence lawyer and Judge.
Police, Public Prosecutor, Government and Judge.
Police, Public Prosecutor, Defence lawyer, and Judge.

questions.explanation: The police, the Public Prosecutor, the defence lawyer, and the judge are the key player in the criminal justice system.

questions.question: ________ which means that everyone is subject to the law of the land.
questions.explanation: Rule of law: It refers that laws apply equally to all persons and that a certain set of fixed procedures need to be followed when a law is violated.

questions.question: It is with the registration of an ________ that the police can begin their investigations into a crime/case.
questions.explanation: First Information Report (FIR). The first information report means information recorded by a police officer on duty given either by the aggrieved person or any other person to the commission of an alleged offence. On the basis of a first information report, the police commence its investigation.

questions.question: When we see someone violating the law, we immediately think of informing the ________.
questions.explanation: When we see someone violating the law, we immediately begin with the lodging of a First Information Report with the police, as police are the one who investigates after the case is filed.

questions.question: Police officers are the one who:

options.html: decide that accused person is guilty
send person to jail directly without court decision

questions.explanation: Police usually begin their duty with the lodging of a First Information Report (FIR) and then investigate the crime after which a case is filed in the court.

questions.question: According to the Constitution, every individual charged of a crime has to be given a ________.
questions.explanation: Fair trial is the term given to a trial in the presence of an impartial judge and jury. The process to be followed ensuring a fair trial.

questions.question: A decision like that no teacher can beat a student, or about the sharing of river waters between states, or they can punish people for particular crimes are taken by ________.
questions.explanation: Courts take decisions on a very large number of issues. They can decide that no teacher can beat a student, or about the sharing of river waters between states, or they can punish people for particular crimes.

questions.question: This refers to the court declaring that a person is not guilty of the crime which he/she was tried for by the court.
questions.explanation: Acquit: This refers to the court declaring that a person is not guilty of the crime which he/she was tried for by the court.

questions.question: The police officials who carry out the arrest or interrogation should:

options.html: wear identification without name tag but with the designation
wear visible identification and name tags

questions.explanation: The police officials who carry out the arrest or interrogation should wear visible identification and name tags to ensure their duties.

tests.name: The Judiciary Test 03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Civics The Judiciary CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The memo should also be attested by at least ________ witness who could include a family member of the family.
questions.explanation: The police officer carrying out the arrest of the arrestee shall prepare a memo of arrest at the time of arrest and such memo shall be attested by at least one witness who may either be a member of the family of the arrestee or a respectable person of the locality from where the arrest is made. It shall also be countersigned by the arrestee and shall contain the time and date of arrest.

questions.question: The arrest memo should be counter-signed by the:
questions.explanation: The police officer carrying out the arrest of the arrestee (person arrested) shall prepare a memo of arrest at the time of arrest and such memo shall be attested by at least one witness who may either be a member of the family of the arrestee or a respectable person of the locality from where the arrest is made. It shall also be countersigned by the arrestee and shall contain the time and date of arrest.

questions.question: A person who is called upon in court to provide a first-hand account of what he/she has seen, heard, or knows:
questions.explanation: A person who sees an event, typically a crime or accident, taken place.

questions.question: An offence for which the police may arrest a person without the permission of the court:
questions.explanation: Generally, cognizable offence means a police officer has the authority to make an arrest without a warrant and to start an investigation with or without the permission of a court.

questions.question: A criminal offence is regarded as a ________.
questions.explanation: Deals with conduct or acts that the law defines as offences. For example, theft, harassing a woman to bring more dowry, murder.

questions.question: The judicial system provides a mechanism for resolving disputes between citizens, between citizens and the government, between two state governments and between the centre and state governments are referred to as:
questions.explanation: Dispute resolution generally refers to one of several different processes used to resolve disputes between different parties.

questions.question: The Supreme Court was established:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India came into existence on 26th January 1950 and is located on Tilak Marg, New Delhi.

questions.question: Which Article provides every citizen the Fundamental Right to Life also includes the Right to Health?
questions.explanation: The fundamental Right to Life guaranteed in Article 21 of the Constitution included the Right to Food. The court also held that the right to health and medical care is a fundamental right under Article 21.

questions.question: This refers to a petition filed before a higher court to hear a case that has already been decided by a lower court.
questions.explanation: In law, an appeal is a process in which cases are reviewed, where parties request a formal change to an official decision. Appeals function both as a process for error correction as well as a process of clarifying and interpreting the law.

questions.question: There are ________ courts at the lower level while there is ________ apex level.
questions.explanation: Currently, there are 24 High Courts but, the Supreme Court is the highest judicial court in a country.

questions.question: The courts through which most people interact with are called:
questions.explanation: The courts that most people interact with are what are called subordinate or district courts. These are usually at the district or Tehsil level or in towns and they hear many kinds of cases.

questions.question: Supreme Court is presided over by the:
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of India (CJI) is the head of the judiciary of India and the Supreme Court of India. The present CJI is Justice Sharad Arvind Bobde and is the 47th CJI since January 1950, the year the Constitution came into effect and the Supreme Court came into being.

questions.question: The decisions of which court is binding on all other courts in India.
questions.explanation: The decisions made by the Supreme Court are binding on all other courts in India.

questions.question: High Courts were first established in the three Presidency cities of Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras in ________.
questions.explanation: High Courts were first established in the three Presidency cities of Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras in 1862.

questions.question: The High Court of Delhi came up in:
questions.explanation: The High Court of Delhi came up in 1966.

tests.name: The Judiciary Test 04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Civics The Judiciary CBSE Online Test 04 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Which of the following is false regarding criminal law?

  1. Deals with conduct or acts that the law defines as offences.
  2. A petition has to be filed before the relevant court by the affected party only.
  3. If found guilty, the accused can be sent to jail and also fined.
  4. For example, theft, harassing a woman to bring more dowry and murder.
questions.explanation: It usually begins with the lodging of a First Information Report (FIR) with the police who investigate the crime after which a case is filed in the court.

questions.question: Punjab and Haryana share a common High Court at ________.
questions.explanation: Punjab and Haryana share a common High Court at Chandigarh.

questions.question: In 2001, the drought in ________, millions faced an acute shortage of food.
questions.explanation: In 2001, the drought in Rajasthan and Orissa and millions faced an acute shortage of food.

questions.question: PAC stands for:
questions.explanation: Provincial Armed Constabulary (PAC) is armed police. It is maintained at key locations across the state and active only on orders from the deputy inspector general and higher-level authorities. It is usually assigned to VIP duty or to maintain order during fairs, festivals, athletic events, elections, and natural disasters.

questions.question: Who was the Chief Justice of India in 2007?
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of India (CJI) is the head of the judiciary of India and the Supreme Court of India. The CJI also heads their administrative functions. On 8 June 2000, K.G.Balakrishnan was appointed a judge of the Supreme Court. He was sworn in as the Chief Justice of India on 14 January 2007.

questions.question: Which of the two states share the same High Court?
questions.explanation: Punjab and Haryana share a common High Court at Chandigarh.

questions.question:
In India, we have an integrated judicial system, which means that:

  1. the decisions made by higher courts are binding on the lower courts.
  2. the decisions made by lower courts are binding on the supreme courts.
  3. the decisions made by district courts are binding on the apex courts.
  4. the decisions made by higher courts are binding on the high court's only.
questions.explanation: The integrated judicial system means that decisions made by higher courts are binding on the lower courts. All the lower courts from the Gram Panchayat to the High Courts are integrated into the Supreme Court of India. The integration is through the appellate system existing in the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: Following are the example of criminal law except for ________.
questions.explanation: Deals with conduct or acts that the law defines as offences. For example, theft, harassing a woman to bring more dowry, murder.

questions.question: Issue of a dispute between two state comes under?
questions.explanation: Dispute Resolution: The judicial system provides a mechanism for resolving disputes between citizens, between citizens and the government, between two state governments and between the centre and state governments.

questions.question: This refers to the removal of persons from land/ homes that they are currently living in:
questions.explanation: Eviction: In the context of this chapter this refers to the removal of persons from land/ homes that they are currently living in.

questions.question: According to Article ________ of the Constitution, every person has a Fundamental Right to be defended by a lawyer.
questions.explanation: Article 22 Protection against arrest and detention in Certain Cases. Article 22 provides procedural safeguards against arbitrary arrest and detention. It is applicable to all individuals (Citizens and non-citizens). It does not confer a Fundamental Right on an individual against arrest and detention. It only extends certain procedural safeguards in case of arrest of an individual. Thus it comes into play only after a person has been arrested. Its object is to prevent arbitrary arrest and detention by the State.

questions.question: The ________ has laid down guidelines that the ________ must follow at the time of arrest, detention and interrogation.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India has issued specific requirements and procedures that the police and other agencies have to follow for the arrest, detention, and interrogation of any person.

questions.question: Who cannot be called to the police station only for questioning

options.html: Women and Boy under the age of 18
Women and Boy under the age of 15

questions.explanation: Women and boy under 15 years of age cannot be called to the police station for interrogation under Section 160 of the Criminal Procedure Code. This law provides Indian women with the right to not being physically present at the police station for interrogation.

questions.question: The Supreme Court of India has laid down specific requirements and procedures that the police and other agencies have to follow for the arrest, detention, and interrogation of any person. These are known as the:
questions.explanation: D. K. Basu Guidelines are the specific requirements and procedures laid down by the Supreme Court of India for the police and other agencies to follow during the arrest, detention, and interrogation of any person.

questions.question: After a person is arrested, it is a court of law that decides whether the ________ person is guilty or not.
questions.explanation: The Magistrate decides if there is enough evidence to find the person guilty. An action by a person that is against the law.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Police file a charge sheet in the court if:

options.html: investigation proved that the person is accused
the person did not commit a crime
the person is suspected of anything by the police

questions.explanation: A charge sheet is filed by the police in the court as part of the completion of investigation in a criminal case. The charge sheet is supplied by the court to the accused.

questions.question: When a friend or relative of arrested person lives outside the district, the time, place of arrest, and venue of custody must be notified by police within ________ after the arrest.
questions.explanation: When a friend or relative lives outside the district, the time, place of arrest, and venue of custody must be notified by police within 8 to 12 hours after arrest.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a function of the judge?

options.html: Decide for how many years the accused will be put in jail

questions.explanation: The judge hears all the witnesses and any other evidence presented by the prosecution and the defence. The judge decides whether the accused person is guilty or innocent on the basis of the evidence presented and in accordance with the law. Recording the evidence is the job of the investigators who visit the scene immediately after the crime.

questions.question: The act of being fair or just and not favouring one side over another-
questions.explanation: Impartiality is a principle of justice holding that decisions should be based on objective criteria, rather than on the basis of bias, prejudice, or preferring the benefit to one person over another for improper reasons.

questions.question: This refers to the trial judge informing the accused, in writing, of the offence for which he/she will face trial.

options.html: To be charged of a crime

questions.explanation: A criminal charge is a formal accusation made by a governmental authority (usually the public prosecutor or the police) asserting that somebody has committed a crime.

questions.question: Identify the following picture
questions.explanation: The above photo shows the Supreme Court of India. The Supreme Court was established on 26 January 1950, the day India became a Republic. Like its predecessor, the Federal Court of India (1937–1949), it was earlier located in the Chamber of Princes in the Parliament House. It moved to its present building on Mathura Road in New Delhi in 1958.

questions.question: Supreme court was earlier located in the Chamber of Princes in the ________.
questions.explanation: Supreme Court was earlier located in the Chamber of Princes in the Parliament House. It moved to its present building on Mathura Road in New Delhi in 1958.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is false regarding civil law?

  1. Deals with any harm or injury to the rights of individuals.
  2. Disputes relating to the sale of land, purchase of goods.
  3. A petition has to be filed before the relevant court by the affected party only.
  4. Deals with conduct or acts that the law defines as offences.
questions.explanation:
Civil Law deals with any harm or injury to the rights of individuals. For example, disputes relating to the sale of land, purchase of goods, rent matters, divorce cases. A petition has to be filed before the relevant court by the affected party only.
Criminal Law Deals with conduct or acts that the law defines as offences. For example, theft, harassing a woman to bring more dowry, murder. It usually begins with the lodging of a First Information Report (FIR) with the police who investigate the crime after which a case is filed in the court.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding District Court?

options.html: They hear many kinds of cases
They are called subordinate or district courts
Each state is divided into districts that are presided over by a District Judge
It is the highest court of the state

questions.explanation: The District Court is the highest court of the district. The High Court is the highest court of the state.

questions.question: Identify the following
questions.explanation: The Madras High Court is the high court of the Indian state of Tamil Nadu. The court is one of the three High Courts in India established in the three Presidency Towns of Bombay, Calcutta and Madras

questions.question: Article 39A of the Constitution places a duty upon the State to provide a ________ to any citizen who is unable to engage one due to poverty or other disability.
questions.explanation: The State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid, by suitable legislation or schemes or in any other way, to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities.

questions.question:
Article 22 of the Constitution and criminal law guarantee to every arrested person the following Fundamental Rights except?

  1. The Right to be presented before a magistrate within 12 hours of arrest.
  2. The Right to be informed at the time of arrest of the offence for which the person is being arrested.
  3. The Right not to be ill-treated or tortured during arrest or in custody.
  4. Confessions made in police custody cannot be used as evidence against the accused.
questions.explanation: Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the court of the magistrate and no such person shall be detained in custody beyond the said period without the authority of a magistrate.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Term Meaning
(a) Accused (i) A person or group of people who are charged with or on trial for a crime
(b) Detention (ii) A breach of a law or rule; an illegal act
(c) Offence (iii) The action of detaining someone or the state of being detained in official custody
questions.explanation:
1. Accused: A person or group of people who are charged with or on trial for a crime.
  2. Detention: The action of detaining someone or the state of being detained in official custody
  3. Offence: A breach of a law or rule; an illegal act.

questions.question: Which of the following is the function of a Public prosecutor?
questions.explanation: The public prosecutor is an important officer who cross-examines the witness.

questions.question:
Match the following
Role

  1. Police
  2. Judge
  3. Defence lawyer

Function

  1. record the evidence
  2. argue the case for the victims
  3. hear and records the statement of the victims
questions.explanation:
Police
The first person to take action against any sort of crime that has taken place.
Function:
1. Writes FIR
2. Records the evidences and statements of witnesses
3. Meets the accused persons
4. Form an opinion, on the basis of the investigation. Then arrest the Fiesta Fans
5. File a charge sheet in the court, if the police thinks the evidences point towards the accused person's guilt.

Judge
The person who decides the cases in law court
Function:
1. Conducts a fair trial in an open court impartially
2. Hears all the witnesses any every evidences presented by the prosecution and the defense
3. Decides whether the accused is guilty or innocent on the basis of the evidences and witnesses presented in the court, in accordance with the law
4. If the accused is convicted, the judge pronounces the sentence
5. Judge may send the guilty to jail, or impose a fine, or both, depending on what the law prescribes.

Defense Lawyer
The lawyer who fights the case on behalf of the accused person. He/She sees to it that the accused is released with his dignity or his/her punishment is lessened.
Function:
1. Defends the accused
2. Cross-examines the witnesses
3. Present witnesses in the accused defense

questions.question: Tribals are also referred to as:
questions.explanation: The term Adivasis literally means original inhabitants are communities who lived and often continue to continue in close association with forests. Tribals are also referred to as Adivasis.

questions.question: Adivasis practise a range of tribal religions that involve the following except:
questions.explanation: The majority of Adivasi practice Hinduism and Christianity. Adivasi beliefs vary by tribe and are usually different from the historical Vedic religion. Although each culture has its own different mythologies and rituals, "animism" is said to describe the most common, foundational thread of indigenous peoples' "spiritual" or "supernatural" perspectives.

questions.question: ________ spirit is often worshipped at specific sacred groves within the village boundary while the ancestral ones are usually worshipped at home.
questions.explanation: Adivasis are often involved in the worship of ancestors, village and nature spirits, the last associated with and residing in various sites in the landscape-'mountain spirits', 'river spirits', 'animal spirits', etc.

questions.question: Jagannath cult is in ________.
questions.explanation: Adivasi religions themselves have influenced dominant religions of the empires around them, for example, the Jagannath cult of Orissa, and Shakti and Tantric traditions in Bengal and Assam.

questions.question: India's tea industry became possible with their (Adivasi) labour in ________.
questions.explanation: The labour intensive tea industry of Assam is totally dependent on the workforce of the tea garden community comprising large tribes such as Munda, Santhal, Kurukh, Gonds, Bhumji.

questions.question: Constitution provides safeguards to religious and linguistic ________ as part of our Fundamental Rights.
questions.explanation: Safe guards are needed to protect minority communities against the possibility of being culturally dominated by the majority.

questions.question: The term minority is most commonly used to refer to

options.html: communities that are qualitatively small in relation to the most of the population
communities that are numerically large in relation to the most of the population
communities that are numerically small in relation to the rest of the population
communities that are qualitatively small in relation to the rest of the population

questions.explanation: Minority is a concept that goes well beyond numbers. It encompasses issues of power,acess to resources and has social and cultural dimensions.Size is a disadvantage and leads to the marginalisation of the relatively smaller communities.

questions.question: Every citizen of India can approach the courts if they believe that their:
questions.explanation: The Constitution provides the safeguards because it is committed to protecting India's cultural diversity and promoting equality as well as justice.

questions.question: Chaired by Justice ________, the committee examined the social, economic, and educational status of the Muslim community in India.
questions.explanation: Recognising that Muslims in India was lagging behind in terms of various development indicators, the government set up a high-level committee in 2005. The Committee Chaired by Justice Rajindar Sachar, the committee examined the social, economic and educational status of the Muslim community in India.

questions.question: Muslims may wear a ________, sport a long beard, wear a ________, and these become ways to identify all Muslims.
questions.explanation: Because of burqa, long beard, fez, they tend to be identified differently and some people think they are not like the rest of us. Often this becomes an excuse to treat them unfairly and discriminate against them.

questions.question: The caste system is a hierarchical system and Dalits are considered to be:
questions.explanation: The term Dalit means 'broken' is used deliberately and actively by groups to highlight the centuries of discrimination they have experienced within the caste system.

questions.question: An area where the presence of the armed forces is considerable.
questions.explanation: A militarized area, country, or organization has a large, strong army and other armed forces and many weapons.

questions.question:
What this means is that untouchability has been abolished?

  1. No one can henceforth prevent Dalits from educating themselves, entering temples, using public facilities, etc.
  2. No one can henceforth prevent Muslims from educating themselves, entering temples, using public facilities, etc.
  3. No one can henceforth prevent Jain from educating themselves, entering temples, using public facilities, etc.
  4. No one can henceforth prevent Christians from educating themselves, entering temples, using public facilities, etc.
questions.explanation: Abolishing of untouchability prohibits preventing a person from entering a place of worship or from taking water from a tank or well. This act lays down that whatever is open to the general public (or Hindus) should be open to the members of the scheduled castes. No shopkeeper can refuse to sell them, no person may refuse to render any service to any person on the ground of untouchability.

questions.question: The term which means broken is ________.
questions.explanation: The word Dalit means "broken/scattered" in Sanskrit and Hindi, is a term mostly used for the castes in India that have been subjected to untouchability.

questions.question: The poet Chokhamela belonged to which caste ________.
questions.explanation: The Bhakti poet Chokhamela from fourteenth-century Maharashtra belonged to Mahar caste which was at that time considered untouchable.

questions.question: ________ refers to the practice of removing human and animal waste/excreta using brooms, tin plates, and baskets from dry latrines.
questions.explanation: A caste-based occupation involving the removal of untreated human excreta from bucket toilets or pit latrines is termed as manual scavenging. It has been officially abolished by law in India as a dehumanizing practice.

questions.question: C.K. Janu was ________.
questions.explanation: C.K.Janu, an Adivasi activist pointed out that one of the violators of Constitutional rights guaranteed to tribal people is governments in various states of India.

questions.question: This refers to groups challenging their marginalization:
questions.explanation: To come face to face or to challenge someone or something.

questions.question: An act that violates all norms of decency and dignity that society believes in ________.
questions.explanation: It usually refers to a hideous and repugnant act that goes against all the values that society has accepted.

questions.question: ________ women are an important part of the women's movement in India.
questions.explanation: Muslim-women led organisations have emerged in urban areas across the country, some of which have formed networks to advocate for Muslim women’s rights. The emergence of two networks, in particular, the Muslim Women’s Rights Network (MWRN) and the Bharatiya Muslim Mahila Andolan (BMMA).

questions.question: Adivasi ________ refer to it to defend their right to occupy land that was traditionally theirs.
questions.explanation: Adivasis are often unwilling to move from their land and are forcibly displaced. Activists have asked that those who have forcibly encroached upon tribal lands should be punished under the Act of 1989.

questions.question: Who noted that in cases where tribal's have already been evicted and cannot go back to their lands, they must be compensated?
questions.explanation: C.K. Janu said that the government must draw up plans and policies for tribals who have been evicted to live and work elsewhere.

questions.question: In 2003, the Safai Karamchari Andolan and 13 other organisations and individuals, including seven scavengers, filed a PIL in the:
questions.explanation: The petitioners complained that manual scavenging which had been banned still existed and it continued in government undertakings like the railways.

questions.question: Soyrabai, the wife of the well known Bhakti poet
questions.explanation: Chokhamela, a Bhakti poet from fourteenth-century Maharashtra, belonged to the Mahar caste which was at that time considered untouchable.

questions.question: The term Dalit is used deliberately and actively by groups to highlight the centuries of discrimination they have experienced within the ________.
questions.explanation: The word Dalit means "broken/scattered" in Sanskrit and Hindi, is a term mostly used for the castes in India that have been subjected to untouchability.

questions.question: Who provides for free or subsidised hostels for students of Dalit and Adivasi communities so that they can avail of education facilities that may not be available in their localities?
questions.explanation: To implement the Constitution, both central and state governments create specific schemes for implementation in tribal areas that have high Dalit population.

questions.question: Village Jakmalgur is in ________.
questions.explanation: This is a case study, adapted from a real-life account, to understand how Dalits use the protection that laws provide.

questions.question: Niyamgiri is a sacred mountain of ________.
questions.explanation: The Dongria Kondh, Adivasis in the East India State of Orissa fought to protect the sacred Niyamgiri Hills-home of their God, Niyam Raja. Niyamgiri is our God. We live in these mountains and survive. We don’t have any land on which we can produce and live. We are dependent on the mountains. We won’t leave Niyamgiri.”

questions.question: ________ a social boycott of an individual and his family.
questions.explanation: Ostracise means to exclude or banish an individual or a group.

questions.question: A group that can express themselves and their views strongly are called:
questions.explanation: A number of assertive Dalit groups came into being and asserted their rights-they refused to perform their so-called caste duties and insisted on being treated equally.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: People believe that Adivasis are:
questions.explanation: Adivasis are blamed for their lack of advancement as they are believed to be resistant to change or new ideas. Adivasis are invariably portrayed in very stereotypical ways.

questions.question: Adivasis the term literally means:
questions.explanation: Adivasis are communities who lived and often continue to live in close association with forests.

questions.question: ________ percent of India's population is Adivasi.
questions.explanation: Around 8% of India's population is Adivasi and many of India's most important mining and industrial centres are located in Adivasi areas such as Jamshedpur, Rourkela, Bokaro and Bhilai.

questions.question: How many different Adivasi groups are there in India?
questions.explanation: Adivasis are not a homogenous population. Adivasis are particularly numerous in states like Chattisgarh, Jharkhand, Madhya Pradesh, Orissa, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal and in North-Eastern states. A state like Orissa is home for 60 different tribal groups. Adivasis are not a homogeneous population: there are over 500 different Adivasi groups in India.

questions.question: A state like ________ is home to more than 60 different tribal groups.
questions.explanation: There are 62 tribal communities residing in Orissa today. They are scattered to all parts of Orissa. The major tribes of the state are Kondhs, Koyas, Gadabas, Oraon, Juangs, and Santals.

questions.question: Adivasis have always been influenced by different surrounding religions like

options.html: Shakta, Buddhist, Muslims, Italian and Christianity
Shakta, Sikhs, Muslims, Polish and Christianity
Shakta, Sikhs, Muslims, Jains and Christianity
Shakta, Buddhist, Vaishnav, Bhakti and Christianity

questions.explanation: Adivasi religions themselves have influenced dominant religions of the empires around them,for example,Jagannath cult of Orissa and Shakti and Tantric traditions in Bengal and Assam.

questions.question: Shakti and Tantric traditions in ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: Shaktism is, together with Vaishnavism and Shaivism, one of the major forms of modern Hinduism and is especially popular in Bengal and Assam. Shaktism is an essential part of Hindu Tantra a system of practices involving the worship of the goddess and designed to empower and release both mind and body.

questions.question: During the ________ century, substantial numbers of Adivasis converted to Christianity, which has emerged as a very important religion in modern Adivasi history.
questions.explanation: In the late 19th century, Christian missionaries converted a large number of people, especially tribals. According to several scholars, Hindu right-wing organizations like the Rashtriya Swayam Sevak Sangh came to Jharkhand in the 19th century to counter the conversion drives of the Christian missionaries.

questions.question: ________ has the largest number of speakers and has a significant body of publications including magazines on the internet or in e-zones.
questions.explanation: Bangla Santali Bhasha Sampark is a collection of essays in the E-Book format authored by him and dedicated to linguist Suniti Kumar Chatterjee on the relationship between the Bengali and Santali languages. Santali Script is widely accepted among Santal Communities, however presently in West Bengal, Odisha & Jharkhand.

questions.question: From the 1830s onwards, Adivasis from Jharkhand and adjoining areas moved in very large numbers to various plantations in India and to which part of the world?

options.html: Mauritius, the Caribbean, and even Asia
Brazil, the Caribbean, and even Africa
Brazil, the Caribbean, and even Australia
Mauritius, the Caribbean, and even Australia

questions.explanation: Most Adivasis migrated to cities in search of work where they were employed for very low wages in local industries and at construction sites. Adivasis from Jharkhand and adjoining areas moved in very large numbers to various plantations in India and the world-Mauritius, Caribean, and Australia.

questions.question: Economic, ________ and political factors work together to make certain groups in society feel marginalised.
questions.explanation: Marginalisation is the social disadvantage and relegation to the fringe of society. It is used across disciplines including education, society, politically, psychologically and economically.

questions.question: Kalahandi district is in:
questions.explanation: Kalahandi was one of the oldest metropolises in Odisha. After Indian independence, Kalahandi joined with the Union of India on 1 January 1948.

questions.question: Safeguards are needed to protect ________ communities against the possibility of being culturally dominated by the ________.
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution protects minorities against any discrimination and disadvantage they may face. The Constitution provides these safeguards because it is committed to protecting India's cultural diversity and promoting equality as well as justice.

questions.question: Muslims are ________ percent of India's population and are considered to be a marginalised community in India.
questions.explanation: Muslims are considered to be a marginalised community in India because, in comparison to other communities, they have over the years been deprived of the benefits of socio-economic development. Muslims are 14.2 per cent of India’s population and are considered to be a marginalised community in India.

questions.question: ________ is a part of a city in which members of a minority group live, especially because of social, legal, or economic pressure.
questions.explanation: A ghetto is a part of a city in which many poor people or many people of a particular race, religion, or nationality live separately from everyone else.

questions.question: Article ________ of the Constitution states that untouchability has been abolished.
questions.explanation: Untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden The enforcement of any disability arising out of Untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with the law. Article 17 of the constitution abolishes the practice of untouchability.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution notes that no citizen of India shall be discriminated against on the basis of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth?
questions.explanation: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth, or any of them. No citizen shall, on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them, be subject to any disability, liability, restriction or condition.

questions.question: A stated course of action that provides direction for the future, sets goals to be achieved or lays out principles or guidelines to be followed and acted upon.
questions.explanation: A policy is a deliberate system of principles to guide decisions and achieve rational outcomes. A policy is a statement of intent and is implemented as a procedure or protocol.

questions.question: The well known Bhakti poet Chokhamela was from ________.
questions.explanation: Bhakti poet Chokhamela from fourteenth-century Maharashtra belonged to Mahar caste which was at that time considered untouchable.

questions.question: Today whose poetry is sung and appreciated by Dalits, marginalised groups, and those critical of social hierarchies in U.P., Rajasthan, Punjab, Madhya Pradesh, Bengal, Bihar, and Gujarat.
questions.explanation: Kabir was a fifteenth-century poet and weaver who also belonged to Bhakti tradition. Kabir attacked those who attempted to define individuals on the basis of their religious and caste identities.

questions.question: In which year, the government passed the Employment of Manual Scavengers and Construction of Dry Latrines (Prohibition) Act.
questions.explanation: The Employment of Manual Scavengers and Construction of Dry Latrines (Prohibition) Act 1993 dated 5th June 1993 was brought with the object to deal with the prohibition of employment of manual scavengers and also for the construction of dry latrines.

questions.question:
The act passed in 2006:

  1. The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act
  2. The Employment of Manual Scavengers and Construction of Dry Latrines (Prohibition) Act
  3. The Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act
  4. Dalits Act
questions.explanation: The law concerns the rights of forest-dwelling communities to land and other resources, denied to them over decades as a result of the continuance of colonial forest laws in India. The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006" was enacted by the Parliament of India in December 2006.

questions.question:
Which Act states that this Act is meant to undo the historical injustices meted out to forest-dwelling populations in not recognising their rights to land and resources.

  1. The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act
  2. The Employment of Manual Scavengers and Construction of Dry Latrines (Prohibition) Act
  3. The Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act
  4. Dalits Act
questions.explanation: Supporters of the Act claim that it will redress the "historical injustice" committed against forest dwellers while including provisions for making conservation more effective and more transparent. The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006" was enacted by the Parliament of India in December 2006.

questions.question: Adivasis are invariably portrayed in very stereotypical ways in following except:
questions.explanation: We seem to know very little about the realities of their lives. This often wrongly leads to people believing that they are exotic, primitive, and backward. The "original inhabitant" contention is based on the fact that they have no land.

questions.question: Many of India's most important mining and industrial centers are located in which Adivasi areas?
questions.explanation: India's most important mining and industrial centres are located in-Jamshedpur, Rourkela, Bokaro, and Bhilai among others. Adivasis are particularly numerous in states such as Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal, and in the North-Eastern states of Assam, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Arunachal Pradesh etc.and these industrial centres are located in these states.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Term Meaning
(a) Displaced (i) to move from their homes people who are forced or compelled
(b) Malnourished (ii) the main current of a river or stream
(c) Mainstream (iii) A person who does not get adequate nutrition
questions.explanation:
1. Displaced means moving from their homes forcefully or are compelled to move away.
  2. Malnourished is the person not getting adequate nutrition.
  3. Mainstream is the main current of a river or stream.

questions.question:
Read the below mention song
Come Mini, let's go to Assam
Our country has so much suffering
The country of Assam, oh Mini
Has a tea garden full of greenery...
The Sardar says work, work
The Babu says catch and brings them in
The Saheb says I'll take off the skin of your back
Hey Jaduram, you deceived us by sending us to Assam
The above song capture:

options.html: the hopes of the Muslims and the real people faced in Assam
the hopes of the government and the reality minister faced in Assam
the hopes of the migrants and the reality they faced in Assam
the hopes of the people of Assam and the reality Adivasi faced in Assam

questions.explanation: Adivasis from Jharkhand and adjoining areas moved in very large numbers to various plantations in India and the world. The story of this migration is full of extreme hardship, torture, heartbreak, and death. In the nineteenth century alone five lakh Adivasis had perished in these migrations. The song captures the hopes of the migrants and reality they faced in Assam.

questions.question: Identify the below figure
questions.explanation: This is a photo of Niyamgiri hill located in Kalahandi district of Orissa. This area is inhabited by Dongarria Konds, an Adivasi community. Niyamgiri is the sacred mountain of this community.

questions.question: Where the above hill is situated
questions.explanation: This is a photo of Niyamgiri Hill located in Kalahandi district of Orissa.

questions.question: The area of above hill is inhabited by
questions.explanation: Niyamgiri Hill located in Kalahandi district of Orissa are in habited by Dongarria Konds an adivasi community. Niyamgiri is the sacred mountain of this community.

questions.question: Which company is planning to set up a mine and a refinery here (above hill) which will displace this Adivasi community
questions.explanation: A major aluminium company is planning to set up a mine and a refinery in Niyamgiri Hills which will displace this adivasi community. Dongarria Konds are strongly resisting this proposed development and have been joined by envioronmentalists as well.

questions.question: Adivasis use around 10,000 plant species approximately ________ species are used for medicinal purposes; ________ are used as pesticides; ________ as gums, resins and dyes; 550 as fibres; 3,500 are edible.
questions.explanation: This entire knowledge system gets wiped out when Adivasis lose their rights over forests and lands. Losing their lands and access to the forest means tribals lose their main source of livelihood and food.

questions.question: People of which community have shown below:
questions.explanation: This is a tribal or adivasi community and this is the only way in which adivasi communities are represented that is in colourful costumes,headgear and through their dancing.

questions.question: The Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act.
questions.explanation: The schedule caste and tribe atrocities Act of 1989 is an Act of the Parliament of India enacted to prevent atrocities against schedule caste and schedule tribes. The objectives of the Act clearly emphasize the intention of the Government to deliver justice to these communities through proactive efforts to enable them to live in a society with dignity and self-esteem and without fear or violence or suppression from the dominant castes.

questions.question:
Read the poem below :
A body is unclean, they say
Only the soul is untainted
But the impurity of the body
Is born within the body
?By which ritual does the body
become pure?
Not a creature has been born
except in a bloody womb.
This is the glory of God,
Defilement exists within.
The body is polluted from within,
Be sure of it says the Mahar Chokha
The above poem is written by
questions.explanation: The poem is written by Soyrabai, the wife of well-known bhakti poet Chokhamela from fourteenth-century Maharashtra.

questions.question:
Read the poem below :
A body is unclean, they say
Only the soul is untainted
But the impurity of the body
Is born within the body?
By which ritual does the body
become pure?
Not a creature has been born
except in a bloody womb.
This is the glory of God,
Defilement exists within.
The body is polluted from within,
Be sure of it says the Mahar Chokha
Poet is questioning the idea of
questions.explanation: The poetess Soyrabai is questioning the idea of purity and arguing that since every human is born in the same manner there is nothing that makes one body less or more pure than the other.

questions.question: Identify the figure poet shown below who is sitting in the middle
questions.explanation: Kabir was a fifteenth century poet and a weaver who belonged to Bhakti tradition. His poetry spoke about his love for the supreme being free of rituals and priests.

questions.question: What he is doing
questions.explanation: Kabir was a poet and a weaver and he is seen weaving in the picture.

questions.question: Which century the poet belonged
questions.explanation: Kabir was a fifteenth century poet and weaver who belonged to Bhakti tradition.Kabir attacked those who attempted to define individuals on the basis of their religious and caste identities.

questions.question:
Read the below poem identify the poet:

Pandit, Look in your heart for knowledge
Tell me where untouchability
Came from, since you believe in it.
Mix red juice, white juice, and air
A body bakes in a body?
We eat by touching, we wash
by touching, from a touch
the world was born.
So who's untouched, asks Kabir?
Only she
Who is free from delusion?
questions.explanation: Kabir turns the idea of untouchability on its head from the lowest to the highest state that a human being can achieve.

questions.question:
Read the below poem:
Pandit, Look in your heart for knowledge
Tell me where untouchability
Came from, since you believe in it.
Mix red juice, white juice, and air
A body bakes in a body?
We eat by touching, we wash
by touching, from a touch
the world was born.
So who's untouched, asks Kabir?
Only she
Who is free from delusion

The poem is written on the issue of:
questions.explanation: Kabir is challenging the priest by directly questioning at him about where untouchability came from.

questions.question:
To whom the poet is challenging
Pandit, Look in your heart for knowledge
Tell me where untouchability
Came from, since you believe in it.
Mix red juice, white juice and air
A body bakes in a body?
We eat by touching, we wash
by touching, from a touch
the world was born.
So who's untouched, asks Kabir?
Only she

Who is free from delusion?
questions.explanation: Kabir is challenging the priest by directly questioning him about where untouchability came from. He asks the priest to look for knowledge in his heart and not in the scriptures.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the figure below
questions.explanation: Manual scavenging refers to the practice of removing human and animal waste or excreta using brooms, tin platesand baskets from dry laterines and carrying it on the head to disposal grounds some dostance away. A manual scavenger is the person who does the job of carrying this filth.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Act Year
(a) The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act (i) 2006
(b) The Employment of Manual Scavengers and Construction of Dry Latrines (Prohibition) Act (ii) 1993
(c) The Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act (iii) 1989
questions.explanation:
1. Recognition of the forests rights Act came in 2006.
  2. The employment of Manual Scavengers and Construction of Dry Latrines Prohibition Act came in 1993.
  3. Prevention of Atrocities Act came in 1989.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Terms Meaning
(a) Assertive (i) To have to give up ownership or to give up authority
(b) Ostracise (ii) To exclude or banish an individual or a group
(c) Dispossessed (iii) This person or group is one that can express themselves and their views strongly
questions.explanation:
1. Assertive - This person or group is one that can express themselves and their views strongly
  2. Ostracise - To exclude or banish an individual or a group
  3. Dispossessed - To have to give up ownership or to give up authority

questions.question: Which of the following is not in Chennai?
questions.explanation: Agra is a city in northern India’s Uttar Pradesh state.

questions.question: Mr. Ramagopal live in ________.
questions.explanation: Senior government officials Mr. Ramagopal lives in Anna Nagar, Chennai.

questions.question: ________ is essential for life and for good health.
questions.explanation: Water is essential for life and for good health. Not only is it necessary for us to be able to meet our daily needs but safe drinking water can prevent many water-related diseases.

questions.question: ________ has one of the largest numbers of cases of diseases such as diarrhoea, dysentery, cholera.
questions.explanation: India has one of the largest number of cases of diseases such as diarrhoea, dysentery, cholera. Over 1,600 Indians, most of them children below the age of five, reportedly die every day because of water-related diseases.

questions.question: The Constitution of India recognises the right to water as being a part of them?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India recognises the right to water as being a part of the Right to Life under Article 21.

questions.question: Right to ________ is a Fundamental Right.
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India recognises the right to safe water as being a part of the Right to Life under Article 21.

questions.question: Essential facilities like electricity, public transport, schools and colleges that are necessary and that need to be provided for everyone are called ________.
questions.explanation: Like water, there are other essential facilities that need to be provided for everyone. Similarly, there are things like electricity, public transport, schools, and colleges that are also necessary. These are known as public facilities.

questions.question: The important characteristic of a public facility is that once it is provided, its benefits can be shared by ________.
questions.explanation: The important characteristic of a public facility is that once it is provided, its benefits can be shared by many people. For instance, a school in the village will enable many children to get educated.

questions.question: Public facilities are so important, someone must carry the responsibility of providing these to the people. This someone is the ________.
questions.explanation: Public facilities are so important, someone must carry the responsibility of providing these to the people. This ‘someone’ is the government. One of the most important functions of the government is to ensure that these public facilities are made available to everyone.

questions.question: Equity in the schooling facilities available to all children is an important aspect of:
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to Education for all children between the ages of 6-14 years. Equity in the schooling facilities available to all children is an important aspect of this Right.

questions.question: ________ are the most important forms of public transport over short distances.
questions.explanation: Buses are the most important forms of public transport over short distances. It is the main link to the workplace for the majority of the working people.

questions.question: Which among the following is not the metropolitian City?
questions.explanation: Goa is not a metropolitan city.

questions.question: The burden of shortfalls in water supply falls mostly on the ________.
questions.explanation: The burden of shortfalls in water supply falls mostly on the poor. The middle class, when faced with water shortages, are able to cope through a variety of private means.

questions.question: People in slums have to make do with less than ________ a day per person.
questions.explanation: People in slums have to make do with less than 20 litres a day per person (one bucket).

questions.question: A shortage of municipal water is often taken as a sign of failure of the _________.
questions.explanation: A shortage of municipal water is often taken as a sign of failure of the government.

tests.name: Economic Presence of the Government Test 02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Civics Economic Presence of the Government CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: A steep rise in the price of water, making it unaffordable for many. Cities saw huge protests, with riots breaking out in places like ________, forcing the government to take back the service from private hands.
questions.explanation: In a few cases, where the responsibility for water supply was handed over to private companies, there was a steep rise in the price of water, making it unaffordable for many. Cities saw huge protests, with riots breaking out in places like Bolivia, forcing the government to take back the service from private hands.

questions.question: To protect people from exploitation, the government makes certain ________.
questions.explanation: Markets everywhere tend to be exploitative of people - whether as workers, consumers, or producers. To protect people from such exploitation, the government makes certain laws.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the essential goods?
questions.explanation: Car is a luxury item.

questions.question: Why the Minimum Wages Act is necessary?
questions.explanation: This law is meant to protect the interests of all workers; particularly, farm labourers, construction workers, factory workers, domestic workers, etc.

questions.question: In October 2016, the government amended the Child Labour Prevention Act, banning children under ________ years of age from working as domestic servants or as workers in dhabas, restaurants, tea shops, etc.
questions.explanation: In October 2016, the government amended the Child Labour Prevention Act, banning children under 14 years of age from working as domestic servants or as workers in dhabas, restaurants, tea shops, etc. It made employing these children a punishable offence.

questions.question: What does UC mean?
questions.explanation: UC stands for Union Carbide which is an American company.

questions.question: The world's worst industrial tragedy took place in ________ 24 years ago.
questions.explanation: The world’s worst industrial Bhopal gas tragedy took place in Bhopal 24 years ago.

questions.question: What does CNG stand for?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court had ordered all public transport vehicles using diesel were to switch to CNG which means Compressed Natural Gas.

questions.question: An individual who buys goods for personal use and not for resale is:
questions.explanation: Consumer is a person who buys goods for personal use and not for resale.

questions.question: In a series of rulings (1998 onwards), the Supreme Court had ordered all public transport vehicles using diesel were to switch to ________.
questions.explanation: Emissions from vehicles are a major cause of environmental pollution. In a series of rulings (1998 onwards), the Supreme Court had ordered all public transport vehicles using diesel were to switch to Compressed Natural Gas (CNG).

questions.question: ________ is another hazardous industry that is growing rapidly in South Asia.
questions.explanation: Ship-breaking is another hazardous industry that is growing rapidly in South Asia. Old ships no longer in use, are sent to ship-yards in Bangladesh and India for scrapping.

questions.question: Workers Union is:
questions.explanation: Workers Union is an organization or association of workers formed to promote collective bargaining with employers over wages, hours, job security, and working conditions.

questions.question: The Right to Life is a _________.
questions.explanation: 'Everyone has the right to life, liberty, and the security of person.' The right to life is undoubtedly the most fundamental of all rights.

questions.question: Enforcement of law become important:

options.html: when law seeks to protect the ministers.
when law seeks to protect the children only.
when law seeks to protect the government.
when law seeks to protect the weak from strong.

questions.explanation: Enforcement becomes even more important when the law seeks to protect the weak from the strong. For instance, to ensure that every worker gets fair wages, the government has to regularly inspect worksites and punish those who violate the law.

questions.question: Children labour prevention act was amended by the government in ________.
questions.explanation: In October 2016, the government amended the Child Labour Prevention Act, banning children under 14 years of age from working as domestic servants or as workers in dhabas, restaurants, tea shops, etc.

tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 08 Social Science Civics Economic Presence of the Government CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.

  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: In the eyes of the law, it is ________ to deny workers their wages.
questions.explanation: In the drive for profits, Owners might deny workers their rights and not pay them wages, for example, in the eyes of the law, it is illegal or wrong to deny workers their wages.

questions.question: Only three states published the plan of child labour prevention act these are :
questions.explanation: The central government had asked state governments to develop plans to rescue and rehabilitate children who are working as domestic servants. To date, only three state governments, namely Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu have published these plans.

questions.question: Within three days of MIC gas leak, more than ________ people were dead.
questions.explanation: Within three days, more than 8,000 people were dead. Hundreds of thousands were maimed.

questions.question: Madipakkam is in ________.
questions.explanation: Madipakkam is a residential locality in the South of Chennai.

questions.question: Over ________ Indians, most of them children below the age of five, reportedly die every day because of water-related diseases.
questions.explanation: India has one of the largest number of cases of diseases such as diarrhoea, dysentery, cholera. Over 1,600 Indians, most of them children below the age of five, reportedly die every day because of water-related diseases.

questions.question: Right to life realting to water come under article ________.
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India recognises the right to water as being a part of the Right to Life under Article 21.

questions.question: The Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to Education for all children between the ages of ________ years.
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to Education for all children between the ages of 6-14 years. Equity in the schooling facilities available to all children is an important aspect of this Right.

questions.question: ________ was spent from the government budget for the construction of the first segment of the metro-rail in Delhi.
questions.explanation: The government has planned ambitious metro rail projects for Delhi and other metropolitan cities. ₹ 11,000 crore was spent from the government budget for the construction of the first segment of the metro-rail in Delhi using the latest technology.

questions.question: The supply of water per person in an urban area in India should be about ________ litres per day
questions.explanation: The supply of water per person in an urban area in India should be about 135 litres per day (about seven buckets) - a standard set by the Urban Water Commission.

questions.question: People living in luxury hotels may consume as much as ________ of water per day.
questions.explanation: People living in luxury hotels may consume as much as 1,600 litres (80 buckets) of water per day.

questions.question: Porto Alegre is a city in ________.
questions.explanation: Porto Alegre is a city in Brazil.

questions.question: Mumbai's suburban railway is the well-functioning public transport system. It is the densest route in the world, attending to ________ passengers daily.
questions.explanation: Mumbai’s suburban railway is a well-functioning public transport system. It is the densest route in the world, attending to 65 lakh passengers daily.

questions.question: The Census of India, ________ puts rural household electrification at 44 percent, leaving around 78 million households still in the dark.
questions.explanation: The Census of India, 2001 puts rural household electrification at 44 percent, leaving around 78 million households still in the dark.

questions.question: Minimum Wages Act specifies that wages should not be below a specified minimum, whose interest is protected by this law?
questions.explanation: This law is meant to protect the interests of all workers; particularly, farm labourers, construction workers, factory workers, domestic workers, etc.

questions.question: According to the 2001 census, over ________ million children in India aged between 5 and 14 work in various occupations including hazardous ones.
questions.explanation: According to the 2001 census, over 12 million children in India aged between 5 and 14 work in various occupations including hazardous ones.

questions.question:
According to the latest amended Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, anyone found violating the ban must be penalised with a punishment ranging from a jail term of six months to two years and/or a fine of 

:
questions.explanation: In 2016, Parliament amended the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986, banning the employment of children below the age of 14 years in all occupations and of adolescents (14-18 years) in hazardous occupations and processes. It made employing these children or adolescents a cognizable offence. Anyone found violating the ban must be penalized with a punishment ranging from a jail term of six months to two years and/or a fine of `20,000 to `50,000.

questions.question: Even today more than a year after Child Labour Prevention Act was passed ________ of child domestic workers are under the age of 16.
questions.explanation: The central government had asked state governments to develop plans to rescue and rehabilitate children who are working as domestic servants. To date, only three state governments, namely Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu have published these plans. Even today more than a year after this law was passed 74 percent of child domestic workers are under the age of 16.

questions.question: What does MIC stand for?
questions.explanation: Methyl-isocyanite (MIC) is a highly poisonous gas.

questions.question: Bhopal Gas tragedy took place:
questions.explanation: The Bhopal disaster, also referred to as the Bhopal gas tragedy, was a gas leak incident on the night of 2nd December 1984 at the Union Carbide India Limited.

questions.question: Most of those exposed to the poison gas in BGT came from poor, working-class families, of which nearly ________ people are today too sick to work.
questions.explanation: Most of those exposed to the poison gas came from poor, working-class families, of which nearly 50,000 people are today too sick to work. Among those who survived, many developed severe respiratory disorders, eye problems, and other disorders. Children developed peculiar abnormalities.

questions.question: South Asian countries particularly ________ play hosts for industries producing pesticides, asbestos or processing zinc and lead.
questions.explanation: Advanced countries are relocating the toxic and hazardous industries to developing countries to take advantage of the weaker laws in these countries and keep their own countries safe. South Asian countries – particularly India, Bangladesh, and Pakistan - play hosts for industries producing pesticides, asbestos, or processing zinc and lead.

questions.question: Union Carbide is an ________.
questions.explanation: Union Carbide Corporation is the major American manufacturer of chemicals, petrochemicals, and related products.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the figure below:

options.html: Doctor taking health check up of baby
Lady is writing something on paper
Doctor is giving the polio drop to the child

questions.explanation: Oral poliovirus vaccines (OPV) are the predominant vaccine used in the fight to eradicate polio. In the given figure Doctor is giving polio vaccine.

questions.question:
Read the lines below, who said these line:
... right to water entitles everyone to sufficient, safe, acceptable, physically accessible, and affordable water for personal and domestic use.
questions.explanation: The above lines are said by the United Nations in 2002.

questions.question: Mahbubnagar district is in ________.
questions.explanation: Mahabubnagar is a city in Mahbubnagar district of the Indian state of Andra Pradesh (now Telangana).

questions.question: Who said these lines: Latrines for us! they exclaimed in astonishment. We go and perform our functions out in the open. Latrines are for you big people.
questions.explanation: The given lines are by Mahatma Gandhi recounting untouchables’ grievances, Rajkot Sanitation Committee, 1896.

questions.question: In 1984, there were very few laws protecting the ________ in India.
questions.explanation: Earlier people and government were not much concerned about the environment. In 1984, there were very few laws protecting the environment in India.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the figure
questions.explanation: The given picture is the textile mill in ahmedabad.

questions.question: Identify the figure below, which pollution is caused by this
questions.explanation: The given picture shows the  air pollution emmiting from a car causing enviornmental damage.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Term Meaning
(a) Producers (i) Money spent to purchase new machinery or buildings
(b) Consumers (ii) A person or organisation that produces goods for sale in the market
(c) Investment (iii) An individual who buys goods for personal use and not for resale
questions.explanation:
1. Those individual or organisation who manufacture goods for the purpose of sale in the market are producers.
  2. Consumer is a person who buys goods for personal use and not for resale
  3. Investment is the money which is required to purchase machinery or other fixed assets like building.
